inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Empathy and mild unease (triggered by the lonely figure in the corner, which stirred my own similar memories of being left out — a genuine, felt sense of recognition); Hesitation (worried about the consequences of acting, afraid of awkwardness or being distanced from my friends, moderate intensity); A gentle final push (not a fierce moral impulse, but a steady, quiet force of 'not doing it would feel worse').
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been through that feeling myself — standing on the margins, nobody coming over — I instinctively felt that the kid in that corner needed someone to go to her, even if all they did was sit there.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'seeing something and pretending I didn't' is harder to live with than 'going over and saying the wrong thing'; I can't accept that I clearly noticed, and then turned back around to chat about cartoons just because I didn't want the hassle.

pairs.final_decision: I chose to step away from my friend group for a moment and walk over to the new student sitting alone in the corner. I told my friends, 'You guys go ahead and eat, I'll be right back,' then carried my tray over to that small table against the wall and sat down across from her, saying quietly: 'Is it okay if I sit here? I'm K — today's your first day, right?'

inner_consciousness.summary: My friend's voice is still ringing in my ears — that excitement, that urgency — and it makes my chest tighten. I look down at the business plan in front of me, fingers tracing the edges of the paper, my mind a complete mess. Over a decade of it — from that run-down newsstand, to interviews in abandoned warehouses, to that afternoon the editor-in-chief dressed me down in front of everyone — everything I've scraped together bit by bit: the familiar beats, the steady income, that unspoken understanding between colleagues — am I really supposed to just let all of that go? But then the other side pushes back — what the painter said keeps surfacing: 'I just can't feel the passion anymore.' And that old watchmaker, forty years doing one single thing. Am I slowly turning into someone who just runs on autopilot? The opportunity my friend is describing is real, but those words — 'limited income in the early stages' — sit on my chest like a weight I can't breathe under. The financial spreadsheet is right there beside me; numbers don't lie. I'm not the kind of person who can go all-in on a gamble — I never have been. But if I keep observing, waiting, refusing to take a position like this forever, isn't that its own kind of running away? I need more time to actually understand the situation. I can't let one phone call push me into something.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety (core): my sense of stability feels threatened — the security I've built over more than a decade is being shaken, rooted in a deep-seated resistance to environmental change that goes back to childhood and the self-doubt I've cycled through again and again in my career; Ambivalence (secondary): a quiet, creeping weariness with the status quo and a fear of the unknown coexist at the same time, moderate in intensity; Unease (undertone): being pressured to take a position on the spot — every instinct I have toward caution is pushing back against making a rushed decision.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I've been through too many moments of reckless action that left me scrambling to recover, so my instinct is firm: any major decision should wait until I actually understand the situation — not get swept along by someone else's emotions and timeline.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'get clear on the facts first' matters more than 'act immediately'; I cannot accept betting everything I've built over more than a decade on a future I can't even properly articulate — not when I don't have enough information.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hold off on giving an answer and buy myself more time to do proper due diligence — I won't commit on the spot. I tell my friend: 'I really am taking this seriously, but this decision is too big. I need to go through the plan more carefully and get a clear picture of the financials and the team. Give me a week — I'll come back to you with a real answer.'

inner_consciousness.summary: The child's focused profile through the glass window, sunlight falling across it — that look of pure concentration left me momentarily speechless. The teacher handed me the brochure and I flipped through it; the paper felt heavier than I expected. A flood of thoughts rushed in all at once — those photos hanging in the hallway, the kids who'd gone on to specialist schools, what had become of them? A few had really made it out into the world, and a few others I simply didn't know about. I thought of my father slamming the table when I chose journalism — he said passion doesn't put food on the table, but I took that road anyway, and I've managed to hold on this far. But my child's situation is different, that much is real. This is genuine talent; the teacher said it was rare, and I can see it too. Still — giving up a conventional education, what if things stall halfway through? That road is very hard to come back from. I thought again about that potter, that old clockmaker, that fruit farmer — all of them had committed to one thing from childhood, yet some people do change course midway. I can't make this decision for my child, but I can't just stand on the sidelines either. The teacher said the window of opportunity is short, and I don't want hesitation to cost us something real — but I don't want impulsiveness to leave us with regrets either. I need to go home first, sit down properly with my child, hear what he/she actually thinks, and then talk it over with the rest of the family.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Cautious, yet genuinely moved (moderate intensity): the image of my child's talent stirs something real in me; at the same time, an old wariness toward major changes — the unease of that move, the suppression I felt when my father dismissed my own ambitions — makes me instinctively want to pause and think it through rather than agree or refuse on the spot.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through the feeling of being pushed forward at a critical crossroads, or being held back from one, I instinctively believe this kind of decision shouldn't rest on my shoulders alone — I have to understand what my child actually wants before any next step can even be discussed.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'getting the full picture before doing anything' matters more than almost anything else. I cannot accept making a hasty decision — one that could shape my child's entire life — on insufficient information. But at the same time, I refuse to let excessive caution cause my child to miss an opportunity that is truly theirs.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hold off on giving an answer on the spot, and to make a decision only after having a thorough conversation with my child and family first. I tell the teacher: 'Thank you so much for bringing this to us — we take it very seriously. I need to sit down and really talk with my child first, hear what he/she thinks, and then discuss it with the family. We'll get back to you as soon as we can.'

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment he walked over, the glass of orange juice in my hand suddenly felt very heavy. Twelve years. That conference room, that proposal, those colleagues who all signed their names together — it all came rushing back in a single second. I thought I'd stopped caring long ago, but now here he is, standing right in front of me, suit immaculate, smile wide, everyone around us watching, and I feel like my feet are sinking into something. The things he said — assistant, tea runs, errand girl — every word felt calculated, designed to pin me to some fixed position in front of all these people. I know what he's waiting for. I know what everyone around us is waiting for. My heart is pounding, my face feels a little hot, and I'm gripping this glass tighter and tighter. Take it or don't? If I take it, I'm accepting it — accepting these twelve years, accepting that proposal, accepting all that isolation. But making a scene right here and now — that's not who I am. My whole life, in moments like this, I've never been able to say those things. But this time is different from before. This time, too many people are watching. I don't need to win. I just can't lose this completely. I can't let him use that business card to define me as someone who needed his charity.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Humiliation (triggered by being publicly shamed, dredging up a lifetime of accumulated memories of being belittled — intensity high); Tense self-restraint (instinctive defensiveness under the public gaze — wanting to retreat, yet knowing there is nowhere left to retreat to); Suppressed anger (twelve years of old scores pressing down on my chest, ignited in this moment, but it will not erupt).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I've known since I was young that confronting someone head-on in front of a crowd usually makes things worse for yourself, so my instinct is not to explode on the spot — but this time, that business card is a public declaration about who I am as a person, and I cannot accept it in silence. Doing so would be stamping my own approval on everything he did twelve years ago.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: I can't accept being treated, in front of all these people, like someone who needs to be handed a gracious way out — that's even harder to bear than the isolation back then. I don't need to make a scene, but I need to walk away from this moment still standing on my own two feet.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to decline politely, avoid direct confrontation, and push the card back to him in a calm but clear way — giving him no room to perform. I shook my head slightly, gently slid the business card back toward his hand, and said evenly: 'Thank you for the kind thought, but my current work suits me quite well — I'm not looking to make any changes.' Then I took half a step back, glass still in hand, and shifted my gaze away.

inner_consciousness.summary: The words on that note are still swimming in front of my eyes — "the answers passed along pretty smoothly, didn't they" — and then he pushed the door open, carrying a glass of milk, eyes all lit up, saying "hard work pays off." Those words went in like a splinter. My palms went a little cold, something pressing down on my chest, and yet I couldn't get a single word out. He stood there waiting for me to respond, completely unaware of what I'd just seen. I wanted to say something, but the words jammed right at the back of my throat — he was so happy, going on about the commendation ceremony, the speech, the competition selection, one thing after another, while I sat here with nothing in my head but that crumpled little note. I don't know how to bring it up, and I don't even know if right now is the right time to try. He's still young — if I handle this badly, will it push him away? But if I say nothing and just let him walk up on that stage to accept the award, stand in front of everyone and say "hard work pays off" … that would be the real disaster. I need to get the full picture first, find the right moment — I can't just drop this on him when he's this happy, but I can't let it go either. Tonight isn't the moment, but this can't be put off.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Conflict and suppression dominate, threaded through with a faint, quiet ache. The child's beaming face and the words on that note create a sharp contrast, stirring a heaviness I can't release right now; the old instinct to hold back — learned from watching conflicts explode in childhood — is also whispering at me not to act rashly. Moderate intensity: restrained, but real.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since I was small that forcing a head-on collision at the peak of someone's emotions only makes everything messier — so my instinct is telling me this isn't the moment to speak up. But this has to be faced. I just need to find a steadier way to do it.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, getting the full picture and finding the right moment matters more than blowing up on the spot; but letting my child stand on that commendation stage carrying a lie — that's something I simply cannot accept.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation in this moment — but I've already made up my mind to find the right time tonight to talk with him alone. For now I respond to him calmly, saying, 'The speech sounds good — but let's sit down a bit later and talk through the competition thing more carefully,' keeping my tone perfectly ordinary, nothing out of the usual.

inner_consciousness.summary: That message on my phone screen hit me like a stone to the chest — the club president had bailed. I stared at those words, ears ringing, three people around me with their heads down, and that silence weighed more than anything anyone could have said. I knew they were waiting for me to say "let's cancel" — that way everyone gets off the hook. But that activity room, all those plans I wrote out line by line, the auditorium we fought so hard to book… a whole month, and not just my month. Mom's voice from last night came back: "If you don't crack the top hundred, forget about summer camp" — I was already barely holding on, and if I say give up right now, doesn't that just prove I really can't do anything right? There's a voice in my head screaming: you can't carry this alone, you're going to mess it up, you're going to let everyone down. But there's another voice — if I give up on even this, what does that make me? I can't let everything people have already put in just rot away like that. I can't be that person again — the one who can't do anything right, who can't hold anything together. Even if I'm terrified the whole way through, even if I really do mess it up, I have to try to take this on.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety (core): rooted in a deep fear of failure and rejection — the president's sudden disappearance has triggered a sense of abandonment; Shame (underlying): fear that giving up will once again prove she "can't do anything right"; Defiant resolve tinged with tragedy (surface-level drive): a preemptive self-attack that paradoxically pushes her forward, because "actively shouldering it" preserves what little sense of self-worth she has left, more than "being forced to quit" ever could. Intensity: highly strung, teetering on the edge of collapse but not yet there.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been through it too many times — pulling back at the critical moment and then drowning in self-blame — this time I know instinctively that if I say "cancel" right now, that voice saying "you can't do anything right" will be louder than any criticism anyone else could throw at me. I'd rather charge in and crash and burn than prove that voice right before I've even started.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, "not letting everything people have already put in go to waste" matters more than "protecting myself from getting hurt"; what I cannot accept is this: snuffing it out with my own hands while there's still a breath left in it, and then consoling myself with "I always knew I wasn't good enough."

pairs.final_decision: I choose to take over all the preparation work the president left behind on my own, pushing the event forward through sheer willpower, refusing to agree to cancellation. I take a deep breath, voice trembling slightly but I speak anyway: 'We're not canceling. I'll take it from here. Help as much as you can — if you can't, I'll figure it out myself.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择独自接手社长留下的全部筹备工作，强撑着推进活动，不同意取消。我深吸一口气，声音有点发抖但还是开口说：'不取消，我来接。你们能帮多少算多少，帮不了我自己想办法。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择不直接取消，先接住筹备并立刻缩方案。 我说先别散，我今晚重排清单，明天去找团委申请精简和延期备案。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择逃避巨大压力，同意取消活动并自我贬低。我躲开他们的视线，死死掐住手心说：“那就取消吧，反正我也弄不好，我去跟团委说。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment his gaze landed on me, it was like someone had reached into my chest and squeezed my heart. Every head in the class turned — that feeling, I know it too well, well enough to make me sick. The weak-kneed sensation of standing at the front of the classroom as a kid, the sound of people laughing at me, it all came flooding back at once. I kept my head down, flipping through my notebook — the real reason being that I knew he had it wrong. I'd spent an entire weekend with that book: the archives, the citations, the most recent scholarship, all of it laid out plain as day. But now he'd called my name, everyone was watching, and my throat was starting to close up. I knew the evidence was real — but if I said something, would he frown? Would he correct me in front of the whole class? Would I become that student who 'doesn't know her place'? And then everyone would remember me as the one who made a fool of herself. But I also knew that if I said nothing — just nodded and said 'no problem' — all those dense, careful notes would have meant nothing, that whole weekend would have been wasted, and the historical claim he made really is wrong. I don't want to shrink back again, and yet I'm scared — scared enough that my palms are soaked. In the end I decided to stand up, but I would keep my voice soft, careful, framing it as a question rather than a challenge, giving him enough face to save, and leaving myself a way out.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety (highly activated, layered over countless memories of humiliation from being called on in class); Conflict (the urge to speak the truth pulling against the fear of being shot down); A faint but creeping sense of anticipated shame (already rehearsing the scene of being dismissed); A fragile yet genuine intellectual confidence (she really did read that book — one of the very few handholds she has)
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every single time I've opened my mouth in a classroom, for as long as I can remember, something has gone wrong — so my instinct is to retreat. But this time I actually have real evidence in my hands. If I can't even speak up now, I'll have fully become that person who hides in the corner forever, and that feeling is worse than being contradicted.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'verifiable truth' is the one thing I can hold onto — if I give that up too, I have nothing left. But I can't bear the thought of being humiliated in public, of being dismissed by the teacher in front of everyone. So I have to find a way to say it that lets me get the words out without leaving myself completely exposed to that shame.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to stand up and respond, but I wrap my disagreement in an extremely cautious, deferential register — framing the challenge inside a shell of 'I may have misunderstood' to reduce the risk of being attacked. I stand, my voice slightly trembling, eyes still lowered: 'Professor, I came across a book about this period of history last week — it cited some primary source documents, and it seemed like… there might be a small discrepancy with the conclusion you mentioned. Is it possible I've misread it? Could you help me work through it?'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择站起来回应，但用极度谨慎、请教式的措辞来表达异议，把质疑包裹在'我可能理解有误'的外壳里以降低被攻击的风险。我站起来，声音有些发颤，低着头说：'老师，我上周读到一本关于这段历史的书，里面引用了一些原始档案，好像……跟您讲的这个结论有一点点出入，是不是我理解得不对？可以请您帮我解释一下吗？'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择委婉表达异议，不正面顶撞老师。我站起来先说“老师，我可能理解得不够完整，但我在书里看到另一种说法，想请教一下。”
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择彻底退缩，用自我贬低来逃避冲突。我慌乱地站起来，双手死死捂住笔记本，低着头结巴地说：“没、没有想法……我只是刚才没听懂，有点走神。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment the door shut, I felt it was over — like I'd been shoved under the spotlight again, the easiest one to pin it all on. The director was staring at me, and that line about 'mailing it home' twisted my stomach in a knot; the back of my head still stung from that hit, and that person's threat was ringing in my ears. I knew what I saw, and I knew that staying quiet might drag me down with everyone else — but if I actually spoke up, I could already feel it landing on my face: the looks in the classroom tomorrow, getting cornered in the hallway, everyone assuming I was the snitch. The more scared I got, the more I felt like some weak, pathetic person — I even wanted to tear into myself first, just to beat everyone else to it. But then I thought about the disciplinary notice being mailed home, about being labeled the 'problem kid' all over again, and I just couldn't hold it together. All I wanted was to get myself out from under this as fast as possible, even if things got worse afterward.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear, Shame, Panic, and a sense of Wronged Helplessness. The fear is intense — it comes from the threat I just received, the looming punishment, and the image of being interrogated at home all over again; the shame comes from the way being put under an authority figure's gaze instantly makes me feel like I've done something wrong; the sense of being wronged comes from the fact that I wasn't the one who brought it in, yet I might get lumped in anyway.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know all too well that once I'm branded an accomplice, every bit of blame will come pouring down on me — so my instinct is to pull myself out of the picture first. More than protecting anyone else, I'm terrified of ending up in that place again where no one believes me and I still have to go home and face another round of interrogation.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me right now is protecting myself first — not letting this spiral into my parents, my teachers, and my classmates all closing in on me at once. What I absolutely cannot accept is taking the fall for something I didn't do, sacrificing myself for some idea of loyalty, and then having not a single person speak up for me.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to tell the truth about who brought the contraband in, prioritizing my own protection and making clear I had no part in it. I'll quietly tell the director exactly who I saw and what happened — and I'll also let him know that person just threatened me.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择模糊应对、尽量不直接指名，试图在两边的压力之间找一条最小伤害的缝隙。对主任说：'我确实看到有人在打牌，但我没看清是谁带来的，我真的不知道。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择如实说出带牌的人，优先自保并否认自己参与。 我会低声把我看到的人和经过说清楚，也告诉主任那个人刚才威胁我。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择屈服于权威的施压，供出带头打牌的同学。我死死低着头，眼泪砸在鞋面上，声音发抖地念出了那个同学的名字，然后指甲掐着手心不停地小声说“对不起”。

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment the note unfolded, my heart was seized like someone had grabbed it with both fists — that round, bubbly handwriting, that little sun doodle, the big banyan tree after school. I know what this means. I've written about it in my diary more times than I can count. But my hands hadn't even stopped shaking yet when my best friend leaned over: 'Who's passing you notes?' — and my blood just froze. What she said to me last week detonated inside my head: 'I think I've fallen for someone.' She likes the same person. I'd just patted her on the shoulder back then, said nothing, because the words simply wouldn't come. And now this note is in my hands, burning like a hot iron. If she sees it — what will she think of me? Will she think I've been lying to her this whole time, sneaking around behind her back to steal the person she likes? I didn't steal anything. I just fell for them first — but what good does saying that do? Would she even believe me? Or would she decide I'm not worth trusting as a friend at all? I can feel my eyes starting to sting, that familiar feeling of everything about to fall apart. No. I can't let her see. I'm not even sure whether being called out there means they actually like me — maybe they just want to reject me in person, like that time with the letter, right in front of everyone — no, I can't go there. All I know is that right now I have to hide this note first. I have no right to hurt her, and I don't have the courage to lay myself bare.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Panic (core): the memory of my best friend's confession and the note activate simultaneously, triggering a double fear of abandonment and of betraying someone I love — intensity extremely high; Shame (underlying): shame at secretly liking someone while being unable to be honest, layered over countless past memories of being seen through and mocked; Longing (suppressed): the genuine flutter of feeling is crushed beneath the fear, barely able to breathe.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many moments where 'exposing myself' led to public humiliation, my instinct tells me the most important thing right now is to hide the note and keep anyone from seeing it — even if that means lying to my closest friend, even if I still don't know whether I'll actually go after school.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'not hurting her' and 'not being seen through' matter equally — but both are built on avoidance. What I cannot bear is letting my best friend get hurt because of me, and I equally cannot bear exposing the most vulnerable part of myself in front of someone else all over again.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hide the contents of the note — I shield it with my body, fold it quickly, and stuff it back into my textbook, then brush off my best friend with a lie. As for whether to show up after school, I haven't decided yet, but I'm leaning toward not going, or putting off the decision until the very last second. I press my textbook down over the note and look up at her with a small smile: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking to borrow my notes.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择隐瞒纸条内容，用身体遮住纸条并迅速折叠塞回课本，同时对好朋友撒谎敷衍；关于赴约，内心尚未决定，但倾向于不去或拖到最后一刻才做决定。我把课本压在纸条上，抬头对她笑了一下：'没什么，就是同学问我借笔记的。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择先瞒住朋友，但放学后去赴约。我立刻把纸条合上压进课本里，说没什么，放学我会去榕树下。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择掩盖真相与彻底逃避。我迅速将纸条揉碎塞进桌斗，谎称是别人传错的废纸，放学后直接回家，绝不赴约。

inner_consciousness.summary: Those words are still burning behind my eyes — "we never should have let her hang out with us" — I thought I'd braced myself for the worst, but actually seeing that message was like having something scooped out of my chest. Now she's sitting right next to me, acting like nothing happened, "don't overthink it, okay," then she turns away laughing, passes her phone over, the two of them covering their mouths, and that invisible wall goes up again — thicker than it was in the cafeteria. I know that screenshot was about me. I know she knows I know. And yet there she is, standing there like it's nothing. There's something pushing up my throat — the words I want to hurl at her, tears, that feeling I've been stockpiling since I was six years old: "why does no one ever believe me, why am I always the one left on the outside." But if I open my mouth, if I bring up the screenshot, she'll deny it — or she'll say "you were snooping through someone else's feed," and then I become the weird one, the one making a mountain out of a molehill. I'm already "the girl who thinks too highly of herself" — I can't hand them any more ammunition. My eyes are burning. I force my head down and push everything — all of it — back under. Hold it down. Just get through today.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: A dull, blunt pain of betrayal (from seeing the screenshot with my own eyes and confirming I've been talked about and shut out); Shame and self-doubt (years of "is there something wrong with me" converging all at once in this moment); Helplessness and suppression (I want to confront her but instinctively play out the worst-case scenario, feeling that speaking up will only humiliate me further). Intensity is extremely high — teetering on the edge of collapse, but forcibly held down.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every time I've spoken up for myself since I was little, the outcome was always that I ended up looking stranger, more out of place — so my gut tells me that confronting her now will only make me lose more completely. Silence at least preserves the last small piece of myself that hasn't been entirely destroyed.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: The thing I can't stand most is being treated like I don't exist, right to my face — it's worse than being insulted outright. But I equally cannot let them see how much I care, how badly I'm hurting, because that means they win — it means I really am the kind of person they said I was, the one who "thinks too highly of herself."

pairs.final_decision: I choose to swallow the confrontation — to hold it down and fake composure long enough to get through this moment — without bringing up the screenshot or the private group chat. I keep my head low, eyes fixed on the surface of the desk, giving no response to what she said, not glancing at either of their phones, just gripping my sleeve cuffs tight in my fist and waiting for the burning behind my eyes to slowly fade.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择压下质问、用沉默和假装镇定撑过这一刻，不当面提截图和小群的事。低着头把视线钉在桌面上，没有回应她的话，也没有看那两个人的手机，只是用手攥紧袖口，让眼眶的热度慢慢退下去。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择当面戳破，不再假装不知道。我压着哭腔问她：小群和那句“烦死了”，也是我多想吗？
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避冲突，用冷漠和主动切割来保护自己。我僵硬地说“没事”，然后低头拿出手机，默默退出了原来的群聊。

inner_consciousness.summary: That gesture — the way she yanked her sleeve down — I saw it, I really saw it. Those red marks, some deep, some faint, some still fresh. My stomach dropped like someone had reached in and grabbed it. Her eyes were fixed on me, waiting for me to say something, and my mind exploded with two things at once: one was her, and one was me. She said not to tell anyone — said if I did, she'd never speak to me again — and that 'never' was like a needle, finding exactly the place I'm most afraid of. All at once I thought of so many times before: being pushed away, being treated like I was invisible, being told 'you're too sensitive' — that hollow feeling in my chest. I was terrified she'd actually leave me. But then I looked at those wounds again. Those fresh wounds. I know I should tell an adult. I know this isn't an ordinary secret. But the moment I say something, she'll hate me — I'll be that person again, the one who betrayed her friend, the one who lets everyone down. The two things tore at each other inside me. My hands were shaking, the wind was still rushing in, and she was still waiting. I can't let her carry this alone — but I can't bring myself to promise her secrecy either. I can't make that promise, because I'm scared something will happen to her, scared she'll cut again, scared that one day I'll regret it. I want to steady her first. Let her know I'm here. Then figure out what to do.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (the threat of abandonment has activated my deepest dread): the 'never speak to me again' landed exactly where I'm most vulnerable; Heartache and helplessness (the raw, real pain of seeing those wounds): the moment I saw them, something in me broke; Anticipatory guilt (already blaming myself in advance for 'betraying' her): I'm pre-emptively punishing myself before I've even done anything; Torn apart (the irreconcilable pull between protecting her and keeping her). Emotional source: every memory of being pushed away, of being treated like I wasn't there — all of it surges up right now and layers itself onto this moment, making 'losing her' hit my nerves before 'her being hurt' does — even though I know, rationally, that's wrong.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many moments where telling the truth only got me abandoned, my instinct is to protect this friendship first — to keep her from feeling like I've betrayed her. But at the same time I know those wounds are not a secret I can carry alone, so I won't make the promise outright. Instead, I'll reach for her hand first and tell her I'm not going anywhere.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: Right now, the most important thing to me is that she still trusts me enough to have shown me this — because losing that trust would be proof, once again, that I'm the kind of person who disappoints everyone. But what I cannot live with is this: if I stay silent and she adds another wound, and I did nothing — that regret would be harder to swallow than her hating me.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to promise secrecy right away. Instead, I steady her first — with my body and my words — so she feels caught, held. And inside, I've already decided I need to find a way to tell an adult. I take her hand, my voice shaking a little: 'I'm not going anywhere, I'm not going anywhere at all — but I can't promise to keep this secret, because I'm scared something will happen to you, and I'm more afraid of losing you than I am of you being angry at me. Can we just talk first? Tell me what's actually been going on with you lately.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择不立刻答应保密，而是先用身体和语言稳住她、让她感到被接住，同时在心里已经决定要找一个方式告诉大人。我握住她的手，声音有点抖：'我不走，我哪儿都不去——但我没办法答应你保密，因为我怕你出事，我比失去你更怕你出事。我们先说说，好不好，你最近到底发生了什么。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择不答应绝对保密，但先尽力安抚、陪住她。我要先说我不会丢下你，然后陪你去找老师或校心理老师。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择答应保密，独自背负巨大的焦虑。我反握住她的手，声音发抖地说：‘我答应你，我谁都不说，你别不理我。’

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment that voice message ended, my chest tightened — like I could already hear a door slowly closing in front of me. Pay cuts for AI roles, traditional positions getting axed, a one-on-one with my manager tomorrow: those words all stuck together at once, as if to prove I'm about to fall behind again, to be left standing there as the most useless person in the room. I've looked through the courses, I really have — but the second I think about quitting, losing my income, starting over, my stomach twists into a knot, and the mortgage sits on me like a stone. But what scares me even more is staying put, waiting for someone else to declare me obsolete first, to cut me first. I know that feeling of passively waiting for a verdict all too well — the longer I drag it out, the more it looks like I'm just accepting that I'm not good enough. In the end, what actually pushed me to move wasn't courage. It was that I couldn't stand sitting here waiting to be laid off, and I couldn't stand walking into my manager's office tomorrow looking like someone who hadn't prepared for any of this.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety, shame, fear, a sense of being cornered. The anxiety comes from my habit of reading vague signals as bad news; the shame comes from how quickly I jump to wondering whether I've already fallen behind; the fear is dredged up by all those past moments of being criticized, overlooked, and given up on — it hits hard, almost enough to stop my breathing.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know all too well what it feels like to wait for someone else to decide whether I'm worth keeping, even though reinventing myself is terrifying, my gut instinct is that I have to at least make the first move myself — otherwise it's like sitting there waiting to be left behind. More than the active struggle of changing course, what I can't bear is having reality prove, right to my face, that I've already become useless.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters more to me isn't holding onto an old identity with dignity — it's not handing my fate over entirely to someone else's single sentence. The one thing I absolutely cannot accept is seeing the danger coming, pretending everything is fine, and then not even having the chance to speak up for myself when it's too late.

pairs.final_decision: I'm choosing to take the risk and make the transition — but I won't quit cold turkey right away. Instead, I'm going to lay my cards on the table proactively. Tonight I'll sign up for a course and put together a learning plan; tomorrow I'll tell my manager that I want to move into an AI-related role and ask about the possibility of an internal transition.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择以防御性姿态应对，对外表现为'还在考虑中'的模糊立场，既不明确拒绝转型也不承诺行动，同时为明天的谈话准备一套自我贬低式的开场白以抢占先机。我回复前同事：'你比我勇敢，我这边情况复杂，还没想好，你先加油，我再看看'；并在日历邀请上备注：'我知道自己最近状态不稳定，明天想坦诚聊聊，也许我不是最适合留下来的人。'

inner_consciousness.summary: The warm light in the private dining room was almost harsh, and the moment those words — "doing alright income-wise?" — landed, I felt my stomach drop. Everyone's eyes came flooding toward me, full of goodwill, but there was something inside that goodwill, sharp as a needle — I can't quite name it, but I felt it. A voice in my head had already jumped ahead: you're the only one at this table who rents, the only one doing nonprofit work, the only one without a property, a car, a kid in an international school. They're not asking about you — they're shining a light on the distance between you and them. I thought of the red X's on my math tests as a kid, of all those times I cried in the corner of the hallway, of every time something of mine was called 'ordinary.'
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Defensive shame (core): the instant class contrast at the table triggered memories — stretching all the way back — of being dismissed and overlooked; the shame spread outward from somewhere in my stomach. Anxiety (high intensity): every pair of eyes converging on me brought back that familiar dread of being scrutinized. Forced self-protection: I don't want anyone to see through to the vulnerability, and that's driving an impulse to hold myself together through sheer will.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since I was little that the moment someone sees the side of you that's 'not good enough,' something shifts in their eyes — even if they mean well, it shifts — so my instinct is to get ahead of their judgment, to frame myself as someone who 'chose this life,' not someone who 'ended up here.'
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not being pitied as a failure — even if my life looks 'behind' by their standards, I have to make it sound like the outcome of a deliberate choice. What I cannot bear is having the painting, the exhibition, the things I actually care about reduced to a consolation prize at this table — met with some 'oh, still keeping at it, huh.'

pairs.final_decision: I chose to go on the offensive — to strike first with a breezy, self-assured tone, packaging my life as a clear-eyed choice rather than a losing position, and dropping a mention of the art show as a line of defense. I smiled, picked up my glass, and said: 'Ha, I'm running projects at a nonprofit right now — the pay's nothing to write home about, honestly — but last year I had a painting selected for the local young artists' exhibition. I feel like the life I'm living is the life I actually want. As for the whole housing thing — I'll get to it eventually.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择主动出击、用轻描淡写的自信语气先发制人，把生活包装成一种清醒的选择而非落败的现状，同时顺带提一句画展作为防线。我笑了笑，端起酒杯说：'哈，我现在在一家公益机构做项目，工资确实不高，但去年我有幅画入选了本地青年艺术展——我觉得我现在过的就是我想过的日子，房子的事嘛，以后再说。'

decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避冲突、顺从并自我贬低以转移焦点。我端起清酒杯掩饰微微发抖的手，挤出笑脸说：“我就是瞎忙，哪能跟你们比啊，还是老老实实当个穷人吧，快说说你那新车开着怎么样？”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment that hard drive was lifted into the air, my stomach clenched like someone had grabbed it in their fist — and then he slapped the money down on the coffee table. That sound, that gesture — something snapped inside my head. Six months. Staying up until two, three in the morning every single night. Those files, those versions, everything I carried on my own — just like that, smashed by a kid, then settled with a few bills. He said 'what's the big deal,' my mom said 'don't make people think you're being petty,' and I'm standing here feeling like I've turned back into that child who broke a cup and had everyone staring at me — feeling like I'm back in that hallway being called 'ordinary' — feeling like I'm back in that conference room being told I had 'an attitude problem.' I want to cry, but if I cry he'll think I'm making a scene over nothing. I want to lose it, but my mom just sent a voice message, and if I lose it then it'll be my fault. That money sitting on the coffee table feels like an insult — like it's saying: this is what your work is worth, this is what your six months adds up to, stop being so dramatic. But I genuinely cannot take that money. Taking it would mean admitting this is something that can be handled this way — admitting that I am someone who can be handled this way. There's something lodged in my throat, burning, and I can't get it out — but I know that if I say nothing right now, I will disappear right here in this room.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anger (suppressed — like a red-hot iron being forced underwater): the sound of that money hitting the table was too loud to pretend away; Shame (being dismissed and paid off has activated every old memory of being told 'what's the big deal'): a deep sense of being looked down on and brushed aside; Despair (the files on that drive may truly be gone, those six months may truly have vanished): a grief that has no fix; a suffocating feeling of being on the verge of breaking. Anger and shame are surging up simultaneously, amplifying each other, at an extremely high intensity.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since I was small that whenever I made a mistake or was publicly dismissed, everyone would decide I was making a mountain out of a molehill — so my instinct is to push my own anger down first. But this time the sound of that money hitting the table was too loud. Too loud for me to pretend nothing happened. I cannot take that money, because taking it would mean agreeing that 'this is no big deal.'
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: I cannot accept that something I spent six months grinding out could be 'resolved' with a stack of cash. This isn't about the money — it's that this situation should never have been treated this way to begin with. What I cannot bear is being written off as 'dramatic' — being labeled like that is more suffocating than the hard drive being destroyed in the first place.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to refuse the money and, in a trembling but clear voice, speak the true weight of what was lost — not to demand compensation, but to keep myself from disappearing into silence. I push the stack of bills back across the table, my voice shaking a little: 'Uncle, this isn't about replacing a hard drive. There were six months of my work on there — things that may be gone forever. I need you to understand how serious this is.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择拒绝接钱、用颤抖但清醒的声音说出损失的真实重量，不是为了索赔，而是不让自己在沉默里消失。我把那叠钱推回去，声音有点抖：'大伯，这不是买几块硬盘的问题，里面有我六个月的工作，那些东西可能找不回来了，我需要你知道这件事有多严重。'

inner_consciousness.summary: Her eyes are this red… the tissue in her hands is practically shredded from squeezing. I'm sitting here and my heartbeat starts going haywire, that familiar twisting in my stomach coming back again. She said "I'm not asking you to report it for me" — but then she also said "if you're willing to help me." Both sentences land on me at the same time, and I don't know which way to lean. That senior colleague… the power dynamics are complicated… my mind is already running through it: if I get involved, how will he see me? How will the company see me? Will I become the next person who gets frozen out? I know that feeling all too well — being pinned in place by everyone's stares, not a single person saying one word for you, Dad sitting right there beside me, not even lifting his head. That hollow emptiness is something I'll never forget for the rest of my life. But she's sitting right across from me right now, and she came to me — because she trusts me. Her phone buzzed again and the color drained from her face. If I pull back now, what makes me any different from all those people who stayed silent? I'm scared. I really am scared. But what scares me even more is this: if I do nothing, I'll become the person who left her to carry it alone — and I know what that feels like, I know the weight of being abandoned like that. I can't pretend I didn't see this. I just can't.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (stemming from anticipating professional risk and social isolation, which activates memories of being ignored and blamed as a child with no one to speak up for me) intertwined with intense empathic pain (her situation strikes directly at my own wound of abandonment); alongside this, a fierce refusal to stay silent — the intensity is overwhelming, nearly drowning out every instinct toward self-preservation.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through moments when no one stepped forward, and that shame and loneliness can settle into your body for a very, very long time — so I find myself unable to accept that staying silent right now would be anything other than a betrayal: a betrayal of her, and of the version of me who once curled up small in a corner.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not becoming the person who "never looked up" — even if there's a price to pay. What I cannot accept is this: seeing it clearly, and then pretending not to see it just to spare myself the trouble, leaving her to face that man's one-on-one meeting tomorrow all on her own.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to step in — but in a way that puts protecting her first, without pushing for a formal report just yet. I place my hand over hers, my voice shaking a little, but I speak anyway: 'You don't have to carry this alone tonight. That meeting tomorrow — I'm going to figure out a way to make sure it doesn't happen. But first, tell me everything he's done.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择介入，但以保护林小雨为优先、暂不强推正式举报的方式回应她的求助。我把手放在她的手上，声音有点抖，但我还是开口说：'你今晚不用一个人扛这件事了，明天那个会议，我来想办法让它不发生——你先告诉我，他都做过什么。'

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment he said "unrealistic," my stomach clenched. Those looks, those pauses — they were all saying I'd screwed up again. My mind flashed through every memory of being shot down — getting cut off in group discussions, my advisor frowning during my defense, a friend telling me I was "too much" — playing on a loop like an old tape. I knew that if I pushed back, he'd think I couldn't take advice, that I didn't know my place, and then he'd stop taking me seriously. But if I backed down, all that data, all those controlled experiments — wasted. My palms were soaked, my throat was tight, and there was a weight pressing down on my chest. I heard myself thinking: "Just let it go, don't make him angry, at least don't get completely written off." But another voice was screaming: "Are you really going to do yourself in again?" What finally pushed me over the edge was that familiar panic — being abandoned is more terrifying than being proven wrong.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Shame, fear, anxiety. The shame stems from 'my proposal being dismissed in front of others' and a long-accumulated memory of 'I always get things wrong'; the fear comes from the activation of an abandonment schema — 'he will give up on me'; the anxiety is a physiological response to the conflict itself and its possible consequences. Intensity is extremely high, nearly out of control.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every time I was questioned growing up, it meant being looked down on and shut out — so my instinct tells me that the moment I make him unhappy, he'll discard me. Even with evidence in my hands, even knowing I'm right, I can't bring myself to gamble on the possibility that he might actually give up on me. Backing down preemptively at least lets me hold onto the relationship, even if the price is surrendering my own judgment.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, being needed — not being abandoned — matters more than being proven right. What I cannot bear is that look that says 'you're not good enough, so I don't want you anymore.' That look makes me feel like I have no worth at all. Even if it means swallowing my pride and giving up on what I believe, as long as I can stay in this relationship, I still have a reason to exist.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to give in, to move forward with the existing plan — but inside I'm full of resentment and self-doubt. I lower my head and say: "I understand. We'll go with what you said — I'll get the materials filled in as soon as possible." My voice comes out barely above a whisper, like an apology.

decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避冲突，全盘妥协。我僵硬地把那份说明从桌角抽回来，低着头小声说：“对不起老师，是我没想清楚，我这就回去按原方案补材料。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The things he said — co-founder, CTO, 8%, eight million — those words hit me like bullets, and my first reaction wasn't excitement, it was that familiar thing rising up from my stomach. He's looking at me, eyes lit up, like he genuinely believes I can do this. But me… what gives me the right? He said he read my competition proposal, but that was just a proposal — I happened to stumble onto a few ideas, what if he's overestimated me? What if I say yes and then blow it, and then the way he looks at me — that look of disappointment, I've seen it too many times, from my parents, from teachers, from teammates. My palms are a little damp. I'm staring at that financial projection model — eighty million, Series A — what do any of those numbers have to do with me? Am I really the kind of person who can hold up an entire company? Taking a gap year, my advisor sent a message just this morning — lab meeting, progress report — that's what I'm supposed to be doing. But he said the window is only six months… if I say no, will he think I'm a coward? If I say yes and then fail? I need to say something first, I can't stay silent too long, he'll see that I'm panicking. I need to come across like someone who can actually think this through, even though my head is a complete mess right now.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Dread (core): rooted in years of being told 'you're not good enough' — when faced with a high-expectation invitation, my first instinct is that I don't deserve it; Excitement quickly suppressed: the opportunity itself touches a deep longing to be recognized, but it's immediately swallowed by the fear of 'what if I mess it up'; Anticipatory shame: I'm already picturing the scene after the failure — that feeling of being seen through, of being a disappointment, feels more real than any concrete risk.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been through too many cycles of being overestimated and then letting someone down in humiliation, I instinctively read this opportunity as a trap — not one set by anyone else, but one my own inadequacy will turn it into.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: Being recognized matters more to me than almost anything, but being recognized and then screwing it up — letting the other person down — is more terrifying than never having been seen at all. What I cannot live with is this: saying yes, and then using my own failure to prove he had the wrong person.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to stall with a vague, positive-sounding response rather than actually commit — neither refusing nor promising, keeping a way out for myself. I smiled, pressed my hands against my knees to hide the trembling, and said: "I'm honestly so surprised you thought of me — this opportunity… I need to think about it seriously. Everything you said, I've taken it all in. I'll give you an answer by Tuesday for sure."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择用模糊的正面回应拖延真正表态，既不拒绝也不承诺，为自己保留退路。我笑了笑，把手放在膝盖上压住颤抖，说：「学长，我真的很意外你会想到我，这个机会……我需要认真想想，你说的那些我都听进去了，周二之前我一定给你答复。」
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择表达强烈心动，但暂不当场承诺。我说这机会对我很重要，我认真想两天，也想先和导师、家里谈清楚。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择退缩与自我贬低，试图通过暴露自己的无能来推开这个高风险的邀请。我下意识地避开他发亮的眼神，手指在桌下死死绞在一起，小声说：“学长，我真的没你想的那么厉害……休学我做不到，我太怕把你的项目搞砸了。”

inner_consciousness.summary: Those blue underlined lines on the screen drove into my eyes like nails, a hundred-percent match on the search results page — my heart lurched into a sprint and my palms went damp instantly. I spent three days combing through sources, wrote nearly four thousand words, and this is a core course for my major; if my grade gets wiped to zero… I can't even let myself think past that point. Then I saw his WeChat Moments post from two in the morning — 'I'm barely holding on' — and something seized my chest. I know that feeling too well. It's like standing at the edge of something years ago, not knowing whether you were about to be pushed off. He's under so much pressure; maybe he really had nowhere left to turn. But what about me? What about my three days? The footsteps in the hallway are getting closer, the door handle just moved, and my whole body froze — I have no idea how to even begin saying something. If I do say it, will he think I'm betraying him? If I don't, our entire group goes down with him. I can't confront him face to face. Just imagining him pushing the door open and finding me sitting at his computer makes my throat tighten. I can't let him know I saw his stuff — that would make everything so much worse. And I can't pretend nothing happened either; those blue underlines have already burned themselves into my brain. All I can do is close his screen first, erase myself from this moment, and then figure out another way — maybe send an anonymous email to the professor, maybe… I don't know. I only know that right now I have to make this room go back to normal.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Panic (racing heartbeat, sweaty palms: triggered by the threat of having my grade in a core course wiped to zero); a tearing sense of being caught between two impossible choices (pulled between sympathy for my roommate and the need to protect my own interests); the impulse to avoid (rooted in memories of countless times I worked up the courage to speak the truth and was turned on, isolated, labeled a troublemaker); Shame rehearsal (from imagining being caught 'snooping' on someone else's computer). Intensity: extremely high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I've lived through too many moments where I summoned the courage to tell the truth and ended up being accused, shut out, and cast as the one who causes problems — so I know instinctively that confronting him directly will only make me the person who destroys the relationship, not the person who fixes anything. I have to pull myself out of this scene first, then find a way to handle it that doesn't require me to expose myself.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not being seen as someone who sells out another person — while also not standing by and watching three days of my own work come to nothing. What I cannot accept is a face-to-face confrontation: the look in his eyes when he sees me 'accusing' him, the feeling of a relationship shattering in an instant — that is harder for me to bear than any other consequence.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to sidestep the direct confrontation for now — quietly close my roommate's laptop screen, act as though nothing happened, and privately turn over in my mind the idea of contacting the professor anonymously afterward. I quickly fold his screen shut, retreat to my own chair, and fix my eyes back on my own document; by the time the door handle turns, my expression has already settled back into the look of someone who has seen nothing at all.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂时回避直接冲突，先悄悄关上室友电脑屏幕，假装什么都没发生，同时在内心盘算事后以匿名方式联系教授。我迅速把他的电脑屏幕合上，退回自己的椅子，重新盯着自己的文档，当门把手转动的那一刻，我的表情已经恢复成了什么都没发生过的样子。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择当面摊牌，要求他立刻重写，不替他隐瞒也不先告教授。我关掉搜索页面等他回来，直接说我看见了重复内容，这份不能交，今晚必须改。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避正面冲突，暂时隐瞒发现。我猛地关掉他电脑上的浏览器页面，迅速转回自己的书桌前，死死盯着自己的屏幕假装在打字。

inner_consciousness.summary: The pill bottle rolled to my feet with a soft rattling sound, and my heart stopped completely for that one second. I bent down and picked it up — almost empty. Those two words detonated inside my head like a flash of light that made everything too bright, too sharp. Two weeks of drawn curtains, takeout boxes, those muffled sobs in the middle of the night — all of it collapsed into a single, complete, terrifying shape in that one second. He said 'just kick it back,' his voice so hoarse it sounded like it had crawled up from somewhere very deep, and my hands started shaking. I wanted to say something, but my throat felt like it was blocked by something — what if I said the wrong thing? What if he thought I was prying? What if opening my mouth pushed him even further away? Those familiar thoughts started flooding in, urging me to shrink back, to put the bottle down and pretend nothing had happened. But that almost-empty bottle was still in my hand, and I held it, feeling the coolness of the glass. I thought about all those times I was at my lowest and just wanted someone to not leave — even if they only stayed right where they were. He asked me why I hadn't gone out tonight, and there was something in that question — it wasn't really asking about me, it was checking whether anyone was there. I can't pretend I didn't hear it.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (rooted in the perception of a real and present danger signal — the pill bottle triggered the deepest panic of 'losing someone'); Frozen impulse (the weight of countless past moments when she held back out of fear of saying the wrong thing); A small but undeniable feeling of being unable to walk away (intense, almost instinctive).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know what it feels like to muffle your crying in the middle of the night and wish someone would just stay — I can't pretend this pill bottle is an ordinary thing, can't kick it back and go on sitting under my desk lamp as if nothing happened.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: Being seen, being kept — that matters more to me than almost anything else. So I can't turn my back on someone who might be disappearing. The thing I could least live with is looking back one day and realizing that on that night, I chose to pretend I hadn't seen.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to stay. I don't return the pill bottle. I use his question as an opening and speak softly, trying to let him know that someone is here. I keep the bottle in my hand without moving, and say quietly: 'I didn't feel like going out tonight. Are you… doing okay over there?'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择留下来，不归还药瓶，以他的问话为入口轻声开口，试图让他知道有人在。我把药瓶握在手里，没有动，轻声说：'我今晚不想出去。你那边……还好吗？'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择主动介入，并立刻寻求外部帮助。我先稳住他说我不把药踢回去、想陪他说话，同时拿手机准备联系辅导员和急救。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择顶着恐惧主动介入。我攥紧药瓶，声音发抖：“我没朋友叫我出去……我嘴笨，但今晚就在这陪你行吗？”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment he walked over, I knew it wasn't good news. That smile — I know that smile, it shows up every single time right before 'trouble's coming.' When he finished talking, my first reaction wasn't anger — it was fear, and then a feeling so familiar it made me sick: I had no way out. Dad's gastroscopy images, Mom's voice dropping low on the phone, the promise I made that I'd 'definitely come home' — all of it flashed through my mind, then got crushed under something bigger and heavier. 'You're the lead writer on this — if you can't make it work, I'd be embarrassed to pull someone else in at the last minute.' That line landed like a nail being driven in. He was telling me that if I said no, I'd be the one who let the whole thing fall apart. I felt my stomach drop. I wanted to say 'I have something going on,' but the words caught in my throat and I started thinking: would he decide I wasn't committed enough? Would he quietly cross my name off some list? Year-end performance reviews, the department KPIs, 'just between the two of us' — he'd put me in a special position, but I couldn't tell whether that position was a trap or a sign of trust. All I knew was that if I let him down, I'd become the person who 'falls through when it counts,' and once that label gets stuck on you, I have no idea how to peel it off. As for my parents… I didn't dare let myself think too deeply about it, because the moment I did I felt like a complete bastard — but if I didn't think about it, I felt like a coward. In the end I heard myself speak, my voice steadier than I expected, but I knew I wasn't actually steady.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (the threat of being abandoned / written off struck the deepest nerve I have); Guilt (the promise to my parents being forced aside — this feeling is like replaying all those old wounds of 'letting people down'); Numbness within compliance (I didn't genuinely want to agree — I just didn't know how to refuse, so I took the path that required the least explaining). Intensity: extremely high, the kind that drowns out rational thought almost instantly.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I learned early on that people who say 'I can't' at critical moments get remembered — not for their courage, but for that one absence. So my instinct is to treat 'saying yes' as the only way to keep myself from being crossed off the list, even if the cost is breaking a different promise.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'not disappointing the person right in front of me' feels more urgent than 'not disappointing the person far away' — because the person in front of me can see me, can evaluate me, can decide whether I'm worth keeping around. I can bear my parents' disappointment, because they won't truly abandon me — but I'm not sure that conclusion is actually true. It's just something I need to believe right now.

pairs.final_decision: I chose to agree to the overtime request, set aside the plan to go home, and didn't raise any conditions or alternatives on the spot. I heard myself say: 'Okay, I'll do my best to carve out time over these two days — we'll do the final review Sunday evening.'

inner_consciousness.summary: The hallway lights flickered on and off, and the moment he brought his cigarette close to my face, my whole body locked up. My legs were shaking — not from the cold, but that kind of shaking I've known my whole life — the kind that comes when someone has you cornered, when someone is staring you down, when there's nowhere to run. The deposit. I know how hard I scraped that money together. But now he's got one foot on my suitcase, and there are two more guys behind him gripping bats, and my mind can't turn over a single thought — just this one long buzzing static. I wanted to say 'this is illegal,' but the words made it all the way to my throat and then crawled back down — he said he knows everyone at the police station. I believe him. I've always believed men like him, because I've never once won a fight like this. My clothes are scattered across the floor — my clothes, my things — and yet I'm standing there like I'm invisible, like something that can be shoved aside at any moment. I think about all those times I was dismissed in front of everyone, all those times I was ground underfoot and still had to smile and say thank you, and all that shame is crashing down on me at once now. I can't call the police — I'm scared, I really am scared — and it's not just him I'm scared of, it's the longer, messier trouble that comes after, it's the fear of losing whatever foothold I still have in this city. The only thing I can do, it seems, is get out of here first. Get out of here alive.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (extreme: the immediate threat of violence layered over a lifetime of memories of being suppressed, unprotected, with no one coming); Shame (deep-seated: being publicly driven out, cleared away like refuse, activating the most core sense of being fundamentally defective); Powerlessness (total: the impulse to fight back immediately overridden by the body's learned knowledge that resistance only brings greater harm); Grief (latent: the deposit is gone, and this city has once again proven she has no solid ground to stand on).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every single time I tried to speak up, from childhood on, it ended in greater humiliation, my body has already learned — in the face of real power, opening your mouth only makes things worse; so right now my instinct puts 'get out alive' ahead of 'get justice,' even if that means absorbing every last bit of the loss.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'not being completely destroyed' matters more than 'winning back that money'; I can't bear to lose the last scrap of footing I have in this strange city, but I equally can't bear to be knocked flat right here in front of everyone — so I choose to leave first, and keep that 'deal with it later' possibility alive for myself.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to comply and leave for now, giving up any confrontation in the moment — but I haven't given up on recovering the deposit. I press that thought down into the deepest part of myself, to be dealt with another day. I lower my head, and in a voice that's barely audible, I say: 'I'm leaving now, but this isn't over.' Then I bend down and start picking up the clothes scattered across the floor, hands trembling, eyes never once lifting to look at him.

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment he took my hand, my heart went haywire — not the good kind of haywire, but that familiar kind, the kind that makes me want to run. Two years — all those nights we had, all those times we laughed and talked about where we'd go someday, and in the end it still fell apart. I thought I'd let go. But now he's sitting right across from me, and his eyes still have that warmth that makes me go soft, and my chest is starting to tighten. He said, "Have you really let go of me completely?" — that sentence was like a needle, jabbing straight into the most unguarded part of me. I haven't fully let go, I know that, and that fact makes me ashamed. But what about my current boyfriend? He's gentle, he's steady, he never makes me guess what he's thinking — what he gives me is the kind of security I've been craving since I was little. If I say something, if I waver, will he just disappear? That feeling of being abandoned — it's coming back. I can't go through that again. But my ex says he's changed, says he's been waiting all this time — what if it's true? What if I turn him down and spend the rest of my life regretting it? No — the fact that I'm wavering right now is already a kind of betrayal in itself. I have to make things clear, but I'm terrified of saying the wrong thing, terrified of the look of disappointment on his face, terrified that I'll start crying mid-sentence and then everything will just fall apart.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: A heart-racing, destabilizing sense of wavering (triggered by my ex's sincerity, two years of emotional memory suddenly flooding back); a deep-seated fear of abandonment (dreading that no matter what I choose, I'll lose someone); Shame (realizing I never fully let go, feeling like I've already been unfaithful to my current boyfriend); a powerful impulse toward self-attack (preemptively condemning myself for my own weakness). Intensity is extremely high — clear thinking is almost impossible.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since I was little that once you disappoint someone, or once they discover you're flawed, they leave — so my instinct is to tear myself down first in front of my ex, while at the same time desperately clinging to the small sense of security my current boyfriend gives me, too afraid to let it shatter.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'not being abandoned' matters more than 'making the right choice'; what I cannot bear is this: letting my current boyfriend find out that I wavered today — because that would prove I'm someone who doesn't deserve to be loved.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to turn down my ex's request to get back together, but my response is vague and riddled with self-attacking apologies — and I decide to hide the details of this meeting from my current boyfriend. I say to my ex: "We can't go back to what we were. You deserve better. I'm sorry for making you wait so long" — then I lower my head, unable to meet his eyes, while inside I've already started running through how I'm going to explain to my current boyfriend why I'm coming home late tonight.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择拒绝前任的复合请求，但态度模糊、充满自我攻击式的道歉，同时决定对现任隐瞒这次见面的细节。我对前任说："我们回不去了，你值得更好的，对不起让你等了这么久"——然后低着头，不敢看他的眼睛，心里已经开始反复盘算要怎么跟现任解释今天为什么回来得晚。

decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择立刻拒绝并切断联系，对现任绝对隐瞒。我猛地抽回手，声音发颤地说：“别再找我了，我已经有新的生活了。”然后抓起包落荒而逃，决定把这件事烂在肚子里。

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment he said it, my stomach clenched. 'Oh, look who's the new boss'——that pause, that glance around the room, that half-smirk——I know this feeling too well. It's like standing at the podium as a kid with the paper shaking in my hands, like being cut off in a meeting while everyone turns to stare at me. He's testing me in front of the whole team. I know that. And I know everyone is waiting to see how I'll react. My heartbeat is already pounding in my ears, my palms are sweating, and several voices are exploding in my head all at once——has he always resented me? Do they all think I'm not cut out for this? Can I actually handle that unfinished module? I don't deserve this seat——he said it out loud, he said it on behalf of everyone. But I can't fall apart. I can't let them see me shaking. If I back down now, I'll confirm every judgment they've already made. But if I force my way through and push back hard, what if I really can't handle it? I should just admit it first, say the worst thing out loud myself, so they can't use it against me——that thought flashed through my mind, familiar as an old friend. No. No, I can't do that. Deep breath. I'm running this meeting.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (being publicly challenged has activated countless memories, stretching back to childhood, of being scrutinized and ridiculed——intensity extremely high); Shame (the feeling of being 'not good enough' surges up like a conditioned reflex); A thread of forced composure (an instinctive awareness that if I fall apart right now, it's completely over)
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I have always seen through myself before anyone else can see through me, my first instinct was to preemptively concede defeat——but this time I realized that if I collapse in front of the whole team, the label of 'defective' will be stuck to me permanently, so I have to hold on, even if it's only on the surface.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What I find most unbearable is being completely seen through in front of everyone——being proven to be 'just as hopeless as they thought.' More important than winning this exchange is not falling apart publicly in this moment——because if I do, I'll never get another chance.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to respond directly in a way that appears composed on the surface but is in fact an act of self-protection——neither pushing back hard nor conceding weakness, pulling the conversation back to the meeting itself. I take a deep breath and say: 'Whether I can handle it or not——we'll find out by doing it. I've already gone through that unfinished module, and that's exactly why we're here today, to align on the schedule. Everyone take a seat. Let's get started.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择用表面平稳、实则带着自我保护意味的方式直接回应，既不正面硬刚也不示弱认输，把话题拉回会议本身。我深吸一口气，开口说：'接不接得住，做了才知道。那个未完成的模块我已经看过了，今天开会就是来对齐排期的——大家都坐下，我们开始。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择压住慌乱，正面接招但不跟着撕破脸。我先说排期会按现状逐项过、未完成模块我会亲自跟进，并请他会后和我单独对齐细节。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择退让讨好，通过先发制人的自我贬低来化解冲突。我勉强挤出一个僵硬的笑，声音发紧地说：'你说得对，那个模块确实太难了，我肯定搞不定，以后还得靠前辈你多指导我才行。'

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment my collar was grabbed, I felt like I'd been nailed to the spot — not out of anger, but because this feeling was so familiar. Everyone's eyes, the phone cameras, the red live-stream dots, all trained on me. When I was six and the vase broke, everyone looked at me exactly like this. I didn't do it. I didn't do it. But no one listened. The viewer count on that live-stream screen kept ticking up, up, up — my face, my face was being seen by hundreds, thousands of people. My phone screen was still lit up, messages in the work group chat, but no one would look, no one would believe it. Something was lodged in my throat — I wanted to explain, but where was my voice? The man with the glasses was blocking the exit. Did I actually do something? No — no, I didn't. But they've already decided. I've already been convicted. Just like when I was little, just like every single time — the more you explain, the more it sounds like you're making excuses; the more you open your mouth, the more it becomes evidence against you. I can feel my face burning, my stomach dropping, my legs going soft. I should cry out. But what good would that do — they'd only film harder. I just want to disappear. I just want all of this to stop.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Panic (extreme intensity): being cornered, live-streamed, and publicly accused instantly reactivates every memory — from childhood to the workplace — of being wrongly blamed and never believed; Shame (extreme intensity): having my face filmed and broadcast triggers the deepest fear of all — 'I am flawed, and everyone will see right through me' — detonating it completely; Helplessness and freeze: every past attempt to speak up and explain only made things worse, so right now her instinct is to lock up entirely, unable to act.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through so many moments of 'I didn't do anything wrong, but no one believes me,' I know instinctively: opening my mouth under this kind of public scrutiny will only make them film harder and convince everyone watching the live stream that I'm guilty — the explanation itself becomes the evidence.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, the most unbearable thing isn't being wrongly accused — it's being seen in my humiliation in front of everyone, having my breakdown captured on camera. That feeling of 'my flaws put on permanent display' suffocates me more than any punishment ever could. I would rather stand there frozen and do nothing than open my mouth and let them film my trembling voice.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to fall into a brief frozen state — neither fleeing nor managing to explain myself — and instinctively lower my head, raising my hand to shield my face from the cameras. I lift my hand to block the phones aimed at me, keep my head down, and say in a voice barely above a whisper: 'I didn't… I was just looking at my phone…'

decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择放弃辩解、陷入僵直与自我封闭。我任由对方揪着衣领，低下头死死躲避所有的镜头，身体控制不住地剧烈发抖，一个字也说不出来。

inner_consciousness.summary: I stared at that project plan for a long time, feeling something slowly sinking inside me. Those flowcharts, those milestones — I know them too well, well enough that my stomach started to ache. The collapse of that project twenty years ago was like an old photograph suddenly falling out of a drawer: that afternoon when the team's morale crumbled, the feeling of standing in the rubble, that shame and helplessness — it never really left. I wanted to say something; my fingers hovered over the comment box, and every sentence took shape in my head only to fall apart. Speak up, and I look like some old-timer pulling rank; stay quiet, and I watch someone walk straight into that same pit. Then my phone buzzed, and that private message made me even more anxious — if someone else has already spotted the problem, what am I still hesitating for? But who am I to say anything? We're not that close. Who do I think I am, swooping in to 'advise' her? Will she think I'm showing off my credentials, implying she's not as good as me? The moment I open my mouth, I'm exposed — what if I say the wrong thing, what if she doesn't appreciate it at all, what if… forget it. I've made a mess of my own life too. That project twenty years ago — wasn't I the one leading it? I'll reply to the private message first. At least that person reached out to me; I don't have to be so afraid of that.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety (core): the trauma of a project failure twenty years ago is directly reactivated — seeing a mirror-image situation triggers an instinctive spike in my heart rate; Shame (underlying): I want to speak up but fear being seen as an old-timer pulling rank, and the terror of self-exposure makes every sentence feel like an announcement of my own inadequacy; Conflicted tension (moderate intensity): I have real experience and genuine goodwill I want to share, yet it's all suppressed by the self-negating voice asking 'who am I to say this'; Mild relief (an outlet): the private message offers a passive, relatively safe point of entry, slightly easing the pressure of having to take the initiative.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because the collapse of that project twenty years ago is a shame I lived through personally, opening my mouth feels instinctively like putting that failure back on display for everyone to see — rather than let people see how much ugliness I've been hiding behind the identity of 'someone who's been there,' I'd rather keep my mouth shut. So I can't bring myself to comment publicly, but I can reply to that private message — because the other person reached out first, which means I'm not forcing my way in.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'not intruding on others' and 'not making myself seem superfluous' matter more than sharing experience — not because I don't care, but because the shame of being seen as someone flaunting their credentials, or of saying something wrong, is more than I can bear. What I cannot accept is this: speaking up with good intentions, only to be seen as a failure using someone else's mistakes to prove I once mattered.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to avoid commenting publicly, and instead respond to my colleague through the relatively safer channel of a private message, without directly engaging with the younger colleague's post. Private message reply: 'I saw it too — I'm a little worried, but it doesn't feel right to say something outright. If you think there's a problem, do you want to talk it through first?'
