decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避公开表态，以自我贬低的方式应对私信。我退出了年轻人的评论区，切到私信回复同事：“我当年也搞砸过，可能没资格评判，还是让ta按自己的节奏来吧。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment he started talking about 'flexibility' and 'independent space,' what exploded in my ears wasn't some new proposal — it was that familiar dread: I'm being slowly edged out of his life. He said he didn't want to leave, but this quiet, restrained way of saying it is scarier than any fight, like he's been turning it over for a long time and all that's left is informing me. My stomach clenched instantly, and all those old shadows came flooding back — being left behind as a kid, being seen as a burden, being told I was too sensitive — until even the way he set down his teacup felt like he was drawing a line between us. My phone lit up for a second and it only made me more panicked, as if the outside world was also urging me to admit that this was really happening. Part of me wanted to lunge forward and demand to know whether there was already someone else; another part immediately turned on myself — of course it's because I'm not good enough, of course that's why someone would want to run. But if I actually nodded and agreed, it would feel no different from handing myself over with my own two hands. So I'm going to hold my ground — at least in this moment, I can't just go along with what he's saying.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Panic, Shame, Anger, a stinging hurt. The panic comes from the fact that the moment I hear the relationship is changing, I immediately picture being left behind, being replaced. The shame comes from how instantly I concluded it must be because I'm not good enough. The anger is because he acts like he'd already made up his mind long ago and is only telling me now. The intensity is overwhelming — it's nearly drowning out any rational thought.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I know that feeling all too well — 'I'm not leaving,' followed by a slow, steady push toward the door — so my instinct reads this as the relationship pulling back. Rather than pretending to be gracious and agreeing, I trust that holding onto the original commitment is safer; at least that way I'm not actively cooperating in my own abandonment.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me in a relationship is being chosen — firmly, unambiguously — not being gently downgraded. I can live with fights, with awkwardness, with humiliation. What I cannot live with is someone saying they love me while asking me to learn how to lose them with dignity.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to refuse the proposed adjustment to our relationship boundaries and demand that everything be said plainly. I don't look at my phone — I keep my eyes on him and say: 'I can't accept this. To me, this is just another way of backing out.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择用表面的沉默和顺从掩盖内心的崩溃，不敢直接拒绝也不敢追问，先发制人地在心里把自己定罪。我低下头，声音很轻地说："你是不是早就觉得我太难相处了……是不是我哪里让你觉得很累？"把所有的锋芒转向自己，让他的话变成对我的审判。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择拒绝关系边界调整，要求把话说透。我先不看手机，盯着他说：我不能接受，这对我就是变相退出。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择将提议灾难化为抛弃。我无视手机，颤声反问：“受够了就直说，别拿独立空间当借口，你是不是想离开我！”

inner_consciousness.summary: Empty. That seat is empty, and I'm just sitting here, the morning light coming in like it's mocking me. They walked in, let out a yawn, and said "let's each do our own thing" — the moment those words landed, I felt something cave in somewhere in my chest. Each do our own thing. Does that mean they don't need me anymore? Does that mean they don't know how to be around me either, so they'd rather just push me away? I immediately thought of that retreating figure I watched leave yesterday, of that closed bedroom door, of the diary left behind on the desk. Am I starting to become someone who doesn't belong here either — even in this home? But I also know they just don't know how to say it, same as me. That line — "this feels a little strange, doesn't it" — they said it felt strange too, they don't know what to do either. We're both just standing lost in this suddenly emptied-out morning. I'm afraid that if we really do each go our own way, I'll be left sitting alone facing that empty seat, and then I'll start crying, and then I'll start feeling like everything is over. But I'm also afraid that if I say "let's stay together," they'll think I'm too needy, too clingy, too much. I don't want them to think I'm a burden. But right now I'm genuinely terrified of being alone.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Loss and fear (rooted in yesterday's separation and the empty seat right in front of me, activating deep-seated childhood fears of being forgotten and abandoned — intensity extremely high); longing for connection yet too ashamed to ask (memories of being resented for being 'too needy' make her instinctively want to take her own needs back); mild self-attack (she has already started blaming herself internally for being 'too fragile').
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many times when 'each do our own thing' eventually turned into real distance, I instinctively read that phrase as a signal — they're drawing a boundary, they don't want to be dragged down by my emotions. But at the same time, I know that if I retreat now, today will be me sitting alone in this hollow house, and that feeling will swallow me whole.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me right now is not having to bear this emptiness alone — even if it just means having someone sitting nearby; but what I cannot accept is letting anyone see how fragile I am, how much I need people. That would make me feel like a useless burden.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to respond — to tuck my need inside a suggestion, phrased lightly, as if it just slipped out, not as if I'm scared. I lift my head, my voice steadier than I expected: "Maybe… let's go out for a walk together — I don't really know what I'd do with myself today anyway."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择主动回应，用一句轻描淡写的话把自己的需求藏在提议里，假装是顺口说出来的，而不是因为害怕。我抬起头，声音比想象中平稳："要不……一起出去走走吧，反正我也不知道今天能做什么。"
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择主动靠近，一起试着面对新的日常。我低声说，那我们出去走走吧，我现在一个人待着会更糟。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避亲密与改变，用虚假的忙碌作为自我保护的硬壳。我垂下眼睛盯着空杯子，声音僵硬地说：“不用了，我正好也有一堆稿子要赶，各忙各的吧”，然后迅速起身走向书房关上门。

inner_consciousness.summary: The light from my phone screen falls on my face, and I stare at that photo — my stomach feels like someone's grabbed it and is pulling it down. That smile — the studio, the awards, the sunlight — every single one of those things is something I said "I want that too" about twenty years ago. The steering wheel is still warm, the engine is still running, but my hands have stopped listening to me; they're just gripping, white-knuckled. Twenty years. I told myself it was maturity, it was responsibility, it was love. But right now those reasons are lodged in my throat — I can't say them out loud and I can't swallow them down either. That afternoon in the coffee shop comes flooding back — we said "we're going to live differently," I said "I want that too," and then I chose safety. I have always chosen safety. The most familiar voice in my head starts up: see, this is exactly who you are, you never had the guts, you were never good enough. Knocking yourself down first always feels a little better than waiting for something else to knock you down. There's dinner waiting for me at home tonight. Things to do tomorrow. A deadline next week. All of that is real — and yet right now it all feels paper-thin, one poke and it falls apart. I'm sitting here in respectable clothes, living inside a "right answer" I built with my own hands, and I feel like I have never truly existed.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Emptiness and collapse (core): seeing someone else live the life I once longed for, twenty years of self-persuasion collapses in an instant, leaving a hollowed-out feeling; Shame and self-attack (habitually triggered): rooted in childhood rejection, public humiliation in adolescence, and the accumulated weight of countless criticisms at work — the thought "I chose wrong" immediately converts to "I was never good enough to begin with"; Grief and helplessness (underlying): not anger, but something deeper — a mourning for what has already slipped away, and the intensity of it is overwhelming.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I learned from an early age to reject myself before anyone else could reject me, my instinct right now is to turn "I took the wrong path" into "I was always just the kind of person who lacks courage" — that way it's at least my own choice, rather than being abandoned by fate.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, "not having lived for nothing" matters more than anything — but what I cannot bring myself to accept is this: those twenty years of "being responsible" may have been nothing more than an excuse I used to cover up my fear, and I don't even dare admit that to myself right now.

pairs.final_decision: I allow myself a brief breakdown at the intersection, but in the end I use self-attack to seal off any genuine questioning, substituting "pretending to accept" for truly facing it. I flip my phone face-down, take a deep breath, and murmur to myself: "You didn't have a choice back then — this is just who you are" — then I put the car in gear and drive home.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择在路口短暂崩溃，但最终用自我攻击封堵真正的质疑，以"假装接受"代替真正面对。我把手机屏幕扣下去，深吸一口气，低声对自己说："你当时没得选，你就是这样的人"，然后挂上挡，把车开回了家。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择不直接回家，也不假装无事发生，先承认这场崩塌。 我把车停到路边，关掉社交页面，给自己记下一段话，晚点再回家。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择压抑崩溃，用恶毒的自我贬低来合理化当年的懦弱，继续维持表面的安稳。我锁掉手机屏幕扔到副驾驶，用力抹去眼角的湿意，踩下油门朝着那个让我窒息却安全的家开去。

inner_consciousness.summary: The doctor's text is still glowing on the screen, and the study lamp is casting the family photo in such sharp relief — and here I am, caught in the middle, unable to move. That result… I've turned it over in my head more times than I can count, and every time it gets a little heavier. The moment I imagine opening my mouth to tell them, I can already see the look on their faces — that look where worry is tangled up with disappointment, a look I know too well, have known since I was small, like my father staring at a shattered glass, like my mother staring at that test paper with a 56 on it. I can't become a burden to them. I can't let this crush the project that's still moving forward. I can't let my child see me fall apart. But something on the other side is tearing at me — what if I wait too long, what if I miss the window, what if by then it's worse? That would be the real disaster, the thing that would truly let everyone down. I've always made a habit of imagining the worst outcome first and then tearing myself to pieces over it — but this time even self-attack is useless, because this isn't about how other people see me. This is my body. This is a real danger. The phone screen lit up again. I stared at it, and decided: I'll go to the follow-up appointment alone first, get clarity, and then figure out what to say.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (rooted in catastrophic imaginings about the health threat, and in the rehearsed memory of my family's disappointed faces); Shame (the act of concealment itself activates a sense of 'being defective' — being sick feels like an exposure); Loneliness (the habitual coping pattern of carrying things alone); Anxiety (extremely intense, manifesting as physical tension and a sick feeling in my stomach)
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I learned early on that laying my vulnerability in front of my family only ever produces their panic and disappointment, my instinct is to press this thing down first — to wait until I've figured it out myself, until I have some control over the situation, before saying anything — even if that means carrying the full weight of this unknown entirely on my own.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'not becoming a burden to others' matters more than 'letting others help me.' I cannot bring myself to infect my family with this panic before I have any definite answers — it would feel like a complete exposure of my deepest flaw.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to keep it to myself for now, go to the follow-up appointment alone to confirm the results, and only decide whether to tell my family once I have more information. I press the phone screen dark, quietly note the follow-up appointment time in my memo app, then walk out of the study and tell my family I'm a little tired tonight and am turning in early.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂时隐瞒，独自先去复查确认结果，待掌握更多信息后再决定是否告知家人。我把手机屏幕按灭，在备忘录里悄悄记下复查预约的时间，然后走出书房，对家人说今晚有点累，早点休息。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择告诉家人，但先说明只是初步异常、需要尽快复查。我会今晚就开口，也立刻预约复查，不再一个人扛着。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择隐瞒真实病情，独自承担风险并继续推进工作。我按灭手机屏幕，深吸一口气走出书房，挤出笑容对家人说：“医生说只是常规复查，没什么大碍。”

inner_consciousness.summary: That look in his eyes — it wasn't a normal kind of light, it was the kind that gets lit by something and just can't stop burning. I've known him for years, and I knew something was off. I flipped through the slides and it was all that kind of talk, those numbers, that whole "triple your money in three months" pitch — I've seen it too many times, I could see right through it. But what he said just now is still ringing in my ears: "Are you jealous of me?" That word landed like a blade, and it landed precisely. I want to say I'm trying to help him, but I already know how he'll see me if I keep going — he'll think I'm that kind of person, the kind who never sees an opportunity, who's always throwing cold water on things. That feeling from when I was a kid came rushing back — saying something true and being treated like the villain for it, people staring at you, everyone deciding you're the one who's wrong. I don't want to lose him. Friends you can talk with until sunrise — there are fewer and fewer of those. But if I say nothing, after the eight hundred thousand he'll throw in even more… I can feel something lodged in my throat, and my stomach is starting to twist. The second his gaze went cold, I almost pulled back. But I can't pull back. I'm afraid of losing him — but I'm even more afraid of watching him fall and having said nothing at all.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (of losing this friendship) tangled with Shame — his accusation of "jealousy" triggered that old wound I've carried since childhood, the one where telling the truth gets you cast as the bad guy; alongside that, there's a suppressed anger, because I'm clearly trying to help him and yet this is how it's being read. Emotional intensity is extremely high, nearly at a breaking point.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through so many moments of speaking the truth only to have it received as an attack, my instinct was to retreat — but this time, how much I care about him outweighs the fear of being misunderstood, just barely enough to keep me from going completely silent.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: To me, a real friend is someone who's still willing to pull you back when you're about to fall into a pit — even if you hate them for it in the moment. What I cannot accept is this: staying quiet out of fear of being resented, watching him destroy himself, and then pretending nothing happened.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to keep trying to talk him out of it, but instead of flat-out rejecting what he's saying, I use self-disclosure and emotional connection to try to get around his defenses. I set down the slides and say quietly: 'I'm not jealous of you. I'm just scared. Scared that three months from now you'll call me to say the money's gone — and that today I said nothing.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择继续劝阻，但以自我暴露和情感连接代替直接否定，试图绕开他的防御。我放下PPT，低声说：'我不嫉妒你，我只是怕。怕你三个月后打电话告诉我钱没了，而我今天什么都没说。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择继续劝阻，但尽量不踩他的自尊。 我先说“我不是觉得你傻，也不是嫉妒你”，再指出这像拉人头骗局，劝他先别再投也别再拉人。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择退缩并回避冲突，用自我贬低来平息对方的怒火。我僵硬地低下头，声音发颤地说：“没有……我只是自己太胆小了，不敢冒险而已，你别生气。”

inner_consciousness.summary: My signature is on that document — my eyes are locked onto the investigator's fingertips, and my heart feels like someone is squeezing it, thudding, thudding, thudding. I remember that day: my supervisor handed it over for me to look through, I just flipped through it, never read it carefully, and yet there it is, that signature, black ink on white paper, like a trap. Twenty-eight million. That number detonates inside my head, my stomach starts to cramp, sweat seeps out across my forehead. Their eyes are so calm, so certain — just like that aunt when I was little, pointing at me saying 'did you knock over the vase,' just like the teacher holding up my homework in front of the whole class, just like every single moment when I explained myself and no one believed me. They won't believe me. They've already made up their minds. That feeling of being pinned in place with nowhere to run rushes over me, and my fingers start to go cold. I want to explain — but what good would explaining do? I signed my name, I was there, that's the evidence. But if I start talking now, if I hand over my colleagues' names — they might not be innocent either, but how would I know? I don't know. I don't know anything. All I know is that right now I'm very likely the one being pushed out to take the fall, and I don't even have the right to be angry, because that signature is real. I need a lawyer. I can't just sit here and let people do whatever they want with me, can't knock myself flat on the ground first like I always do.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Extreme fear (the moment of accusation activates every childhood memory of being misunderstood and condemned — intensity is off the charts); Shame and confusion (the signature is real, undeniable, and my sense of being fundamentally flawed is amplified to its absolute limit); A faint instinct for self-preservation (right at the edge of my usual self-collapse, there's a tiny flicker of 'I can't just let it happen again this time').
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every single time I've been accused in my life, no one has ever believed my explanation — so I know instinctively that facing these three people alone, I will fall apart, I will end up taking all the blame onto myself. I need to find some kind of barrier before that happens, even if it's just the presence of a lawyer's voice standing beside me.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'I cannot be convicted again without any protection whatsoever' matters more than anything else; what I cannot accept is being forced to open my mouth — before the truth has even been sorted out — and say things that might hurt other people and hurt myself.

pairs.final_decision: Choose to defer full cooperation and request legal assistance before giving any formal response; do not provide any information about team members on the spot. Say aloud: 'I need my lawyer present before I can formally answer these questions — I'll be in touch with you within 24 hours. But what I can tell you right now is that I only looked over that document; I was never involved in any funding decisions.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂缓全面配合、要求获得法律帮助后再正式回应，不当场提供任何团队成员信息。开口说：'我需要在律师在场的情况下才能正式回答这些问题，24小时之内我会联系你们——但我现在可以告诉你，那份文件我只是过目，从未参与任何资金决策。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择先寻求法律帮助，只做有限配合，不立即提供团队信息。我会说我愿意配合，但先联系律师，之后按正式程序提交材料。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择全面妥协并过度配合。我颤抖着点头，语无伦次地开始解释自己只是被迫签字，并答应交出所有聊天记录。

inner_consciousness.summary: Those two lines of red text are seared into my eyes like a branding iron — "recommend follow-up as soon as possible to rule out the possibility of early pathological changes" — I keep flipping the paper over and back, as if reading it enough times might change what it means. The number-calling announcements echo down the corridor one after another, and each one feels like it's calling my name. My palms are soaked, the edges of the paper are damp. I don't know how long I've been sitting here, but the voice in my head has already started drifting toward the worst — not "it's probably nothing," but "what if it really is." The kid has a math worksheet tonight, he's still in Hangzhou, and I'm sitting here alone — that thought makes the weight in my chest even heavier. Then my phone lights up. It's a video call from him, his profile picture bouncing on the screen, the ringtone cutting through the corridor so sharply I want to cover my ears. I know the first thing he'll say when I pick up is "how did the check-up go" — he says it every year. But right now I'm clutching this piece of paper in my hands. What am I supposed to say? If I tell him, what will he think? Will he think I'm making a big deal out of nothing — or worse, will he think I'm a burden, someone who might fall apart at any moment? I can't let him see me like this: eyes stinging, hands trembling, looking like a person on the verge of collapse. But if I don't answer, he'll worry even more, or assume I'm hiding something. I don't know which is worse. The ringtone is still going, a nurse nearby glanced at me, and I feel like everyone in the entire corridor is staring.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (an intense, deep-seated panic around "being flawed / being abandoned," triggered by those two red lines on the report; intensity: very high); Shame (not wanting to expose my vulnerability or the fact that I might be sick in front of my partner, afraid of becoming a burden to someone else; intensity: high); Isolated, helpless anxiety (sitting alone in this corridor with my partner nowhere near me — the physical solitude amplifies everything I'm feeling inside; intensity: high)
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every single time I've ever shown my "not okay" side — growing up and beyond — it was met with disappointment or ridicule, my instinct tells me that saying something before I have a definitive answer will only make him think less of me first. If it turns out to be a false alarm, won't I just become "the person who overreacted"? And if something really is wrong, I have even less idea how to bring it up. So I'd rather hold it together for now, figure things out on my own first, and talk when I actually know something.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not letting anyone see me fall apart before I even have a clear answer. What I cannot bear is being dismissed — with a "you always do this" or a "you're overthinking it" — at my most vulnerable moment. That would be even harder to survive than carrying this alone.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to answer the video call, but I gloss over everything — I downplay it with "it's fine, a couple of indicators came back slightly elevated, the doctor said to just keep an eye on it" — and I don't breathe a word about the line that says "recommend follow-up as soon as possible to rule out the possibility of early pathological changes." I pick up, manage to arrange my face into something that looks reasonably calm, and say: "Just got out — overall it's fine, a couple of minor indicators were a little high, the doctor said it's probably just been too hectic lately, told me to watch my diet a bit. Nothing serious."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择接起视频通话，但含糊带过，用"还好，有两个指标稍微偏高，医生说观察一下"轻描淡写地带过，不提"建议尽快复查，排除早期病变可能"那行字。我接起来，挤出一个还算平稳的表情说："刚出来，总体还好，有两个小指标偏高，医生说可能是最近太累了，让我注意一下饮食，没什么大事。"
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择接起视频但先含糊带过，暂时瞒住异常结果。我挤出笑说报告刚拿到、还在医院，先回头再细说。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避沟通，独自承担恐慌并隐瞒真相。我按下了红色的挂断键，手抖着发去一条文字：“医院太吵听不清，一切正常，你安心出差。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment he slid that proposal across to me, my hands went cold. That instant of silence still rings in my ears — everyone's eyes landing on me, and I know that feeling, I've known it for far too long, ever since that afternoon the teacher rapped the blackboard with a piece of chalk and stared me down. Now it's just the two of us, and he says "this isn't up for discussion," and I can hear my own heartbeat thundering against my eardrums. I want to push back, I want to say how much of this process I built word by word with my own hands — but something is blocking my throat. He said "old habits holding things back" — which means I'm the one holding things back. I know this feeling. It will crush me, so I have to crush myself first, get there before he does. If I confess first, apologize first, take every charge onto myself, then he can't dig any deeper. I hear a voice somewhere inside me saying: you were never good enough, you know that, he's just saying out loud what's already true. But somewhere else there's a fire burning, burning in a way that's almost unbearable — this isn't fair, this really isn't fair. Only I can't say that right now. I just can't say it.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Humiliation (rooted in public rejection, triggering a decades-long accumulation of memories of being criticized and ridiculed); Fear (of further rejection, of being sidelined, of abandonment — that old wound surging back violently in this closed room); Anger (buried at the very bottom, pinned down by shame and fear, barely able to surface); Intensity extremely high, with clear physical responses — cold hands, racing heart, stomach clenched tight.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every single time I was criticized in front of others, from childhood on, it ended in deeper shame, I have an instinctive belief: knocking myself down first is slightly safer than waiting for someone else to do it. So right now I can't help but reach for compliance and self-deprecation to cut off a larger wound — even if that means surrendering something I built with my own hands.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: I cannot accept that years of effort get wiped out entirely by a single phrase like "efficiency leaves much to be desired" — all those years of work aren't just work to me, they're the only way I've ever proven that my existence has worth. But rather than be shattered on the spot, rather than have my vulnerability completely exposed, I'd rather bow my head first — even though the act of bowing my head already makes me feel like a part of me is dying.

pairs.final_decision: I choose surface-level compliance, preemptively putting myself down, using cooperation to mask the collapse happening inside — while tentatively floating one small, cushioning request to hold onto the last shred of dignity. I glanced down at the proposal, keeping my voice carefully flat: "Alright, I'll oversee the transition period. A few of the handoff cycles are on the longer side — I'll put together a timeline for you to sign off on, that way we can keep the risk down."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择表面服从、先发制人地自我贬低，用配合掩盖内心的崩溃，同时试探性地提出一个微小的缓冲请求以保留最后一点尊严。我低头看了一眼那份方案，声音压得很平："好，我来盯过渡期。有几个环节交接周期比较长，我列个时间表给你确认，这样风险能小一些。"
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择压住正面冲突，表面接下执行，同时当场提出风险和缓冲要求。我会说我配合推进，但过渡节点、人员培训和责任边界今天要先写清楚。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择全盘退让、表面服从以逃避冲突。我低下头避开他的视线，双手攥紧，声音发紧地回道：“好的，我明白了，我会按新流程盯好。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment the teacher's hand landed on my shoulder, my heart seemed to skip a beat. Forty-some pairs of eyes — I could feel them, like thin threads winding around me, tighter and tighter. I was only muttering under my breath, only meant for my seatmate to hear — why did it have to be overheard? Something in my head exploded all at once, and those familiar images came flooding back — the feeling of standing at the podium with my mind gone blank, the sound of a rope catching my foot, and that moment when I sang the wrong note and the teacher stopped playing. I know this feeling. I know it all too well. The teacher said "remarkable," but what I heard was: everyone is staring at you, waiting for you to fall apart. Someone in the back row said "what's so remarkable about that" — that line stung like a needle, but even more terrifying were the envious looks — they were expecting something from me, and what if I couldn't deliver? A speech competition. Standing in front of even more people. A bigger stage, more eyes, more places where things could go wrong. My palms are already soaked. I want to stand up, but my legs won't cooperate. I want to speak, but there's something lodged in my throat. I don't want to disappoint the teacher — but I'm even more afraid of falling apart in front of everyone. I've been through that too many times. I know all too well what every night feels like afterward.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (intense): being forced into the spotlight has triggered memories of countless public humiliations; Shame (latent): being praised feels dangerous — the higher the expectations, the harder the fall; Anxiety (pervasive): a mental rehearsal of the speech competition has already begun, and every version ends in the worst possible outcome.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every time someone notices me, it could be the beginning of being laughed at — my instinct tells me this spotlight isn't a gift, it's a trap. The more eager the teacher is, the more frightened I become — because I know I'll end up disappointing everyone, and that disappointment will hurt far worse than never having been expected anything of at all.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not falling apart in front of people — not becoming someone else's punchline. What I cannot bear is being lifted up high and then shattered in public — that is a hundred times more terrifying than never having been seen at all.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to respond in a vague, retreating way — neither outright refusing the teacher nor actually standing up to perform — trying to shrink myself back into the crowd. I keep my head down and say in a very small voice: "Teacher… what I said just now wasn't very clear, it probably wasn't that good…" and give a small shake of my head, without standing up.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择用含糊、退缩的方式回应，既不直接拒绝老师也不真正站起来展示，试图把自己缩回人群里。我低着头，声音很小地说："老师……我刚才说得不清楚，可能没那么好……"同时轻轻摇了摇头，没有站起来。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择勉强回应，但主动缩小自己并回避比赛提议。我慢吞吞站起来小声讲几句，又赶紧说我只是随便想的，比赛我可能不行。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择极力回避当众发言，通过自我贬低来拒绝提议。我把头埋低，双手死死抠着桌沿，声音发颤地说：“老师，我刚才乱说的，我讲不清楚，也去不了比赛。”

inner_consciousness.summary: My chopsticks froze mid-air, my eyes still caught on that corner. She was sitting alone, and that gesture — sleeve pressed to her eyes — I know that gesture, I know it too well. It felt like someone had shoved something down my throat, because I know that taste: curled up in a corner, surrounded by noise, not a single word of it meant for you. My friends were already calling my name — flash cards, the playground, their voices so bright and full — but my mind kept going back to that hunched figure with her head down. I thought, if I walk over there, would it be weird? Would she think I'm pitying her? What if I say the wrong thing and make it worse? I've never known how to talk to people — what if I mess it up completely… But if I don't go, that gesture is going to keep spinning in my head, and it'll still be spinning tonight when I can't sleep. I don't want to become the kind of person who sees something and pretends they didn't — that feels even worse. My friends are rushing me, and I know that if I go find that new kid, they might think I'm strange. But I can't pretend I didn't just see what I saw. I pick up my tray and stand, legs a little unsteady, heart beating fast — and I walk toward that corner anyway.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Empathic pain (intense): the new kid's gesture of wiping her eyes with her sleeve directly triggered my own memories of being isolated and forgotten, producing a strong physical resonance; Conflicted anxiety (moderate): worried that walking over would mean saying the wrong thing, seeming weird, being judged by my friends — preemptive self-doubt flaring up; but ultimately driven by an inner force of 'I cannot pretend I didn't see this.'
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know all too well what it feels like to sit alone in a corner while everyone around you is loud and lively and not a single word is meant for you — I can't pretend that hunched figure doesn't exist. Even if I stumble over my words when I get there. Even if my friends think I'm strange.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'seeing it and walking away' is something I simply cannot accept — because that would mean handing someone else the exact loneliness I once feared most. Being seen as weird by my friends, or saying something clumsy, is far easier to live with than pretending I never saw a thing.

pairs.final_decision: I leave my friends' table, tray in hand, and walk alone toward the new girl in the corner. I set my tray down beside her and take a seat, saying quietly: "Is it okay if I sit here? My name is… we're actually in the same class today."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择离开朋友们的桌子，独自端着餐盘走向角落里的新同学。我把餐盘放在她旁边坐下，小声说："这里可以坐吗？我叫……我们今天来的是同一个班。"
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择先去找角落里的新同学，不跟朋友们立刻去操场。我端着餐盘走过去，小声问她：“我可以坐这儿吗？”
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择顺从群体，逃避内心的愧疚。我胡乱扒了两口饭，大声附和着朋友的闪卡真厉害，端起餐盘头也不回地跟着他们跑向操场。

inner_consciousness.summary: The paper is burning in my hands, trembling, my heart pounding like it's about to explode. My best friend's voice drills into my ears, every word like another nudge toward the edge of a cliff. "Let's copy" "no one will know" — those words detonate in my head, and instantly I see the worst: the teacher finds out, the whole class staring at me, Mom's look of disappointment, everyone saying I'm a cheater. My stomach starts to cramp, the sweat on my palms soaking right through the paper. I want to say no, but my throat feels blocked — I'm scared she'll think I'm acting holier-than-thou, scared she'll stop talking to me, scared of being left out all over again. But if I use this paper, I really will become that person with "something wrong" with her, the child Mom always calls "such a disappointment." Two voices are tearing me apart: one says
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear and shame intertwined, with a core of double Panic — fear of "being abandoned" and fear of "being exposed as defective." The fear is rooted in childhood trauma memories of being blamed and ridiculed over and over; the Shame comes from a deep-seated belief that I am "not good enough." The intensity is overwhelming, nearly paralyzing rational thought.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through so many moments of "make a mistake and get written off," I instinctively see this piece of paper as a trap — use it and get caught, don't use it and get rejected by my friend. I can't help but reach for the option that "at least won't let everyone see how worthless I really am," even if that means walking away from the chance right in front of me. Striking myself down first is always safer than letting someone else do it.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not being completely written off, not being abandoned by everyone — even if that means going on struggling just to scrape by. What I cannot bear is that feeling of "being exposed in front of everyone, being proven to be nothing but a fraud" — because that would confirm the thing I've always been terrified of: that I truly don't deserve to be loved.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to refuse my friend and either hand the paper to the teacher or put it back where it came from. I whisper to her, "We can't use this — we'll get caught," and then, while she's not paying attention, I slip the paper back into the crack in the desk or hand it to the teacher, pretending I just happened to find it by accident.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择拒绝朋友并交给老师或放回原处。我小声对她说"这个不能用,我们会被发现的",然后趁她不注意把纸塞回桌缝或交给老师,假装是无意中捡到的。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择不抄也不留下，并阻止朋友一起作弊。我把纸折好收起来，说我不想抄，然后去交给老师。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择拒绝作弊并逃避风险。我猛地把纸塞回桌洞，声音发抖地说：‘不行！被发现就完了！’拉着她快步逃离教室。

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment the teacher stepped out, I watched the light fall across my child's profile through the glass, and something in my chest came loose — that focus, that glow, I felt like I'd seen it somewhere before, and yet like I'd never truly had it myself. Then the teacher spoke: "a rare gift," "the window of opportunity is short" — every word landed so clearly that my heartbeat started to stagger. I felt my palms go damp. Not from pride. From fear. Fear of what? I couldn't say. Fear of making the wrong choice and ruining them? Or fear that I have no right to make this decision for them at all? I couldn't bring myself to look too long at those photos on the hallway wall — all those successful kids, their parents must have been so much more certain, so much more sure of themselves. And me — I've tiptoed through every step of my own life, and a single critical word can make me feel like a complete failure. What gives me the right to choose for my child? But if I don't support them, and this window closes, will they hate me for it someday? These two fears are like two hands grabbing me at once, pulling in opposite directions. I want to say "I need time to think," but I also know — give me all the time in the world, and I'll only spiral further, until no matter what I choose, it feels like my fault.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (core): all those nights of being told I was worthless by my parents, every moment of freezing on stage and being laughed at — they all pile up and crash over me at once right now. This decision is too heavy; I'm terrified I'll ruin it. Shame (underlying): a feeling that I have no right to be a 'good' parent, no ability to judge what is actually good for my child. Anxiety (diffuse): I cannot bear any uncertain future; my mind has already started playing out the worst possible ending. A flicker of longing (faint): looking at my child's glowing face, a small voice says — maybe this time, things could be different.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I was told from childhood that nothing I did was ever good enough, my instinct is that I will get any major decision wrong; and yet, precisely because I know what it feels like to be held back, I can't help wanting to protect my child from missing that light.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not letting my child lose something because of my own cowardice — but what I cannot live with is the possibility that if I do give my support and it turns out to be too much for them, then I will have pushed them with my own hands into the exact kind of failure and shame I was always most afraid of myself.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to avoid taking an immediate stance, using "needing to have a serious talk with my child" to buy myself some breathing room, while shifting the decision to my child themselves in order to ease my own fear. I say to the teacher: "Thank you so much — they really have worked so hard… I need to sit down and talk it through with them first, hear what they think, and make this decision together."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂时回避立即表态，用"需要和孩子认真谈谈"来争取喘息空间，同时把决定权转移给孩子本身以缓解自己的恐惧。我对老师说："谢谢您，TA真的很努力……我需要先好好跟孩子聊聊，听听TA自己怎么想，这个决定我们一起来做。"
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择支持申请，但不替孩子擅自决定命运。我先对老师说想进一步了解安排，今晚认真和孩子、家人一起谈。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避高风险路线，用过度保护的姿态拒绝。我没有接那份简章，避开老师的目光干涩地说：“孩子还太小，专业路线压力太大了，我们还是想走安稳的普通路。”

inner_consciousness.summary: He came in holding that glass of milk, eyes bright, talking about the awards ceremony, the speech, the math competition — every single word felt like a needle jabbing into my chest. My hands were resting on my lap, my nails quietly digging into my palms. That note was still in his backpack, and I'd read those few lines more times than I could count — all I could hear in my head was: 'The answers passed along pretty smoothly, didn't they.' He stood there in front of me saying 'hard work pays off,' and those words coming out of his mouth felt like a knife. I thought about that sweet-and-sour pork ribs I'd made that day — I'd gone out of my way to buy the ingredients, even told him how proud I was of his progress — and I'd been fooled. Something was rising in my throat, and I didn't know if it was anger or something else entirely. I was afraid that if I opened my mouth I'd fall apart. I was afraid that if I said it out loud he'd cry. I was afraid I wouldn't be able to control my own voice. But to just let him go up on that stage, give a speech, compete — that certificate would be a lie, that 96 would be a lie, and if I said nothing now, the lie would only grow bigger and bigger, and he'd walk up onto that stage and tell the whole school that 'hard work pays off.' I can't let him stand up there like that. I have to say something — but not now, not like this, not when I'm on the verge of tears. I need to steady myself first.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Shock and heartbreak (betrayed by someone so close, the memory of celebrating instantly curdled); intense inner conflict (wanting to confront him immediately vs. fearing I'll lose control and fall apart); an undercurrent of shame (feeling like a fool for being deceived and celebrating so naively — like all those moments in childhood when I was misunderstood and had no way to defend myself). The intensity is overwhelming, barely containable.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many moments where the truth was buried, where lies snowballed, where I was left completely alone with no one to turn to — I know instinctively that this lie cannot go on. But I also know that if I speak up in the state I'm in right now, it won't be help; it'll just be a breakdown.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: I can't bear the thought of him standing on that stage, using a fake grade to say 'hard work pays off' — that feels more fundamentally wrong to me than any criticism ever could. But I also can't face him in a state of collapse; that would only mean dumping my own fear and shame onto him.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hold it in for now, steady my emotions, and wait until he's gone before finding the right moment to talk to him alone. I took the milk from him, glanced down at it for a moment, and said quietly: 'Let's talk about the speech later — go wash up first. I have something I want to say to you.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂时隐忍，稳住情绪，等孩子离开后再找合适时机单独谈。我接过牛奶，低头看了一眼，轻声说：'发言稿的事等一下再聊，你先去洗漱，我有话想和你说。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择当面点破，但不直接羞辱他。我要先放下牛奶，看着他说：先别谈发言稿了，我在你书包里看到一张纸条，你现在跟我说实话。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择暂时回避正面冲突，以压抑和自我折磨的方式逃离当下。我僵硬地推开那杯牛奶，声音发抖地说：“我胃有点疼，发言稿的事明天再说吧。”然后迅速转过身死死盯着桌面。

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment he walked in, I knew something was off — that hushed, conspiratorial tone, that 'the rest of us talked it over' opener. I spent three months on this. How many nights did I sit there reconciling accounts, cross-checking invoices, while my wife stood there with the color drained from her face — and now he's telling me everyone agrees I found something real, but the letter of complaint can only carry one name. Mine. 'You're young, you can take the heat.' Those words landed in my chest like a stone. My mind went completely sideways — the property manager's assistant with that easy smile, those words 'we can work something out,' the image of construction debris piled up outside the neighbor's door, and my wife's face just now, white as a sheet… Did they decide from the very beginning to make me the target? They 'support' me in private, but the moment it counts, not one of them steps forward — how is that any different from all those 'I didn't see anything' moments when I was a kid? Am I once again that person standing alone in the schoolyard, holding a perfectly intact toy, with no one willing to say a single word on my behalf? But if I back down now, those two million yuan just rot there, everyone goes on pretending nothing happened, the property management keeps stealing, and I — I can't back down. Not because I'm not afraid. It's because if I back down, I lose even myself.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anger (a sense of being used) and fear (isolated, with no one to rely on) are tangled together; underneath it all is a familiar, bone-deep sting of abandonment — one that was carved into me in childhood. These neighbors who 'support' me in private but refuse to stand up publicly have triggered a lifetime of memories of 'no one is ever truly there for you.' At the same time, a stubborn, almost desperate resolve is pushing back against every impulse to retreat.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I have lived through too many moments of bearing things alone, of being pointed at and told 'it's you' — I know instinctively: if I back down this time too, I will spend the rest of my life hating myself for this moment of weakness. That is harder to bear than any retaliation from the property management.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'someone being willing to stand up for the truth' matters more than 'staying safe.' What I cannot accept is knowing something is wrong and pretending not to see it just to avoid the trouble — that kind of silence isn't neutrality to me. It's complicity.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to file the complaint in my own name, as an individual — but I make my disappointment and my boundaries clear to the neighbors who were 'full of advice but unwilling to sign.' I say to the retired teacher: 'I'll go file the report, and I'll put my name on it — but I need you all to promise me this: if the housing authority comes to verify the facts, you cannot deny that you knew. I'm not asking you to be out in front. But I can't stand there alone and lie by saying I discovered all of this by myself.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择以个人名义实名举报，但对邻居的"只出主意不联名"表明失望与边界。对退休教师说：'我会去举报，名字写我的，但我需要你们答应我，如果住建局来核实，你们不能否认知情——我不要求你们冲在前面，但我不能一个人撒谎说这是我一个人发现的。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择实名继续举报，但不再替邻居的退缩粉饰。 我会说我去举报，也请你们别再叫支持却只让我一个人扛。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择退缩放弃，拒绝单独实名举报。我白着脸对邻居说：“算了吧，我也不想管了，就当那两个月我白忙活了。”然后关上门，发着抖删掉了文档。

inner_consciousness.summary: The note unfolded, the handwriting round and bubbly, a little sun doodled in the corner. My heart did give a thud — I'll admit that — but just the one, and then my mind was already moving: what does this person want to say to me? A confession? Something else entirely? Not enough information; drawing conclusions right now would be pointless. My best friend leaned over trying to get a look, and my fingers tightened ever so slightly around the note. Not because I panicked — because I needed to work something out first: I know she likes the same person. She told me herself. I never said anything back then, but I knew perfectly well that we both cared about the same person. If she sees this note, things get complicated — not that I'm afraid of anything, it's just that there's a variable in here I can't process quickly. She's my best friend, and that fact matters more than the little sun drawn on this piece of paper. Should I go after school? Yes. What do I gain by not going? Nothing. I only find out what this person actually wants to say if I show up — only then can I figure out what to do next. But right now, this note — she can't see it.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Mostly calm, with a faint undercurrent of curiosity and a thread of alertness. The thud in my chest was a real, physical reaction, but it never tipped into anxiety; there's a cool, preemptive wariness about the possibility of my friend getting hurt — not guilt, just a judgment that came out of processing the information.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I'm used to getting the facts straight before I make any decisions, I won't let the situation get complicated when I don't have enough information yet; my friend told me how she feels, and that's a known variable — I can't pretend it doesn't exist.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: If something can be dealt with, deal with it; if it can't, set it aside for now — but don't actively create unnecessary trouble. My friend's feelings are a real, existing fact, not background noise I'm allowed to tune out.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hide the contents of the note so my friend can't see it, and decide to go to the meeting alone after school. I flip the note face-down and slide it under my textbook in one casual motion, then tell my friend offhandedly: 'It's nothing — just a classmate asking to borrow my notes.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择遮住纸条内容不让朋友看到，同时决定放学后独自赴约。把纸条随手翻面压在课本下，对朋友轻描淡写地说：'没什么，一个同学问我借笔记的。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择先不让朋友看纸条，并且放学后赴约。 我把纸条一合说“没什么，课后再说”，放学后去大榕树下见ta。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择公开信息并赴约查明事实。我把纸条摊开放在桌上让好友看，并平静地说：'ta约我放学后见面，我会去看看ta具体想说什么。'

inner_consciousness.summary: She said "don't overthink it," and then turned away. I'm sitting here, phone still in my hand, the name of that little group chat still on the screen. Created twenty days ago. I'm not in it. I already saw what was said — "acts like she's so important, so annoying." Okay. So that's how it is. Let me line up the facts: they made a group without me, she said those things in it, at the cafeteria today they all went quiet together, and right now she's over there laughing with the people in the back row. These are all facts. What can I do right now? Confront her? She'll deny it, or cry, or more people will end up on her side. Pretend I don't know? I could — but I already know, and I can't keep that up for long. I don't feel particularly sad, just… strange, somehow. I thought we were friends. But I guess that's just another piece of information. The most useless things I could do right now are cry, or storm over and demand answers. I need to figure out what outcome I actually want first, then decide what to do. For now, I stay still.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Predominantly calm, with a mild undercurrent of confusion and detachment. The confusion stems from the gap between how I understood the friendship and what the facts are telling me; the detachment is a long-standing habit of filtering out the emotional noise of other people. Overall intensity is low — no marked spikes of pain or anger.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've always believed that emotions don't solve anything, my first instinct when faced with these confirmed facts isn't to explode — it's to sort through the information clearly before deciding what action actually means something.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, figuring out "what actually happened" matters more than "how I feel right now." I can't bring myself to react impulsively when the picture is still incomplete — that would only make things messier.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hold back for now — no confrontation, not here, not yet. I'll wait until I've calmly worked out what outcome I actually want before deciding on a next step. I turn off my phone screen, look up, give her one expressionless glance, then drop my eyes and open my textbook.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂时按兵不动，不当场质问，先冷静确认自己想要什么结果再决定下一步。我把手机屏幕关掉，抬起头，面无表情地看了她一眼，然后低头翻开课本。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择平静摊开，不当场失控，也不再假装。 我低声对她说我看见小群和截图了，问她要不要现在说清楚；说完我就不再主动靠近她。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择单方面切断关系，无视对方的试探与挑衅。我平静地收回视线，从书包里拿出下节课的课本翻开，将她们的笑声彻底当作背景噪音。

inner_consciousness.summary: I saw it. Sleeve rolled up to the elbow, those marks — different depths, some of them still fresh. My eyes stayed on them for two seconds, then moved to her face — relief and fear at the same time, like two colors bleeding into each other. She was waiting for me to say something. Her hand gripped my wrist, knuckles white. I felt the pressure, but my heartbeat didn't speed up. I was thinking: these are real injuries, not just once — multiple times, some already scabbed over, some still new. This isn't a small thing. She said she 'can't control it,' said 'cutting once makes it loosen a little' — I filed that line away. That's a signal, not ordinary emotional venting. Then she said I couldn't tell anyone, or she'd cut me off completely. I listened to that condition, and something in me came to a very clear conclusion: what she needs right now isn't just me — she needs someone who can actually help her. If I promise to keep this secret, does that make her safe? No. If I refuse, will she fall apart? Not necessarily. What she needs most right now is someone who can actually step in, not a teenager carrying this alone. I care about the friendship, but her life matters more. This isn't a betrayal — it's the most real thing I can do for her. I can't pretend to agree and then go tell someone behind her back. That would only make her trust no one even less. I have to be straight with her.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm on the surface, with genuine concern underneath. The calm comes from years of instinct — 'gather information first, then act.' The concern comes from some quiet, wordless part of me that stirred when I saw those marks. No panic, no impulse to be pulled along by her tears or her ultimatum.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've seen too many times how 'wait and see' turns into something irreversible — so I know instinctively that friendship can't be used as a bargaining chip here. The kind of help she needs right now is beyond what I alone can give, and promising secrecy is the same as locking her inside this thing by herself.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, actually helping her matters more than preserving the trust she has in me right now. What I can't accept is this: trading an 'I promise' for a surface-level calm, then watching things get worse while I did nothing.

pairs.final_decision: I won't agree to unconditional secrecy — but I won't flatly refuse her either, and I won't turn around and go straight to a teacher. Instead, I choose to be honest with her first about where I stand, and try to bring her along with me. I say to her: 'I can't promise you complete secrecy — not because I don't care about you, but because I do care, and that's exactly why I can't. But I won't go behind your back. Let's figure out together how to handle this, okay?'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 不答应无条件保密，但也不直接拒绝她、不立刻转身去找老师，而是选择先坦诚说明自己的立场，争取她的理解和参与。我对她说：'我没办法答应你完全保密，不是因为我不在乎你，是因为我在乎你才没办法答应——但我不会瞒着你去说，我们一起想怎么处理，好不好？'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我不答应彻底保密，但先站在他这边并马上求助。我要对他说我不会随便传开，可这件事我得陪你一起找能帮忙的大人，现在先跟我去校医室。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择策略性妥协以收集信息，保留后续求助大人的选项。我反握住ta的手腕，平静地看着ta说：“我答应你现在不说，但你得告诉我到底发生了什么事。”

inner_consciousness.summary: A voice message from a former colleague — I listened to the whole thing. His reasoning is actually pretty sound: HR posting AI roles at 20% lower pay is a signal, not a panic move, it's data. I ran through it in my head: the rumor about 30% layoffs, savings that could last a year, a mortgage on the house — these are all known quantities. The unknowns are: the probability of successfully breaking into a new field, and the timeline for when existing roles disappear. I don't have enough information on either variable right now. My colleague asked if I wanted to make a move together — I'm not opposed, but 'together' isn't a basis for a decision. The manager wants to meet tomorrow, which is actually an opportunity to gather information — where they want to take things, what the company's actual plans are for traditional roles, all of that is more useful than rumors from outside. What I can say for certain right now: sitting and waiting without any preparation is a genuinely real risk; but rushing into a transition isn't the answer either — I need to first get clear on the specific path and the odds of success. Tonight's task isn't to make a final decision; it's to organize what I already know and draw up the questions I want to ask before tomorrow's conversation. Anxiety won't solve this, but a checklist will.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm (focused): no anxiety triggered by the layoff rumors or my colleague's sense of urgency — that's just my baseline; mild decision-driven alertness: not pressure exactly, more a recognition that I don't have enough information yet and need to actively fill in the gaps. Emotional intensity is low, but my thinking is highly active.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've dealt with too many things that looked urgent over the years, and panicking never once helped — it was always staying calm and gathering information that actually solved the problem; so when faced with this industry upheaval, my instinct is to break it down as a decision problem with incomplete information, not a crisis I need to flee from immediately.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is getting the facts straight before acting. What I can't accept is being pushed into a decision by emotions or someone else's pace when I don't have enough information.

pairs.final_decision: I'm choosing to actively gather information and hold off on any final decision, while preparing for tomorrow's conversation. Two things to do tonight: organize the industry data and specific transition-path information I currently have, and draw up a few key questions for the manager — what the company's actual plans are for existing roles, whether internal transfers into AI-related positions are a possibility, and what their read is on the staffing structure over the next year.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择主动收集信息、暂缓最终决策，同时为明天谈话做准备。今晚列出两件事：整理目前掌握的行业数据和转型路径的具体信息，以及明天想问主管的几个关键问题——公司对现有岗位的实际规划、AI相关岗位的内部转岗可能性，以及他们对未来一年人员结构的判断。

inner_consciousness.summary: The second I pushed open the door, I saw the hard drive held up in the air — the connector already bent out of shape, fragments scattered across the floor. That clench in my stomach was real — not panic, but a rapid damage assessment. Six months, every night until two or three in the morning, no backups. Okay. That fact is already set. Nothing changes it. My uncle slapped the money down on the coffee table and said, 'That's more than enough to buy a few new ones.' I stared at that stack of bills. He doesn't know that what was in there isn't something money can replace — he thinks it's just a drive that stores files. He doesn't have any malicious intent; he simply doesn't know. My cousin stuck out his tongue, still clutching a screw in his hand. Mom sent a voice message telling me not to make the family look petty. I took all of this in — my uncle's frame of reference, my mom's position, my cousin's age, the current likelihood of data recovery. Whether or not to take the money is a separate question; what I need to confirm right now is: is there any chance the data on that drive can still be recovered? The casing is cracked, the connector is bent, there are fragments on the circuit board. Not completely hopeless. That's the only piece of information that matters right now. Emotions aren't going to help me here — losing my temper won't bring the data back. First, get the drive back in my hands, then find a professional to assess it. I can't be bothered correcting my uncle's logic, but I need that drive to stay with me.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm, with an undercurrent of cold, hard loss. That clench in my stomach is a genuine sense of loss, but it hasn't spiraled into anger or anxiety. My uncle's breezy, dismissive attitude triggered a faint sense of detachment — not offense, but the realization that he's simply operating on a completely different level of information. Overall emotional intensity is low; action orientation is clear.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been through too many situations where emotions solved absolutely nothing practical, my instinct is to put all my focus on the one question that actually has value — 'can this drive still be saved?' — rather than on whether my uncle's attitude deserves to be corrected.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me right now is whether there's still any chance that data can be recovered — that's the real core of the loss; everything else is noise. What I can't accept is wasting time on an emotional confrontation the other person won't hear anyway, and then missing the best window to do something about the drive.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to set aside any emotional confrontation and prioritize dealing with the actual loss — the drive itself. I politely but directly push the money back and ask for the drive. I say to my uncle: 'No need for the money — there are some work files on the drive, I need to take it and see if anything can be recovered. Please, make yourself comfortable.' I casually push the stack of bills back onto the coffee table, pick up the drive and the screw, and head to another room to look up data recovery services.

inner_consciousness.summary: When my phone lit up, I listened to the whole voice message first, then glanced again at the two acceptance letters on the desk. The hoarseness in her voice and the pauses told me this wasn't something she said offhand — but emotional weight doesn't mean I should let the conclusion follow right behind it. I've seen too many people say everything there is to say at the peak of their feelings, only to wake up the next day still having to go back and deal with the fallout. The long distance is wearing us down — no point pretending otherwise; but the path to my own school is also something I worked a long time to get here, not something I can swap out on a whim. I don't really believe that moving to the same city will automatically fix the exhaustion in this relationship, and I don't want the pressure of tonight to make me hastily reroute the next several years of my life. Let me separate the facts first: I want this relationship, and I also want the training path that fits me best. If the two can't coexist, then I'd rather be honest than dress up accommodation as commitment.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm, heavy, clear-headed. The calm comes from my habit of looking at the facts before chasing the feelings; the heaviness comes from the fact that she's clearly been holding on for a while — I can hear it, this isn't just her being needy; the clear-headedness comes from my long-held belief that major decisions can't be driven by a single night's breakdown. Intensity: moderate to high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've seen intense emotions push things in the wrong direction too many times, my instinct is to separate 'she's really hurting right now' from 'should I actually change my application' and look at them apart. For me, a choice that can hold up over time can't just be about stopping the pain immediately.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters more to me is choosing a path I've genuinely confirmed is right and can see through to the end, then facing the relationship with honesty — rather than trading away something real for a moment of relief. I can't bring myself to give an answer that looks deeply devoted but is actually built on shaky ground and more likely to backfire later.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to accept my own school's offer and not change course just to hold the relationship together. I write back to her: 'I've thought about it seriously — I'm staying at my school. If you're willing, let's talk through what the future looks like for us; if you're not, I'll accept that too.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择今晚先打电话沟通，不给出最终选校答案，而是把自己的判断过程和真实顾虑告诉对方，同时明确表示会在截止日期前给出决定。我现在打给你，不是因为我有答案了，而是因为你说的那些我都听进去了，我想先搞清楚一件事——你现在最需要的，是我在那座城市，还是你需要知道我有没有把你当回事？

decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择接受本校的录取通知，不被情绪绑架。我回复她：‘我决定留在本校。这个深造机会对我不可替代，如果异地让你实在无法承受，我理解并尊重你的任何决定。’

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment he flashed that smile, I knew he was tacking on extra demands last-minute. I ran through the information in what he said: present the report early on Monday, my materials are sixty percent done, the weekend is genuinely enough time to finish — but there's tomorrow morning's train ticket, Dad's follow-up appointment, and Mom who can't manage the hospital alone. Those are commitments I've already made. In situations like this I always look first at what truly can't be moved, and a medical follow-up isn't something you reschedule the way you'd revise a draft — family isn't something you trade away for a performance commendation. I don't feel panicked, just the need to lay out the boundaries and the workable options clearly. If I agree to everything, I break my word to my family; if I flatly refuse, the project is left with a gap. Rather than letting his words push me along, I'd rather put the facts on the table: I can hand off everything I have tonight, keep revising on the road and after I get home, and do a remote review Sunday evening — but showing up at the office all day Saturday and Sunday is not something I'm agreeing to.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm, earnest, mildly conflicted. The calm comes from my habit of looking at facts before looking at optics; the earnestness is because the project genuinely matters; the conflict comes from Dad's follow-up appointment and the promise I've already made to go home — that's not something I can casually walk back, moderate in intensity.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've seen too many situations where urgency changes nothing, my instinct is to first sort out what's an unavoidable reality and what isn't. Dad's appointment and the project crunch are both real — rather than being carried along by promises and pressure, I'd rather just lay out a plan that can actually be executed.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters more to me is telling the truth about the situation and then taking on the part I genuinely can. Trading a sick family member and a promise I've already made for a line on a performance review — that's not a trade I'm willing to make.

pairs.final_decision: I won't agree to being in the office both days of the weekend, but I'll propose a compromise and stay responsible for the core content. I'll say: I have to go back home to be with my father for his follow-up appointment, but I'll hand everything off tonight with a clear checklist, revise remotely over the weekend, and do a final online review of the draft Sunday evening.

inner_consciousness.summary: Alright, let me read this situation clearly. Three people, a baseball bat, the deposit already gone — that's the fait accompli he wants me to accept. He says he knows everyone at the local precinct; maybe that's true, maybe it's just a scare tactic — I have no way to verify it right now. But one thing is clear: given the time, the place, and the balance of power, standing my ground head-on serves absolutely no purpose. I'm not afraid, but I'm not stupid either. There are a few holes in his threats — the contract says 'high-wattage appliances'; what's the wattage on my space heater? Does it actually exceed the limit? That's something I can look up. He says the deposit is gone and that's that — but that's his word, not the law. What I need right now isn't to fight for my pride; it's to get out of here, keep myself safe, and then gather every piece of usable information I can. My clothes are scattered on the floor — first, get the essentials: ID, phone, wallet. Find somewhere to stay tonight, then tomorrow look into the contract terms, check with the consumer protection office or legal aid. He thinks I'll panic, or beg — but right now all I need to do is walk out of this hallway.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Mostly calm, with the faintest trace of irritation. The threat itself hasn't stirred any fear — it just makes me feel like this man is wasting my time. The baseball bat is information, not a source of dread — it tells me this is not the moment for confrontation, and nothing more.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've dealt with countless situations throughout my life where 'nothing can change right now, but it can be fixed later,' I instinctively split what's in front of me into two parts: my physical safety tonight, and recovering the deposit afterward — handle the first one now, let the second one wait.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: If a problem can be solved, solve it; if it can't be solved on the spot, fall back and build your case first. What I cannot accept is letting a moment of emotional impulse get me hurt in a standoff where the power is completely lopsided — that would be the real loss.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to comply for now and leave without arguing over the deposit on the spot, but I quietly note every key detail for pursuing my rights afterward. I bend down to pick up my ID and phone from the floor, and say calmly: 'Fine, I'll leave tonight. But the matter of the contract — we'll settle that later.' Then I pick up my luggage and walk out.

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment my phone screen lit up, I saw that photo. The studio, the award certificates, that radiant smile. My hands did tremble slightly — I noticed it, the way you notice a blood pressure reading that's a little high: it's a signal, and it needs to be read. Twenty years. I'm sitting in this car, engine still running, the traffic light cycling through its changes. There's a voice in my head asking "what if things had gone differently," but I quickly realize that the question itself is a trap. That road wasn't taken; this one was — that's just the fact of it. I chose family, stability, the clinic here, and the people who need me. The old man with the canvas bag carrying his late wife's photo, the girl trembling on the playground, the homeless man's faint breathing in that warehouse — these are things that actually happened. That person made his life into what he wanted it to be, and that's a good thing. I'm alive too, and I'm doing things too. A hollow feeling? Maybe a little — but hollowness isn't information, it's just a feeling in this moment, and it will pass. There are people waiting at home, tonight's dinner is real, tomorrow's project is real. I press down on the accelerator.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm with a mild undercurrent of disturbance (roughly 0.3 intensity). That photo triggered a brief signal — like a waveform on an ECG that drifts slightly off the baseline but doesn't sustain any oscillation. The disturbance comes from a momentary, direct look at "the road not taken," but that look is quickly taken over by my habitual way of processing things. No collapse, no surge — just a moment that needs to be dealt with and then filed away.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been through too many moments of feeling utterly paralyzed inside a whirlpool of chaos and emotion — the child who could only clutch a toy and shrink into the corner while his parents fought, the teaching assistant who fell apart in the emergency room — I know instinctively that letting myself sink into the bottomless pit of "what if things had gone differently" solves nothing. It would only leave me sitting at this intersection indefinitely.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters more to me are the things that are real and right in front of me — the people who come to the clinic, the people at home waiting for me to have dinner with them, the things I still have to deal with tomorrow. What I can't accept is letting the shadow of a past I can't change hollow out everything I'm still capable of doing now. That person is living well, and I'm glad for him — and then I keep driving home.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to drive straight home, not allowing this moment to spiral into a sweeping interrogation of my entire life, but instead treating it as a signal that has been read and can now be filed away. I turn my phone face-down, press the accelerator, and head in the direction of home.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择直接开车回家，不允许这个时刻演变成对整个人生的全面质疑，而是将其处理为一个已被读取、可以归档的信号。我把手机屏幕扣下，踩下油门，朝家的方向开去。

inner_consciousness.summary: He slid that PowerPoint across to me, and there was something in his eyes I've seen before — not joy, but a kind of fervor that's been lit up and hasn't burned out yet. I flipped through a few pages; the numbers were stacked up beautifully, but the logic was rotten — recruit downlines, tiered commissions, 'guaranteed to double your money' — I've seen family members outside the ER look at me with that same expression, begging me to promise their loved one would be okay. He said I was jealous of him. I didn't feel any emotional reaction at all; I was just thinking: he's not in a state right now where he can hear reason, but I can't let that be my excuse to say nothing. Eight hundred thousand is already gone — that's a fact, and it can't be changed. He's asking me whether I want to put in a hundred and fifty thousand, and that's the only variable I can control right now. He's an old friend, but friendship isn't a reason for my silence — if anything, it's the opposite. I've seen too many people make decisions in the grip of emotion and then pay the price for it. His defensiveness right now is because somewhere in a corner of his mind he already knows something is wrong — that's exactly why he's so desperate for my approval. I won't give it to him, but I'm not going to get into a fight over the 'jealousy' label either; that's just noise. All I need to do is say what I see, and then let him think it through himself.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm as the dominant tone, with a faint undercurrent of heaviness for an old friend's situation — not anxiety, but the kind of clear-eyed awareness you feel watching someone walk toward a cliff. Emotional intensity is low, but the certainty of my judgment is high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've spent my whole life watching people get pushed into decisions by emotion and fear, and then pay for it in hard facts — so my instinct is that the most genuine form of respect I can show him right now is to say clearly what I see, rather than choosing to stay quiet just to keep the peace over one dinner.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, speaking the truth matters more than preserving a surface-level harmony; what I cannot accept is pretending not to notice a friend jumping into a pit just because I'm afraid of offending him.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to keep trying to dissuade him — I'll lay out the factual hallmarks of a pyramid scheme structure directly, decline the investment invitation, and not dignify the 'jealousy' accusation with a response. I say: 'I'm not putting money in, and it's not because I'm jealous. This model of yours — recruiting downlines, tiered profit-sharing, returns that depend on the people below you rather than on any actual product — that's the textbook structure of a pyramid scheme. There's nothing I can do about the eight hundred thousand that's already gone, but don't ask me to hand over this hundred and fifty thousand.'

inner_consciousness.summary: The phone is lit up, and that message is just sitting there. I read it once, then again. Bookstore, perspectives, unwind — these words lined up together, and I know what they mean, but I'm not going to dance around it. What are the facts? We had a good conversation, she sent a goodnight message, and that's all there is to it. I think about my partner's message about the tutoring session — the two messages side by side, like two signals on different frequencies. I can feel the difference between them, but the difference itself isn't the problem. The problem is what I do next. That feeling of being genuinely heard — I'll acknowledge it exists, the same way I'd acknowledge a test result: no need to exaggerate it, no need to deny it either. But I'm equally clear that if I start treating that feeling like something worth chasing, that's where the real trouble begins. There's a lot of still water in this marriage — that's just a fact — but I'm not going to resolve it with some vague emotional connection. That's not a resolution; that's introducing a new variable. I can reply to the message, but I need to reply cleanly — no loose ends, no unnecessary expectations for her or for myself.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm as the dominant tone, with a faint undercurrent of clear-headedness. No anxiety, no agitation — just a slight cognitive awareness of the gap between things, not sadness exactly, more like the kind of noticing you do when you spot a data discrepancy. Emotional intensity is very low; no sign of being pulled under.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've always believed emotions are noise, I instinctively treat this warm feeling of being seen as a signal to verify rather than a direction to follow. Problems that can be solved should be solved; problems that can't should be accepted — but I will never use a new problem to paper over an old one.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, getting things straight matters more than making myself feel good. What I can't live with is using ambiguity to maintain a false kind of warmth — that's worse than still water.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to reply to the message, but keep the wording concise and measured — clear boundaries, neither deliberately cold nor leaving any opening. I reply: 'Made it home. That line of thinking from today's meeting is worth developing further — the bookstore idea has potential. Goodnight.'
