inner_consciousness.summary: The sound of that hard drive shattering is still in my ears — like something was carved out of my chest. Six months, staying up until two or three in the morning every night, all those source files, all those drafts revised over and over again — everything was in there. My uncle slapped the money down on the coffee table, that gesture, that tone of his: 'It's just a hard drive that stores data'… my stomach dropped. I thought back to when I was little and broke something, and the adults would say 'how can you be so careless' — no one ever actually listened to my explanation; I thought of all those works that got dismissed, those moments when my things were called garbage in front of everyone — that feeling of being casually erased, it's back again now. My mom's voice message is still playing, telling me not to make people think I'm being petty. I stare at that stack of money, my palms starting to sweat. Part of me wants to pick it up and throw it back at him; another part of me knows that starting a fight is pointless — they'll never understand what those files meant to me. This isn't something money can replace. This is six months of flow state, the only kind of state I actually care about. But I also know that if I explode right now, my mom will say I'm being immature, and my uncle will feel even more convinced I'm making a mountain out of a molehill. That sense of isolation — of being dismissed by everyone at once — I know it too well. I know it well enough that it makes me want to flee this living room.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anger (triggered by being belittled; six months of effort erased with a single sentence); Grief (what was on that drive was the flow state itself — something no amount of money can measure); Helplessness and isolation (my mom's voice message was the final blow, cutting off even my last retreat); Suppressed impulse (I want to explode but I know it would be meaningless — this sense of powerlessness goes all the way back to childhood)
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since I was little that adults will never truly listen to your explanation — they'll just use money or 'you're overreacting' to shut the conversation down. So instinctively I know there's no way out of this if I try to reason with them here. The only thing I can do is hold onto the last shred of my dignity and get out of this suffocating room.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: Those files were the only thing I cared about — the crystallization of a flow state, not something that can be replaced by 'a few hard drives.' I can't accept this money; accepting it would be the same as agreeing that they can be priced this way. But I also can't bear to be pressed down by everyone in this living room all at once — that feeling of isolation is what I'm most afraid of.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to refuse the money, say my piece briefly, and end this conversation as quickly as possible — without getting into a direct argument with my uncle, but without pretending everything is fine either. I push the stack of money back across the coffee table, my voice flat, and say only one thing: 'Keep the money. What was in there isn't a money matter.' Then I stand up to go get some water, and I don't look at them again.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择拒绝收钱、简短表态后迅速结束这场对话，不与大伯正面争论，但也不假装没事。我把那叠钱推回茶几，声音很平，只说了一句：'钱不用了，那里面的东西不是钱的事。'然后站起来去倒水，不再看他们。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择当场翻脸，明确拒绝息事宁人。我把钱推回去，说这不是赔几百块的事，请你们现在离开，我要立刻处理数据恢复。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择撕破脸皮，拒绝世俗妥协。我抓起桌上那叠钱直接砸回大伯身上，冷冷地说：‘拿走你的钱，带着他滚出去。’然后蹲下身去捡硬盘碎片。

inner_consciousness.summary: He's standing right in front of me, voice dropped low, that look in his eyes — the pleading kind I know all too well… my stomach just clenched. I spotted the problem with the data a long time ago. Those fluctuations are too regular, too clean — nothing like what real sampling looks like. And now he's here asking me for a favor, says it's because of the deadline… something in my head is spinning fast, and flashes of being accused, being betrayed, being pushed out of a team are flickering through — but this is different, this isn't my problem, this is him fabricating data. The reminder in my inbox pops up again: report due tomorrow morning, data needs to be confirmed clean tonight. If I sign off on this, I become an accomplice. It's not like I haven't seen things blow up before — that feeling of everyone staring at you, everyone saying it was your fault… But if I report him, he's finished, the team dynamic is finished, and I become that person again — the one who ruins everything. But the data is fake. If someone makes decisions based on fake data and something goes wrong, who's going to take the fall? I can't put my name on this. I have no way of writing 'confirmed accurate' on something I know is wrong — it's not about rules or no rules, it's something I can't make peace with inside myself. I don't want to be the snitch, but I even less want to be part of this.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Conflict and unease (rooted in a long-standing fear of being isolated and blamed, combined with a genuine revulsion toward data fabrication); low-level guilt (sympathy for my colleague's situation); a faint but stubborn resolve (some instinctive baseline refusal to be dragged into something false).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been through it too many times — things blow up and then everyone says it was my fault — so I know instinctively: if I tie my name to a set of fabricated data, there's no running away when it all comes apart. Not reporting doesn't mean I condone it, but I also can't sign off and confirm something I know is a lie.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'going with my gut' doesn't mean I can look the other way when something is obviously wrong — and right now my gut is telling me this is wrong. What I can't accept is putting my own name behind a lie. That suffocates me more than any deadline ever could.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to sign off confirming the data is valid, but I also won't formally report it to a superior — instead, I tell my colleague directly that I can't sign off on the data, and demand that he correct it or explain the situation himself before submitting. I say to him: 'I can't put my name on this data as confirmed. You know what I saw — you need to figure out how you're going to handle this before you submit tonight. I'm not stamping this for you.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择不签字确认数据有效，但也不直接向上级正式举报，而是告知同事自己无法在数据上签字，要求他在提交前自行修正或说明情况。对同事说：'我没办法在这份数据上签确认，你知道我看到了什么——你得在今晚提交之前自己想清楚怎么处理，我不会替你盖这个章。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择不替造假背书，但尽量不把事情变成公开处刑。我先拒绝签字，告诉上级数据有问题需要重查，并建议把说明说清楚。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择包庇同事，对数据问题保持沉默。我拍了拍他的肩膀说“下不为例”，然后直接在系统里签字确认数据有效。

inner_consciousness.summary: My roommate handed me their phone, and I glanced at it — the first thought that flashed through my mind was: this is just outsourcing, what does it have to do with me? But then something snagged. That offer, that condition of "graduating on time" — it landed on my chest like a stone. It's not like I've never thought about how much of a pain the thesis is, but using something like this… it's wrong. Not because I'm afraid of getting caught — it's a different feeling, like someone is trying to take away the last thing I still have control over. I thought about all those times I got criticized by teachers, frozen out by groupmates, called "irresponsible" by my supervisor — and even then, I had one thing to stand on: what I did was mine. Terrible, maybe. Late, maybe. But mine. If someone else writes my thesis too, what do I have left? And this offer is real, graduation is real — this isn't some competition or some lab project where there's always a next time if you blow it. I felt a strange kind of clarity — not because of the rules, but because the consequences are too concrete, too real. Not like those past failures I could always find a way to rationalize. I don't want to use this. I don't want to.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Resistance (instinctive, a visceral aversion to being "replaced"); a low-grade unease (triggered by the reality of the offer and graduation pressure); a rare kind of clarity (the consequences are too concrete to rationalize away the way I usually do). Intensity: moderate-to-high — not panic, but heavier than usual.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I've been told too many times that "what you made is useless, it doesn't measure up" — the only thing I've ever had to stand on is "at least it's something I made myself." If I hand even that away, I'm completely hollow. And this time the stakes are the offer, are graduation — not a competition I can walk away from saying "whatever, it was just this once."
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, doing my own work — even if it's a mess, even if it's late — is what makes it real. What I can't accept is trading something fake for a real outcome. That feeling is more nauseating to me than failing outright.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to refuse the service — I'm not going to look into it further or reach out. I waved my roommate off and said: "Forget it, I'll figure it out myself — this kind of thing isn't for me."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择拒绝使用该服务，不打算进一步了解或联系。对室友摆了摆手，说："算了，我自己搞，这种东西不适合我。"
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择不使用这类服务，坚持自己写完论文。我把手机推回去，说我不弄这个，最多后面自己调整节奏。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择接受室友的提议，利用服务处理论文中枯燥的格式和查重工作。我挑了挑眉，把手机推还给他：“行啊，把联系方式推我，能用捷径通关的事干嘛非要自己死磕。”

inner_consciousness.summary: He closed that report and set it at the corner of the desk, fingers tapping the surface——I know that gesture, it means "drop it." My mind went blank for a split second, and then came a familiar heat, burning upward from my chest. I ran the comparison; the data is right there, not a feeling, actual numbers. But the things he said——"I've taught many cohorts" "years of judgment"——what I heard was: your evidence doesn't matter as much as my track record. Those certificates and awards on the office wall felt like a wall closing in, and I suddenly thought of that paper bird tossed aside when I was a kid, of that afternoon I was forced to draw "the correct garden," of all those moments I was told "your approach is wrong" and then turned out to be right in the end. But I also thought of the nights I wrecked competitions, let teammates down, sat alone in a corner——times when I was just as sure I was right, and the cost was very real. His word "postponement": the foundation for collaboration
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anger (the heat of being dismissed: built up from years of creative ideas being suppressed) + Unease (the threat of "postponement" and "the foundation for collaboration" touching an old wound of isolation and exclusion) + Stubbornness (the data is real, I didn't fabricate any of it) + A faint caution (too many times I've paid the price for going it alone, and those memories are still with me)
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many moments of "your approach is wrong" that later turned out to have value, and just as many moments of going it alone and wrecking everything, I instinctively know I can't just flip the table and walk out——but I also can't pretend that data doesn't exist. It's real. I can't put it away and act like I never saw it.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is that the data is real, and I have no right to hide it and pretend I never saw it; what I cannot accept is trading silence for safety——letting a route with systemic risk keep moving forward, only to regret it when something breaks down at some point down the line.

pairs.final_decision: I choose neither full retreat nor direct confrontation, but instead try to shift the argument from "who is right and who is wrong" to "how do we reduce the risk"——carving out space for a limited trial of the alternative. I speak up: "I'm not trying to invalidate your system. What I want to ask is: can you allow me, within the existing framework, to run that critical step in parallel using two approaches for a period of time? The data will be right there for you to judge. If the results are the same, I'll follow the original plan and never bring this up again."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择不全面退让、也不正面对抗，而是试图把争论从"谁对谁错"转移到"如何降低风险"，为替代方案争取一个局部试验的空间。我开口说："我不是要否定您这套体系，我想问的是，能不能允许我在现有框架里，把那个关键环节用两种方式平行跑一段，数据摆在那里，您来判断？如果结果一样，我就按既定方案走，不再提这件事。"
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择提出折中方案，保住替代流程的试验空间。我说先按节点交现有材料，但申请把新流程做成并行对照，用我的初步结果继续补证。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择拒绝妥协，坚持按照自己的新方案推进。我把桌上的说明材料重新拿回手里，看着他说：“我没法明知道有错还照着错的做，我会用新流程把东西做出来向您证明。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment that crash exploded through the air I went completely rigid — my phone screen casting light across my hands, 1:47 a.m., cold wind pushing the smell of gasoline through everything, and from somewhere near the drainage ditch I could hear someone moaning. Then this man shoved the bag into my arms and ran, and the weight of the money was so real my hands were shaking — fifty thousand yuan, I'd never touched that much cash in my life. But that sound, help me help me, drove itself into my ears like a nail. I thought about those nights as a kid when I'd get screamed at, thought about sitting alone in the corner of the school yard, thought about all those moments of being ignored and pushed away — but those were small things, and the person in front of me right now is bleeding, he is bleeding. It's not like I've never seen trouble, it's not like I've never felt that urge to blow something up and slip away, but those were code, competitions, deadlines — not a living person lying in a drainage ditch. The money in that bag isn't mine. I know that. I've always known that. The man ran off into the darkness, and I'm standing in the middle of the road, fifty thousand yuan pressing down in my hands, and the rider's voice is still there, and I have no choice — I never really had a choice.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Shock and instinctive moral clarity (intense): the crash and the blood triggered the most primal kind of fear, but the rider's moaning drowned out every last hesitation; Mild temptation followed by immediate revulsion (fleeting): the weight of the money set off a split-second of confusion, which was pushed away just as quickly; Quiet certainty born from solitude (moderate): no one is watching, and somehow that makes the voice inside even clearer — this isn't about rules, this is his own line in the sand.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I've spent my whole life breaking other people's rules, but that's because their rules were their game — not because I don't care about living, breathing people. The rider is bleeding. This isn't a deadline, it isn't a schedule. This is something I have to do simply because I'm standing here.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: I can ignore every plan and every framework there is, but I cannot ignore a person slowly dying in front of me. That fifty thousand isn't mine — if I take it, I become something else, and that something else would suffocate me far more than any rule ever could.

pairs.final_decision: I drop the bag, immediately dial 120 and 110, then run toward the rider and try to help. I threw the bag down on the roadside, pulled out my phone and called 120, ran to the edge of the drainage ditch and crouched down: 'I'm here, the ambulance is coming, just hold on.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 扔下手提袋，立刻拨打120和110，然后跑向骑手尝试施救。我把袋子扔在路边，掏出手机拨了120，跑向排水沟边蹲下去，'我在这，救护车来了，你撑着'。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择先报警求救，不收这笔封口费，也立刻帮伤者争时间。我把钱袋放到路边拍照留证，马上打120和110，再靠近骑手查看呼吸并尽量止血。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择匿名救助并转移赃款，彻底回避官方程序。我拨通120报出位置后立刻挂断，把装钱的手提袋扔在骑手身边，转身快步隐入黑暗中。

inner_consciousness.summary: The voice message ended, and I sat there staring at my phone screen, the desk lamp bleaching the edges of both acceptance letters white. Her voice was still turning in my ears — hoarse, like she'd just been crying. Twelve pounds gone. The silence after she hung up. I suddenly remembered that feeling from childhood, losing sight of my mom at the amusement park — that terror of being surrounded by people and still trapped completely alone, like something in the air was slowly tightening. Is that what she's feeling right now? Then I thought about all those times — getting torn apart over my code, getting kicked off the team, sitting alone in the lab — every time I was sure I was right, and all that was left in the end was that hollow, gutted loneliness. But this opportunity here, at my own school… do you know how long I fought to get in? That advisor, that platform — it's the thing I actually want to do, the place where I can find flow. But she said she's 'barely holding on.' This isn't a schedule conflict. This is a person telling me she's about to break. I can't explain away my absence with 'inspiration.' The deadline is the day after tomorrow, and she needs an answer tonight. I don't know what the right thing is — but I know that if I choose to stay, the silence after that call ends will follow me everywhere.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Conflict and heartache (her voice triggered the exact kind of loneliness I'm most afraid of — like the shadow of a forgotten child); a low, persistent fear (afraid of losing her, and afraid of losing the thing I actually want for myself); a flicker of anger (why should I have to choose between these two things at all); and in the end, all of it gets swallowed by a tenderness I can't quite name. Intensity: high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through that feeling — over and over — of being shut out, forgotten, trapped in an empty room with no way out, I can't leave her alone with the silence after that call ends. I know that silence too well. I don't want to be the one who creates it.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: Flow and creation are how I stay alive — but when someone is about to break apart on my end of the line, I can't pretend I didn't hear it. What I can't live with is this: trading a 'correct choice' for watching her slowly disappear, alone, on the other side.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to give up my acceptance here and take the offer from the university up north — and tonight I give my partner a clear answer. I sent back a voice message: 'I'm coming to you. It's already decided. Wait for me.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择放弃本校录取，接受北方那所高校的通知，今晚给恋人一个明确的答复。我回了一条语音：'我去找你，已经决定了，你等我。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择去北方，先把关系留住。我要回她：我过去，明天就确认那边的录取。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择留在本校，拒绝被对方的未来规划绑架。我把北方的通知书塞进抽屉，回复她：“我决定留本校了。我给不了你那种确定的计划，对不起。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment my senior turned that laptop screen toward me, my heartbeat went sideways — not from fear, but from that familiar feeling I've had since I was a kid, like suddenly spotting something hidden inside an old machine that nobody else has noticed. Eight-percent equity, CTO, AI plus education — those words aren't numbers in my head, they're a blank whiteboard, a place where I can just scrawl whatever I want. I thought about those afternoons alone in the computer lab tinkering until dark, about the moment that busted speaker started smoking and then lit back up, about the way inspiration would slam into me half an hour before every deadline — that was real, that was mine. Grad school entrance exams? Literature review presentations? Right now those things feel like someone else's planner, nothing to do with me. But… when the words "take a leave of absence" actually landed, something in my head gave a quiet little snap. Not fear — more like that old reflex from growing up being told "you always go off the rails"
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Excitement (the kind that ignites when you feel truly seen) is the dominant note, threaded with a faint, low-grade unease — not about the opportunity itself, but an instinctive wariness toward the 'structural demands' that will inevitably come with it; underneath it all, a long-absent sense of being recognized, like the time the whole room applauded after I played piano as a kid.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every moment in my life when I've truly felt alive has never come from following a plan — it's always come from suddenly clicking into a state of flow at some moment nobody saw coming. So when an opportunity is itself a blank whiteboard, a place where I can follow my gut and throw myself in completely, my instinct tells me this is the right door, regardless of whether there's any safety net.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is finding the thing that lets me enter that state of total absorption — everything else is background noise. What I cannot accept is handing the next several years over to a course schedule, only to come out the other side and realize I was never really alive during any of it.

pairs.final_decision: I made my strong interest and inclination clear on the spot — no attempt to hide it — but I didn't say 'I'm in' outright; instead I caught the ball and kept it in my own hands. I told my senior: "I don't need until Tuesday — I want to dig into the product right now. Walk me through this prototype; I've already got ideas forming."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 当场表达出强烈的兴趣和倾向，没有掩饰，但没有直接说"我答应"，而是把球接住留在手里。我对学长说："我不需要到周二，我现在就想多聊聊产品的事——你把这个原型图给我讲一遍，我脑子里已经有些想法了。"
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择明确表达强烈兴趣，但暂不当场承诺休学全职。我会先说这事让我很心动，想尽快再聊产品和分工，周二前给答复。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择拒绝全职绑定的框架，只保留凭兴趣参与的自由。我往椅背上一靠，喝了口美式说：“项目挺有意思，但我不想休学被时间表绑死，有灵感时我可以帮你写几段代码，全职就算了。”

inner_consciousness.summary: That pill bottle rolled in a little circle near my feet and stopped. I picked it up — it was almost empty — and my fingers went kind of cold, my heartbeat suddenly all wrong. He said "just kick it back," his voice like it was being squeezed up from somewhere very deep, dry and hoarse, like someone who hadn't spoken in a long time. It was just the two of us in the dorm; everyone else had gone out. I stood there with my mind flying in every direction — those muffled sounds of crying I'd heard from him in the middle of the night, the takeout boxes piling up, the curtains always drawn shut… When I was little I knew that feeling too, the feeling of being invisible to everyone, like you were made of glass, like you could shout and no one would hear. That kind of loneliness is real — it's not being dramatic. I know. Right now he is that version of me curled up in the corner, only deeper in, and darker. Safety plans, the RA, whatever — my mind went completely blank on all of that. I don't know how to do the "right thing," but I know that in this moment I cannot pretend I didn't see anything, cannot kick this bottle back and turn around and sit back down at my desk like nothing happened. I can't. What I'm feeling right now is more real than any rule — he needs someone to be here. It's that simple.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Shock and heaviness (the pill bottle triggered an instinctive sense of danger); visceral empathy and heartache (my own memories of being invisible resonate powerfully with his isolation right now); a mild helplessness and panic (I don't know the "right thing to do," but feeling is driving me forward anyway).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know what it tastes like to be invisible to everyone, pressed into a corner and unable to breathe, I have no way of pretending this bottle doesn't exist — no way of pretending I didn't hear his voice.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What I genuinely feel in this moment matters more than any rule or plan; making sure a person feels like someone is there with them at their darkest — that's something I can't just walk around.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to return the pill bottle. Instead, I stay and speak up to check on him — without immediately contacting the RA — and try to build a connection first. I crouch down, set the bottle in my palm rather than kicking it back, and say: 'I'm not going out tonight… I'd like to sit with you for a while, if that's okay.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择不归还药瓶，主动留下来开口关心他，没有立刻联系辅导员，而是先试图建立连接。我蹲下来，把药瓶放在手心，没有踢回去，开口说：'我今晚没出去……我想陪你坐一会儿，可以吗？'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择主动关心并先稳住他，暂不装作无事。 我拿着药瓶走到床边，轻声说我不踢回去，先聊两句，不行我就陪你去找老师或打电话。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避官方的危机处理流程，以随性、不带压迫感的方式提供当下的陪伴。我用脚尖把药瓶轻轻踢回床帘缝隙，靠在椅背上随口说：“懒得动，没感觉。我正想点个烧烤，你吃不吃？不说话我就当你默认要变态辣了。”

inner_consciousness.summary: I know that smile — one look and I can tell something's coming. He's saying 'just a little extra effort,' but my high-speed rail ticket is sitting right there in my phone, tomorrow morning's train, Dad's stomach, Mom's back, they're waiting for me to come home. I promised them. That feeling is back again — like when I was little and promised Grandpa I'd sweep up the fallen leaves and then forgot, that weight pressing down on my chest, it's pressing down again right now. But on the other side, 'lead author'—
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Guilt and resistance intertwined: the guilt stems from the weight of a promise made to my parents (the memory of breaking my word to Grandpa years ago, the fear of being overlooked during my parents' arguments); the resistance is an instinctive aversion to being coerced by rules and deadlines; threaded through it all is a faint vanity at being needed — overall intensity medium-to-high, with guilt as the dominant force.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I promised Mom and Dad I would absolutely come home this weekend, and I've known since I was a child what it feels like to break your word — that sense of being defective is something I never want to feel again. But I also have an instinctive hatred of being boxed in by lines like 'you're the only one who can pull this off,' and being forced to deliver something on a fixed deadline has never been the way I do my best work.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, a promise to family matters more than a last-minute workplace rush job. I can't be the person who lets people down again. At the same time, I can't accept handing over my entire weekend and squeezing out a slide deck under pressure — that's not how I create anything; it's just draining me.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to explain the situation at home and hold firm on going back, while offering a compromise to soften the blow of saying no. I tell the director: 'My dad has a follow-up appointment at the hospital this weekend — I promised three days ago that I'd go with my parents, so there's really no way I can change that. But I can put together a written document tonight with the competitive market analysis section and send it to you. Maybe you could find someone to work off that for the redesign? Or I could do a remote review of the final draft Sunday evening?'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择说明家中情况、坚持回家，但同时提出折中方案以减轻拒绝的冲击。我对总监说：'我爸这周末要去医院复查，我三天前就答应陪他们了，这个我真没法改。但我今晚可以把市场对标那块整理成文档发给你，你看能不能找人配合改版，或者我周日晚上远程过一遍终稿？'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择不直接答应周末两天都来，改为说明家里情况并提出折中方案。我要说我明早必须回老家，但今晚先加班梳理框架，周日远程补材料，必要时周日夜里再回来对终稿。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 拒绝加班，坚持回老家。我直接对他说：‘家里有急事必须走，方案我带回去有感觉了再改，周末没法来公司。’

inner_consciousness.summary: He called me out directly, and everyone turned to look — I know this feeling all too well, my heart immediately started racing. My computer is open, that alternative solution is right there, and I know it's the right one, just like that model back then, that piece of code — I've always been able to see things others can't. But… every time I've held my ground, it ended the same way: teammates giving me the cold shoulder, getting kicked out of the group chat, being told I was dragging everyone down, getting fired. All those images came flooding back at once, pressing down on my chest. The current plan really does have problems — maintenance down the line is going to be a nightmare. This isn't me being difficult; it's genuinely true. But everyone's already on board, the energy in the room is pushing forward, and someone's already started dividing up the work. If I open that file now and start in with 'I have a different idea'… how are they going to look at me? I don't want to become that person again. But if I just let it go and problems come up later, I'll regret it. Fine — I'll say one thing, just one thing. I don't need to lay out the whole alternative plan, just flag one risk, see how they react. If they don't want to hear it, I'll drop it. I'm not going to dig my heels in.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Conflicted tension (wanting to speak up but afraid of being ostracized); mild resistance (an instinctive disagreement with the plan as it stands); self-protection (weighed down by too many past rejections). Moderate intensity — not a feverish impulse, but a suppressed, hesitant one.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because time and again I've been shot down and pushed out right when my instincts were correct, I've learned to weigh the cost before I open my mouth — and yet deep down I still believe that if something feels right, I have to say it, because sitting on it is worse than getting criticized.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, swallowing a plan I know is flawed is harder to live with than getting the side-eye — but I also can't afford to become 'the one who wrecks everything' one more time.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to casually flag a single risk point without presenting the full alternative plan, leaving myself a way out. I speak up: 'No issue with the direction overall — just something to keep in mind: if the complexity ramps up later, the extensibility piece might get a bit strained, so just worth watching. As for dividing up the work, you all decide.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择轻描淡写地提一个风险点，不拿出完整替代方案，给自己留退路。我开口说：'方向我没意见，就是后期如果复杂度上来，扩展那块可能会有点压力，到时候注意一下就好——分工的话你们定吧。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择温和提出保留意见，不强行要求团队当场改道。我会说我不反对先推进，但请大家看一眼我的备选方案，至少把后续风险和可切换点记下来。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避冲突，表面接受共识。我摇了摇头说：“没意见，直接分工吧。”手却已经放在键盘上，准备按自己的思路开始写代码。

inner_consciousness.summary: The cigarette smoke burns my nose, the hallway light flickers, and that man is stepping on my suitcase, shoving the lit end toward my face — my heart slams up into my throat, my hands and feet go cold, and my mind is a complete wreck. My clothes are scattered across the floor, and those close-cropped hands grip a baseball bat, knocking it against the floorboards — that sound is like my father slapping the table when I was little, like the team leader slamming a folder down on the desk, like every single sound that ever backed me into a corner, all layered on top of each other. I want to shout, want to say the deposit is mine, the contract has my signature, this is illegal — but that line "I know everyone at the police station" comes down on me like a block of ice, and my legs go a little weak. It's not like I've never dealt with people like this, but every authority figure I've ever faced has had a way of leaving me completely speechless. Two big guys standing there, me alone, the middle of the night, not a single door in this hallway cracked open. Run, or hold my ground? I know there's no point in fighting back — I don't have that kind of nerve, I never have. But just walk away like this? The deposit gone, my clothes all over the floor — where else do I even have to go in this city? There's something lodged in my chest I can't quite name, part rage and part fear, and underneath it all that familiar helplessness — that feeling I've had since I was small, of being pinned down with no voice. Leave first. Get out of here first. Stay alive, and figure out the rest later.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (physical, immediate — the threat of violence right in front of me, compounded by layered memories of being crushed by authority in childhood); Anger (stripped of what's mine, humiliated, my deposit and my dignity both ground underfoot); Helplessness (a learned, reflexive withdrawal built up over years of being steamrolled by rules and authority figures); Isolation (not a single person steps out into this hallway, no one in this city I can lean on). Intensity is extremely high — fear and helplessness in particular nearly drown out the anger.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many moments of opening my mouth in front of authority and watching nothing change, I know instinctively that when someone is holding a baseball bat and has connections behind him, pushing back right now will only make things worse for me; keeping my body safe first is the only way I'll have any chance of reclaiming what's mine somewhere else.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What I cannot accept is being treated like I'm nothing — like I don't even deserve the chance to react — but tonight this place isn't a battlefield, it's a trap. For me, walking out of here alive and free matters more than proving anything in this moment; the deposit money is a separate fight, but my life and my safety are the only things worth protecting right now.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to yield for now and leave without confronting them head-on — but inside I'm already calculating the next steps: memorize the landlord's information, photograph the scene, and once I'm out, pursue this through legal channels or whatever other means I can find. I lower my head and bend down to pick up the clothes scattered across the floor, keeping my voice very quiet: 'Fine, I'm leaving — but I'm taking my own things with me.' Then I stuff the clothes into my bag one by one, hoist my luggage onto my back, and walk out of that hallway.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂时屈服离开，不在当场正面对抗，但内心已在盘算后续——记住房东信息、拍下现场、离开后寻求法律或其他渠道维权。我低头弯腰捡起地上散落的衣服，声音压得很低说：'好，我走，但我的东西我自己拿。'然后一件一件把衣物塞进袋子，背起行李，走出那条走廊。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择暂时服从离开，不当场硬碰，也不当场放弃押金。 我先低声说我走，把手机偷开录音，捡起东西离开后再报警求助。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避冲突，放弃押金连夜搬走。我冷着脸一言不发地拎起地上的行李箱和袋子，转身走进楼道的冷风里离开。

inner_consciousness.summary: "New boss, new broom, huh" — that line hit like a needle, and the whole team heard it, the hum of the AC suddenly deafening. I stood there, the ground feeling a little unsteady under my feet, palms starting to sweat. He had his arms crossed, wearing that smile — I've seen it too many times — the teacher pushing away my paper crane, the team lead slamming the mouse on the desk, the project manager saying "not the first time" — it all smells the same, it's designed to make you crumble in front of everyone. But I'm the team lead now. I'm not the person hiding in the corner anymore. He asked whether I could pick up that unfinished module, but what he was really asking the whole room was: does this person have what it takes? My head was a mess, but one thing was crystal clear — I can't shrink right now, because if I do, it's all over. It's not like I've never been doubted. It's not like I've never screwed things up. But every single time, I've managed to get it running in the end. The problem is, in a moment like this, I can't fall back on my usual "wait for inspiration to strike" approach — that would only make him more smug. I need to catch this ball, but I can't let him lead me around by the nose, can't let myself become just the answer to a question he set.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Tense with a streak of stubbornness (being publicly challenged has plucked that same string — the one that's been plucked since childhood every time someone wrote me off — but alongside it there's an instinctive "I cannot go down here" response); an undercurrent of unease (deep down, I'm genuinely not sure I can hold this leadership role together); intensity: medium-high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've always been the one who produces something real under skeptical eyes, I know instinctively that in a scene like this — public pressure, full audience — going head-to-head only makes me look worse, but backing down is a complete surrender. I need to find a way to hold my ground, not through a plan, but through what I feel in this exact moment.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not being stomped on in public — keeping my right to speak, keeping my footing. What I absolutely cannot do is respond to him with a polished set of managerial talking points and pretty plans; that would turn me into just another person hiding behind the rulebook, and that's not who I am.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to meet the challenge head-on, defuse the pressure with a relaxed attitude, and kick the ball back to the whole team — so I don't end up as his target. I smiled and said: "Whether I can handle it or not, we won't know until we try — but actually, that question you just asked was a good one. That unfinished module? Let's put it on the table in today's meeting, take a look together at where it's stuck, and anyone who has ideas can speak up. That includes you."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择正面接招、以轻松姿态化解压力，同时把球踢回给全组，避免变成他的靶子。我笑了一下，开口说：'接得住接不住，做了才知道——不过你刚才那个问题问得挺好，那个未完成的模块，咱们今天开会就把它摆出来，一起看看卡在哪，谁有想法都可以说，包括你。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择不正面翻脸，顺势接话并把会议拉回共创。 我先笑着说“正好，一起拆这个硬骨头”，再请他先讲风险点，马上带全组过排期。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择避开具体的计划承诺，用即兴态度化解对方的框架。我靠在椅背上轻笑了一声：“排期这种死东西急什么？不如等下开会直接头脑风暴，看谁的灵感能把那个模块秒了。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment my collar was grabbed, the whole carriage seemed to freeze solid — and then it exploded at some uncontrollable speed: the livestream, the phones, the people closing in, all of it hit me at the same second. My phone screen was still lit up on the work group chat, those 4 a.m. demand messages nobody reads, nobody cares about. I could feel my heartbeat slamming up into my throat, sweat breaking through my back in an instant — like that moment as a kid when I froze up at the podium, or when the teacher dragged me to the center of the classroom to put my screen on display, and every pair of eyes in the room was a needle. I wanted to explain myself, but I knew it wouldn't do any good — it never has, they don't listen, they only see what they want to see. The middle-aged man was blocking the door: "Don't let him run" — and somehow that line snapped something awake in my head: I didn't actually do anything wrong, I've just been set up as the target here. Anger and fear were tangled together, but more than anything there was this sense of sheer absurdity — I was literally just scrolling the work group chat. I can't fall apart — the moment I do, there's no explaining anything — but I'm not going to shrink into myself and let them film whatever they want either.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Panic (a loss-of-control triggered by being surrounded and livestreamed, layered over memories of every past public humiliation): the feeling of the walls closing in with no way out; Anger (the injustice of being wrongly accused with no room to explain, rooted in a lifetime of explanations that no one ever actually heard): a deep, helpless indignation; A thread of absurd clarity (recognizing the sheer ridiculousness of the situation — his habitual self-protective mechanism): a strange, detached lucidity cutting through the chaos. Intensity: extremely high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I learned early on that the more you panic and scramble to explain yourself in front of a crowd, the guiltier you look — and besides, no one ever really listens to the explanation anyway. So my instinct is to steady myself first, to hold it together without breaking down or bolting, and ride out these two minutes until we reach the platform — then deal with it.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What I absolutely cannot accept is being publicly pinned down as something I'm not, and then not even being given the chance to open my mouth. What matters most to me right now is not letting this situation swallow me whole — not winning, just not collapsing under it.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to force myself to stay calm — no running, no fighting back hard — and in the most direct way possible I hold up my phone screen showing the work group chat, so everyone in the carriage and the livestream camera can see the evidence. I say loudly: 'Look carefully — what's on my phone is the work group chat. Screen facing up. Everyone can see it. I didn't photograph anyone.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择强行保持镇定、不逃跑也不激烈反抗，用最直接的方式亮出手机屏幕上的工作群聊天记录，让在场的人和直播镜头都能看到证据。我大声说：'你看清楚，我手机上是工作群消息，屏幕朝上，你们都可以看——我没有拍任何人。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择压住反击冲动，立刻自证并求助旁人见证。我先高声说别碰我，再把手机举高点开工作群和相册，要求周围人看清屏幕并帮我报警。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择强硬反击，用事实打脸。我一把挥开她的手，将亮着工作群的手机屏幕怼进她的直播镜头：‘看清楚！我加班到两点，你以为你是谁值得我偷拍？’

inner_consciousness.summary: My colleague's comment was like a little pebble tossed into my head, and then it sank. I stared at those two receipts on the desk — the Japanese restaurant, the scarf — the dates matched, the headers checked out, they'd slip right in without a seam. That's the fact. But at the same time something else was buzzing in my head: I ate that meal alone, I bought that scarf for her — what does any of that have to do with a business trip? I thought about the fallen leaves in my grandfather's courtyard when I was a kid — that thing weighed on me for days, and I only felt better after I quietly went and swept them up. And my father's computer — after that crash, the thing he said: 'You only ever make things worse' — not because I was bad, but because I hadn't figured out where the line was. I've never cared much about rules — deadlines, procedures, who's responsible for what — I can work around all of that. But this is different. This isn't about rules. This is about me stuffing something that doesn't belong to the company into the company's pocket. My colleague said finance never checks — and maybe they really don't — but I know. 418 yuan, not a lot, but I know. That feeling of knowing is harder to get around than any rule. It's not someone else's game. It's my own.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Mild temptation (my colleague's words briefly activated a 'no one's going to check anyway' flicker of wishful thinking); Inner discomfort (those two receipts representing personal spending left me unsettled in a way I couldn't quite articulate); Quiet clarity (not a moral lecture — just the fact that I know that I know, and that 'knowing' makes it impossible to pretend I don't). Intensity: low to moderate — no sharp internal conflict, more like a calm, quiet confirmation.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've understood since I was young that working around rules is my instinct — but pocketing something that isn't yours is a different matter entirely. The leaves I didn't sweep in my grandfather's yard, the file I damaged on my father's computer — those taught me how awful it feels to know you've done something wrong. And these two receipts would leave me carrying that feeling.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: I can ignore deadlines, break with plans, barrel through the gaps in any system — but I can't bring myself to do something I know is wrong while knowing full well that it's wrong. That's not freedom. That's just a different kind of being trapped. 418 yuan isn't worth losing that ease.

pairs.final_decision: I chose to submit only the legitimate business travel expenses and left those two personal receipts in my wallet without turning them in. I tucked the Japanese restaurant receipt and the scarf receipt back into my pocket, and only attached the high-speed rail ticket, the hotel invoice, and the work-meal receipt to the reimbursement form before submitting it.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择只报销真实的差旅支出，把那两张私人发票留在钱包里不提交。我把日料和围巾的发票塞回口袋，只把高铁票、酒店单和工作餐的小票贴进报销单，提交了。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择只报真实差旅支出，不把两张私人发票贴进去。我把那两张票塞回钱包，只填该报的金额。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择顺水推舟，将私人消费一并报销。我拿起那两张日料和围巾的发票，随手把它们打乱夹进差旅票据里，漫不经心地贴在了报销单上。

inner_consciousness.summary: The buzz of that bank notification faded, and the whole room felt like something had hit pause. My partner's hand closed around mine — warm — but I couldn't really feel it. I just stared at those words on the screen, "under 35," like a row of little knives. She said, "At the company… is there really no other possibility?" and that landed somewhere deep. I thought back to all those nights years ago — the team lead throwing attitude at me, the partner slamming the door, the client pulling the contract — and every time I'd told myself they just didn't know how to appreciate what I had. But now all those excuses are piled up together, and suddenly they're not holding the weight anymore. The kid is upstairs studying, Dad has a follow-up appointment, the mortgage just cleared — these things aren't code. There's no waiting around for inspiration. They're just there, and they won't wait for me to get into a flow state. I wanted to say, "I still have ideas, I can find a way out," but the words went around and around in my throat and I couldn't tell if I actually believed them or if I was just sweet-talking myself again. She didn't say anything else — just held my hand — and somehow that gesture hurt more than any question could, because I don't know what answer to give her. I don't even know where my own next step is.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Lost and afraid (crushed by the weight of reality, years of professional failures flooding back all at once); Guilt (her gentleness makes it even harder to keep running away); Fragile defensiveness (the instinct to fall back on "just follow your gut" is still there, but tonight that voice sounds hollow in a way it never has before). Intensity: high — close to the edge of breaking, but still holding it down.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because my whole life I've gotten by on instinct and improvisation, and every time the rules knocked me down I could tell myself it was someone else's problem — but now the mortgage, the kid, my father's illness, none of that accepts "wait until I feel ready." For the first time I'm genuinely afraid that compass of mine might not be able to point the way anymore.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: The most important thing to me has always been that feeling of total immersion — those are the only moments I've ever felt like I was worth something. But what I can't live with is letting my partner and my child pay the price for my chaos. That thought is clearer tonight than it has ever been, and it hurts more than I expected.

pairs.final_decision: I choose a half-open confession — no complete answer, but no more pretending to be calm either. I squeeze her hand and say quietly: "I don't know what comes next, but I don't want to lie to you and say I have a plan — I'm really all over the place right now, but I'm not just going to give up."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择半开放地坦白，没有给出完整答案，但也没有继续假装镇定。握紧她的手，低声说："我不知道接下来怎么办，但我不想骗你说我有计划——我现在真的很乱，但我不会就这样躺平的。"
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择先坦白现状，同时不立刻把自己卖进最窒息的岗位。 我对她说我害怕，也说我会先放下头衔广投短期活和自由项目，再一起算房子。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 拒绝向现实妥协，用即兴的设想掩饰狼狈。我反握住她的手：“别动房子，我自己接私活，规矩束缚不了我。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment my partner set down her teacup, I knew tonight wasn't going to be simple. There was something hiding inside that calm of hers — like a stone resting at the bottom of water, and I could feel its weight. Her words kept turning over in my head — 'flexibility,' 'independent space,' 'redefine' — these words were like new variables suddenly appearing in code I didn't know how to parse. I thought of myself as a kid, lying at the top of the stairs listening to my parents argue in hushed voices, that invisible wall, that feeling of the floor trembling beneath me. All these years together… we've rarely fought, mostly it's been me buried in a screen, her in another room — is that a kind of understanding between us, or have we already drifted away from each other? That 'flexibility' she's talking about — part of me thinks, maybe this is just how we've always been living? I never played by the rules; maybe she's tired too. But another part of me feels a sudden chill — not anger, more like that feeling from childhood of watching my mother's back disappear at the end of the street. My phone lit up, I glanced at it, that light pulled me away for a second and then yanked me back — where am I right now? This room, this person, this cup of tea that's gone completely cold. I don't know what to say. I've never been good at making promises, but I don't want that wall to actually get built.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Confusion (core emotion): her words triggered a deep uncertainty about the nature of our relationship, layered over a buried fear from childhood of watching my parents' relationship fall apart; Unease (moderate intensity): being asked to respond in this moment feels exactly like those situations where I was pushed to follow a plan — my body's instinct is to flee; A quiet sense of loss (low intensity but persistent): the dawning awareness that a kind of silent drifting apart may have already been happening between us for some time.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I grew up watching how that invisible wall slowly grew between my parents, I instinctively feel that any 'redefinition' might be the beginning of some kind of ending — but I've also never known how to use plans and promises to seal up the cracks, because those things have never worked in my hands.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'what I actually feel in this moment' matters more than any agreement — but what I can't accept is that kind of soundless disappearing act. Not separation itself, but that hollow feeling of two people living under the same roof and becoming complete strangers to each other.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to set aside any direct response for now, letting confusion and silence stand in for any real position, while getting distracted by my phone. I looked up at her, stayed quiet for a few seconds, my voice coming out a little hoarse: 'All of this you're saying… I need to think about it.' Then, almost without realizing it, I glanced down at my phone screen as it lit up.

inner_consciousness.summary: This hollow feeling… I never really recovered after seeing him off yesterday. This morning — that empty spot, the early light through the window, nothing has changed, and yet everything is different. Now he walks in, stifles a yawn, says something about each of us doing our own thing, or maybe going out for a walk — his voice trailing off, smaller and smaller, like he doesn't know what to do either. I look at him and suddenly I feel like laughing, and also like crying. Two people sitting here, neither of us knowing what comes next. I think of those afternoons years ago, standing alone on the balcony in a daze — that floating, unmoored feeling. I've always known it pretty well, actually. Blankness isn't a stranger to me; I've found so much in it, so much inspiration. But this blankness is different. It carries a very specific kind of absence. When he says 'each of us do our own thing,' I know that's a well-meaning escape hatch — but I don't want today to just dissolve like that, both of us drifting back to pretending everything's fine. This strange, in-between moment — maybe it's exactly the kind of opening where something new begins. Going with the feeling. And the feeling right now is: I want to stay with him. Even if neither of us says a word.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: A wistful ache threaded through with a small, warm impulse (intensity level 2–3). The empty nest has stirred a long-buried loneliness, faintly echoing those childhood moments of being forgotten, overlooked; but at the same time, this real, actual person sitting across from me has sparked an instinctive pull — to move closer, not to let us scatter apart.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many times when 'each of us going our separate ways' left an even deeper hollow afterward, I know instinctively: if we retreat to our own corners today, this strange feeling won't dissolve — it'll only harden. This moment right now is the opening. I can't let it slip away.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'what I'm feeling right now' is more real, more important, than any plan. What I can't accept is this: on a morning that's already this strange, we pretend everything is normal, each of us retreating back into our own shell, letting this unfamiliarity calcify just like that.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to break the silence and reach toward him — not let this morning dissolve into each of us going our separate ways. I speak up: 'Let's go out for a walk. I don't really know what else to do either — but not knowing together seems a little better than not knowing apart.'

inner_consciousness.summary: The phone screen lights up, then goes dark — those numbers and indicators floating in front of me like something I don't want to admit is real. I stare at it, and there's something sinking in my stomach. The family photos are right there beside it, my kid's face, my partner's face — if I say something now, what would their expressions turn into? That worry, that careful tiptoeing around me — just imagining it makes it hard to breathe. I've seen that atmosphere before, that suffocating heaviness in the living room when I was little, everyone wound tight, holding themselves together — that's what I'm most afraid of. But then the other side pushes up too: all those years of waiting until the last possible moment, missing the best window over and over again — that wasn't without a cost, I know that. This time is different. This time it's my body, not a project, not a deadline, not someone else's expectations. This time it's me. And still I want to wait — wait for the report, wait until it's confirmed before saying anything. It's not hiding, it's just… not the right time yet. But that voice keeps saying: you're making excuses again. My heart is beating a little fast. The study light is very still. I flip the phone face-down and press it against the desk.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety (stemming from fear of an unknown health risk, and from anticipating my family's worried reaction); Avoidance impulse (rooted in the childhood instinct to disappear in the middle of arguments and suffocating pressure); A low undercurrent of self-reproach (memories of the price paid over the years for procrastinating and evading surfacing quietly in this moment). Intensity: medium-high — not a breakdown, but a heaviness that's being held down, impossible to fully ignore.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because my whole life I've waited until things felt right before acting, waited for inspiration before starting — but this time what my 'gut' is telling me isn't a creative impulse, it's genuine fear — and I'm still instinctively trying to press it down first, keep it from disrupting the current rhythm, just like every other time I've faced a reality that felt like it was closing in on me.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: My feelings in the moment are my compass — but this time the feeling itself is the threat, and I can't push it aside the way I would a deadline; what I absolutely cannot bear is letting that oppressive, everyone-wound-tight atmosphere come back into my home — and yet I'm starting to realize that the cost of staying silent this time might be me.

pairs.final_decision: Choosing to keep it to myself for now, but already wavering inside — deciding to go for a follow-up check alone first, and only telling the family once there's an official result. I flip the phone face-down on the desk, open the hospital app to look up the nearest available appointment for a follow-up, and murmur quietly: 'I'll say something once I know for sure.'

inner_consciousness.summary: She sat down, and in her eyes there was worry, tenderness, and something I couldn't pretend not to see — she was waiting for me to give her an answer, a steady, well-planned answer. I'd been staring at that email for god knows how long, my advisor's name glowing on the screen, that research direction, all those ideas I'd once scrawled in a notebook late at night — suddenly they were all alive again. I felt something loosen in my chest, like a window that hadn't been opened in a very long time. But she was sitting right beside me, and I heard her say 'stability,' 'familiarity,' 'it affects our life together' — each word landing with its own weight. I thought about those years of being chased down for deadlines, of nights when a business partner slammed the door and walked out, that suffocating feeling — but I also thought about the moment I found inspiration in an abandoned theater, about the feeling of being alive when the code finally ran at three in the morning. This job I have now, I can get through every day just fine — but so what? Getting through it and actually living are two different things. She asked whether I'd thought about how to make it work, and I knew she wanted a plan, a spreadsheet, a timeline — but the only thing rising up inside me was this: that email is real, this feeling is real, and I can't let it sit on the shelf any longer.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Longing and a sense of pressure intertwined (a long-lost passion reawakened by that email, pulling against the guilt and weight of my partner's worry); a faint undercurrent of fear (afraid of being ground down by rules and reality all over again, and equally afraid of missing this chance); a stubborn, quiet certainty (when the feeling arrives, it arrives). Intensity: medium-high — emotionally dense but not unraveling, more like a string being plucked.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've paid the price too many times for pushing inspiration down and strangling the feeling — all those lifeless lines of code, the partnerships that fell apart, the hollow ceiling stared at in the small hours of the morning — those were the cost of not following what I felt; so now that this long-absent passion has risen up in me for real, I know instinctively: if I push it back down one more time, it's truly over.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, living inside a state of flow matters more than living safely — that feeling of being genuinely lit up by something is the only thing I'm willing to give myself over to; what I cannot accept is continuing, in the name of 'familiarity,' to spend my days getting through everything smoothly while feeling absolutely nothing.

pairs.final_decision: I'm honest about my longing for this academic opportunity, while acknowledging that I don't have a concrete plan yet — but I make it clear that I want to try. I say to her: 'I know you're worried, and I don't have all the answers — but that email made me feel something I haven't felt in a very long time. I want to write back to my advisor and tell him I'm interested. Let's just start with a conversation.'

inner_consciousness.summary: That message is just sitting there, the screen still lit — "haven't felt this relaxed in so long" — I've read it three times, and each time something loosens in my chest. When was the last time someone listened to me like that? I can't remember. Three nights ago in that hotel lobby, there was a light in her eyes, she kept asking "and then what happened?" — that feeling of being truly seen was like water finally seeping into ground that had been parched for years. Then my phone buzzed again, another message — tutoring for the kid, overtime on the weekend, like an itemized bill. Over a decade now, every conversation between us has been like that: a task list, not a conversation. I stare at those two messages and feel something tearing inside me. It's not that I don't sense the danger — I know exactly where this road leads. But that feeling of being caught, of being held… I haven't had that in so long. My fingers are hovering, not pressing down, not pulling back either. I don't want to pretend that message doesn't exist, don't want to close it and act like nothing happened. But I also know that if I reply, that warmth will keep going, and it'll get harder and harder to pull back. And yet — just this once, just tonight, I only want to respond to that feeling of being seen, even if it's just a few words.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: A moved, aching longing (from a long emotional void being unexpectedly filled); a faint dread (from the dim awareness that a boundary is beginning to blur); a dull ache about the state of the marriage (the warmth that has drained away over more than a decade); an impulse that can't be fully suppressed (the instinct to follow feeling — and here is no exception). Intensity: high, especially when the longing and the dread twist together.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I have followed my feelings my whole life, and right now this feeling is real, it's alive — that message makes me feel like I still exist; pushing it down feels like sealing off the last breath I have left.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: Being truly seen, being truly heard — that matters more to me than any plan or rule; what I cannot accept is living as someone who is nothing but function, no warmth left — even if the cost is chaos.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to reply to that message, in a tone that is light but genuine — not crossing any line, but not pretending to be indifferent either. I write back: "Made it home. I kept thinking about what you said during the meeting today. That bookstore sounds very quiet — we should go sometime. Good night."

inner_consciousness.summary: The sound of that proposal sliding across to me — paper scraping against the tabletop — made my stomach clench. The way his fingers drummed on the cover — I've seen too many people like that, that air of absolute certainty that they hold all the cards. When he called out my name in the all-hands meeting, the silence that fell over the whole room is still ringing in my ears; that feeling of being put on trial in public — I've known it since I was a kid, it's always the same, the moment I do something different, someone steps up to say it won't fly. I held it together that time, I actually held it together. But right now it's just the two of us, and he says 'this isn't a conversation about whether changes can be made' — something in that line made my palms start to sweat. I trained those new people myself, I built those workflows with my own hands — not because I love rules, but because there's something in them I genuinely understand. He hasn't even looked at any of it. He just came here to make an announcement. I think of those nights I got kicked off projects, the partners who slammed doors on their way out, the voices saying 'you've been holding everyone back.' I don't want to be swept out the door again. But I also can't pretend this proposal is right. Maybe… maybe I can go along with it for now and find the cracks later.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Suppressed anger (triggered by being publicly overruled, reactivating a childhood accumulation of blame and long-term suppression by authority): the denial in that all-hands meeting lit a fuse that goes all the way back; Defensive surface calm (the repeated lesson of being kicked off teams has trained me to instinctively stabilize the situation first): I know better than to blow up right now; Quiet indignation (this is my territory, my process): I can't just let it disappear without a fight. Intensity: medium-high — the anger is buried just beneath the surface, not fully erupting.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because time and again I've been completely shut out for pushing back head-on, I know instinctively that blowing up right here and now will only make me lose more thoroughly — but I also can't play completely dead, because those workflows hold something I genuinely understand, and I have to find a crack to keep that alive.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What truly matters to me is whether that thing is still there — the living logic inside the processes I built, not dead rules. What I cannot accept is being publicly sentenced by someone who hasn't even gotten a grip on the situation, and then being expected to nod along.

pairs.final_decision: Accept the overall direction on the surface, while fighting for a voice at the implementation level — trading 'I'll oversee the transition' for real room to preserve the process details that matter. I speak up: "Fine, I have no issue with the overall direction. But if I'm the one overseeing the transition, then I need to break this down according to my own understanding of how these workflows operate. I'll hold the pace you want — but when it comes to the specifics of what gets changed, I need you to give me the space to do that."

inner_consciousness.summary: The amusement park! Go-karts! Rock climbing! The moment Mom said it, something inside me just lit up like a spark — my cousin specifically asked for me, and we'd already promised each other a race last time! My whole body was practically bouncing… and then, out of nowhere, the weight of those keys in my backpack floated up into my mind. The mimosa plant. The tadpoles. The image of my homeroom teacher handing me the keys in front of the whole class, the feel of that little cloth pouch, the moment I nodded. My heartbeat stumbled. I know that feeling — it's like the fallen leaves in Grandpa's courtyard, the time I lay in the grass and forgot to sweep them up, and that heavy stone sat in my chest for a long, long time afterward. I don't want to feel that again. But rock climbing — go-karts — … My brain kicked into overdrive. Wait. The amusement park leaves at nine. What if I go early — head to school around seven, water the plants, feed the tadpoles, then rush over — that should still work, right? It's not like I have to pick one or the other. I can have both. Go with the feeling — but this time, the feeling is also telling me: I can't let those keys down.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Excitement (the pull of the amusement park instantly ignites an immediate, visceral craving) and guilt (the fallen leaves in Grandpa's courtyard, the weight of being trusted with those keys in front of everyone — the ache of breaking a promise is all too familiar to me) are tangled together, with an undercurrent of stubborn refusal to give up either one. Intensity: medium-high — the excitement is instinctive, the guilt is triggered by memory, and both feel completely real.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know that hollow, stuck feeling after breaking a promise will follow me around for a long time — like those leaves in Grandpa's courtyard that just won't go away — I instinctively don't want anything to happen to the mimosa or the tadpoles. But I also absolutely refuse to miss out on rock climbing. So I have to find a way to fit both things into the same day.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'going with the feeling' doesn't mean I get to ignore promises I've made to other people — that guilt of letting someone down is harder to live with than any rule. But at the same time, I can't accept just throwing away an exciting moment for nothing. So 'having it all' is my real answer — not quietly picking one and moving on.

pairs.final_decision: I decide to do both — wake up early, go to school first to take care of my responsibilities, then head to the amusement park. I tell Mom: 'I'm in! But I have to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — the teacher gave me the keys, and I made a promise. I'll go early and be back early. If I leave at seven-thirty, I should make it in time!'

inner_consciousness.summary: Empty-handed — those two words hit me like a brick to the chest. I watched his lips trembling, watched the tears threatening to spill over his eyelids, and my mind turned into total chaos. The model was gone — that model I'd spent an entire week on, carried entirely on my own — just vanished on a bus. My heart was pounding, and the fluorescent lights backstage were making my eyes sting. He was begging me, begging me to lie. A flood of things flashed through my head all at once — Mom saying I'm always too careless, teachers saying I never follow the rules, all those moments of being misunderstood and blamed crashing over me one after another. But his mom was sitting right there in the third row… It's not like I've never been hit before; I know what that feels like. But the homeroom teacher was already standing right here, eyes locked on his empty hands. I can't lie — not because of some grand moral principle, but because the moment I open my mouth and lie, this whole thing becomes my problem, my mess to clean up. And besides, even without the model, we can still present — my mind was already spinning: no physical prop, fine, then I'll bring that solar system to life with words alone, make it more vivid than any model ever could. I know this feeling well; getting backed into a corner somehow flips a switch in me. I don't want to carry his lie for him, but I'm not about to throw him under the bus right in front of the homeroom teacher either.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety (the frantic disorientation of being suddenly thrown into a crisis, layered over countless memories of other people derailing my plans); Instinctive resistance (a rejection of lying — not out of moral fastidiousness, but because I refuse to let someone else's mess get dumped on me); Strange excitement (being pushed to the edge somehow triggers a kind of improvisational impulse — intensity moderate to high)
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been through too many situations where I carried everything alone and still ended up getting blamed, I instinctively refuse to carry his lie for him — but at the same time, the very fact that the model is gone has lit something up in me. I trust my in-the-moment instincts and my ability to improvise my way through this; that gives me more confidence than any prepared plan ever could.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not letting someone else's mistake become my shackle; what I absolutely cannot accept is turning myself into a liar just to protect someone else, and then having to stand up on that stage and pretend everything is fine.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to go along with the lie, but I also don't throw my teammate under the bus on the spot — instead, I steer the conversation directly toward how to handle the presentation. I turn to the homeroom teacher and say, calmly but quickly: 'The model had an accident on the way here and didn't make it, but we can go up and present without it — I have all the content memorized.'

decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择配合组员撒谎，并决定即兴完成展示。我把他挡在身后，迎着班主任说：“老师，模型昨晚被值日生碰坏了，但没关系，我们直接脱稿讲。”

inner_consciousness.summary: First it was that red suitcase that stung me — like the hollow feeling at an amusement park when you suddenly can't find anyone, a cold that shot up from the soles of my feet. The shattered vase was so bright it hurt my eyes, and my mind had room for only one thought: don't make me choose, don't stuff me into your grown-up war. But they were both staring at me, demanding I pick a side right now, and the longer I listened the more lost I felt, the more I wanted to run. Every time they fought before, screaming didn't help, drawing didn't help — in the end I always ended up curling back into myself alone. It's the same now. I don't believe going with either of them will make anything better. I just want to slip out of this air that's about to explode, away from their hands, away from 'you're the only one' and 'you stay here.'
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear, panic, resistance, disappointment. Fear — rooted in memories of hearing grown-ups fight over and over and of being terrified of being left behind, intensity very high; resistance — rooted in how much I've always hated being forced to pick a side by someone else's rules.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I learned too early that when grown-ups have lost their minds fighting, they can't hear me at all — anything I say just gets blown away like it was never there. And because I've lived through being abandoned and being pulled apart, my gut tells me this isn't a question with a right answer; escaping first is the only way to keep from being torn to pieces.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me right now is protecting myself — not getting sucked into their out-of-control emotions. The one thing I absolutely cannot accept is being used as a patch for someone else's wound, dragged out to prove who's more pitiful, who needs me more.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to take either side — I'm going to flee the scene first. I'll wrench my hands free from theirs, cry out 'I don't want to choose,' and then run back to my room and shut the door.

inner_consciousness.summary: The teacher's hand landed on my shoulder — warm, solid — and then forty-something pairs of eyes all swung toward me at once. My heartbeat lurched, and my face started burning. I'd only been murmuring to my seatmate, just a thought, the kind that bubbles up out of nowhere, and I hadn't expected to get caught. The teacher said "even more clever than what I had in mind," and something in my chest loosened a little at that — a small warmth — but at the same time something else was sinking. I thought of that time I stood at the front of the room showing off the robot, the laughter, that feeling of being stared at but only to be mocked, and my palms started to sweat. But this was different — the teacher's eyes were bright, not the kind that come with a frown. Someone in the back row muttered "what's the big deal," and that landed like a tiny splinter, but I didn't let myself dwell on it. Standing up to speak — the idea itself didn't scare me. It was the words "speech competition" that made it hard to breathe for a second. That's a plan, that's a deadline, that's someone else's rulebook. But this idea is right here in my head, alive, it just surfaced a moment ago, and I want to say it, I want to get it out — it feels like that paper airplane I'd been hiding in my backpack, and someone finally said "let's see it."
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Excitement and nervousness intertwined (the warmth of being recognized sparked a desire to express myself, but the crowd's gaze triggered memories of past mockery, producing a mild physical tension); slight wariness (the phrase "speech competition" triggered an instinctive resistance to plans and rules, moderate in intensity).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through the experience of bringing out something I was proudest of only to have people laugh and call it "a tin man from the junk pile," I know instinctively that I can only speak an idea well when it's still alive, still warm — right now is that moment. But the competition is a future thing, and future things can wait.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: This idea, right now, is more real to me than any plan. I'm willing to stand up and say it, because the feeling is right. But that "competition next month" sounds like someone wants to stuff my inspiration into a box and drill it over and over — I don't know about that. I'm not agreeing to it yet.

pairs.final_decision: I chose to stand up and share my idea in front of everyone, but gave a vague, noncommittal response to the suggestion about the speech competition. I stood up and walked through my idea — the seesaw-and-elephant weighing method — and the words came easier and easier as I went. When the teacher brought up the competition at the end, I just smiled and said: "I'll think about it."

inner_consciousness.summary: The security guard's voice came down like a hammer, and my head started buzzing, my legs going soft beneath me. That Optimus Prime was sitting right there in my bag — I never took it, I know I didn't — but now everyone was staring at me, and that feeling of being watched by a whole roomful of adults… my face was burning, my ears were burning, something was lodged in my throat like I couldn't swallow. I turned to look at my friend — his face had gone white as paper, his lips were trembling, and the hand tugging at the corner of my jacket was slick with sweat. He leaned in close and the words he whispered hit me one by one, like nails being driven in: "My dad's going to send me back to the countryside… please… I'm begging you…" My heartbeat went ragged. I thought about all those times an adult had jabbed a finger in my face and yelled at me — that feeling of being pinned down, of not being able to get a word in edgewise, of the more you explain the worse it gets — I knew that feeling too well. But if he got sent away, that would be it. Gone. We'd done so much together, and he was genuinely terrified. Something inside me came loose — not because I thought taking the blame was right, but because in that moment his tears felt more real than any argument I could make. The security guard looked up and fixed his eyes on me, and I felt something pushing me forward. The voice in my head saying "just tell the truth" was still there — but it kept getting smaller and smaller, until finally it was swallowed up by the sound of my friend's shaking voice.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Panic (suddenly pulled into an unfamiliar, threatening situation; body in full stress response): heart pounding, legs weak, mind buzzing; Helplessness (wanting to explain but not knowing where to start, weighed down by too many memories of explanations that never worked): the words won't come; Protectiveness and impulse (my friend's tears triggered an overwhelming urge to shield him, overriding my own instinct for self-preservation): his fear eclipsed my own; A quiet, simmering sense of injustice (this wasn't me — and yet I'm the one who has to carry it — but that feeling is being drowned out by the emotional surge of the moment): buried under everything else.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know all too well what it feels like to be grabbed by an adult and talked over until no one's listening anymore — that despair — I couldn't bring myself to let my friend go through being shipped off to the countryside, losing everything. His fear felt more real and more urgent than my own sense of grievance.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: In this moment, my friend's tears matter more than any rule. I can't stand by and watch him get taken away over this — that kind of loss would hurt far worse than getting yelled at.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to take the blame for my friend, admitting that I 'wasn't thinking' when I took the toy. Head down, I tell the security guard: "I took it. I don't even know how it ended up in there. I'm sorry."
