inner_consciousness.summary: This paper is in my hands, heart pounding like crazy — kind of like that time I took apart the radio when I was little, that feeling of touching something I shouldn't, right there in front of me anyway. Mom's stone-cold face floats up again: "if you don't do well, forget about going out this summer" — those words stick in me like a nail. Math has always been like this for me, it's not that I don't try, my brain just doesn't get along with those formulas. The paper is right here, ready-made, nobody's watching, the hallway is so quiet I can hear my own heartbeat… But then Xin pokes his head out from behind the door, eyes lighting up, already ready to copy. Something feels off inside me — not because he wants to copy, but because of that line he said, "no one's gonna know anyway" — I know that feeling way too well. Every time I got caught by a teacher, got yelled at by my dad, got hit with "why are you always like this" — it always started with "no one's gonna know anyway." It's not that I'm scared of rules, I've never been scared of rules, but I'm scared of that feeling of getting dragged out into the open, everyone's eyes on you all at once. And besides, this paper belongs to the class rep — it didn't just appear out of nowhere — if the scores come back looking off, the teacher will clock it immediately. I don't want to go down that road, not like this — this isn't my thing, there's no point in using it, and even if I get through this time, what about next time? I tell Xin we can't copy from this, but I don't say anything about turning it in to the teacher either — I just slip it back into the crack under the desk leg, let it stay where it belongs.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Conflict and instinctive wariness (moderate intensity): anxiety and temptation stirred up by my mom's pressure, held in check by the memory of being dragged out into the open so many times before, and ultimately a gut-level rejection of "winning with someone else's stuff" — not driven by any sense of morality, but by that instinct of "this isn't my way."
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been through that feeling too many times — everyone staring at me, getting hit with "why are you always like this" — I know instinctively that using this paper will only make things worse, and even if I win, it won't be a real win.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, what's real is going with what feels true — a score earned off someone else's answers isn't mine, that feeling is hollow; what I can't stand is the shame of being exposed, of everyone staring at you — that's worse than doing badly on a test.

Getting caught would be way worse than just bombing the test. You head out first, I'll be there once I finish sweeping up.'

inner_consciousness.summary: My friend's voice is still echoing in my ears — 'core member,' 'key role,' 'confirm as soon as possible' — those words blew through like a gust of wind, sending the stack of financial spreadsheets on my desk rustling. I looked down at all those numbers and felt something inside me start to tighten. Over a decade — all those meetings, all those deadlines, all those suffocating standard procedures… but I also know that's the reason I can pay rent right now. And yet the field my friend is talking about — I've been steeped in it for years. Those side projects in my spare time, those ideas that suddenly surface at two in the morning — that's where I actually feel alive. I think about those nights in abandoned theaters, on rooftops, in old server rooms — that feeling when the flow state hits and the whole world just disappears. Now this opportunity is right in front of me, and it's real, not some little project I'm quietly tinkering with on my own. But the financial spreadsheet is still sitting there, and those numbers are cold. 'Confirm as soon as possible' — that phrase makes me instinctively want to take a step back, not because I don't want to go, but because someone is rushing me, setting a deadline for me — and that feeling is just as wrong in my body as being pushed to follow someone else's plan all those years. But this time is different. This is something I actually want… right?
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Excitement and anxiety intertwined (passion ignited by a real opportunity, layered over a genuine fear of financial instability); instinctive resistance ('confirm as soon as possible' triggers a deep-seated aversion to being rushed and managed); longing (years of amateur work finally have an outlet — the call of the flow state drowns out reason).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Every time in my life I've truly felt alive, it's been when there was no plan, when I was just following my gut — so I instinctively trust that the direction that makes my heart race is the right direction. The financial spreadsheet is a rule from someone else's game, not my compass.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, that flow state — that feeling of being completely, wholly absorbed — is more real and more worth having than a steady paycheck or what other people call 'security.' What I cannot accept is spending the rest of my years grinding away somewhere that doesn't move me, no matter how much it pays.

pairs.final_decision: The impulse called forth by the flow state outweighed the gravity of the financial spreadsheet, and I chose to accept the invitation — but I tried to carve out a little breathing room for myself rather than committing fully on the spot. I told my friend: 'Honestly, I already know what I want — I'm in. But can you give me a week to wrap things up on this end?'

inner_consciousness.summary: The envelope was in my hands, and then the door opened — in that split second I caught the expression that flickered across his face, like someone who'd just been caught. My heart lurched, my palms went a little damp. These past few weeks he's been spacing out, not eating, answering me like his mind is somewhere else entirely — I figured it was work, but now this piece of paper… I didn't want to look closely, but I did. There's a voice in my head saying don't push it, put the envelope back, pretend nothing happened — the way I used to shrink into a corner as a kid when my parents fought, making myself invisible — that feeling is so familiar it makes me sick. But he's standing right in front of me now, reaching out to take it back, his eyes drifting. What I'm most afraid of isn't the confrontation; it's that feeling of being kept in the dark, of him carrying something alone that I know nothing about. He's hiding something from me, and this isn't a small thing. I'm not the kind of person who handles feelings by the book, step by step, but right now I only know one thing — I need to know what's going on with him. Not tomorrow. Now. Hand the envelope back and chat about what to have for dinner? No. I can't do that.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety and tenderness tangled together (a restlessness triggered by being kept out): underneath it, a stubborn, almost defiant impulse — rooted in those childhood memories of being ignored, of being left in the dark; intensity running fairly high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since I was little what it feels like to be left outside the loop — that sense of being invisible is suffocating; so right now I can't instinctively pretend I didn't see anything. I need him to tell me the truth, even if it makes us both uncomfortable.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me in this moment is honesty — he shouldn't be carrying this alone, and I shouldn't be kept in the dark. What I can't accept is being brushed off with 'it's nothing serious' and then the two of us pretending everything is fine while we figure out what to have for dinner tonight.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to make it clear that I've already seen the report, and I ask my partner to be straight with me — I'm not going to follow his lead and let the subject drift away. I set the envelope down gently on the table, look at him, and say: 'I saw it. Can you tell me what this means?'

inner_consciousness.summary: The child on the other side of the glass window, his profile lit up by the sunlight, that look of total absorption… I recognize it. That's real burning, not a performance for anyone's benefit. I had that feeling once too, as a kid — sitting at the piano with my eyes closed, my fingers moving beyond the reach of reason, the whole world reduced to nothing but notes. Those were the most real minutes of my entire life. The teacher came out holding that brochure, his voice both excited and solemn, and I could feel that he meant every word — the child really does have that thing. But then he said 'the window of opportunity is short, a decision needs to be made soon,' and something in my chest tightened at that. Plans, timelines, professional tracks… those words took me straight back to the years of being pushed along step by step, back to the nights when schedules were thrown to the floor, back to every voice that told me 'you'll only make a mess of things.' But this child is not me. What he has right now is something I never had back then — someone has seen him, someone is willing to open a door for him. I don't want to cage his possibilities inside my own chaos. And yet I'm afraid, too — afraid that a professional track will turn him into a machine that only knows how to practice, afraid that all those rules will slowly grind out the light in his eyes. I need to ask the child himself what he thinks first. This is his fire, not mine.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Complex stirring mixed with protective anxiety. Memories of a childhood creativity that was suppressed have been reactivated, producing intense resonance and projection; at the same time, years of paying a price for improvisation leave me both resistant to and faintly awed by 'the structured path.' Emotional intensity is medium-high; the core of it is: 'I don't want the child to repeat my chaos, but I also don't want him to lose that flame.'
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I have spent my whole life paying for my feelings and my instincts, I know instinctively how precious that burning state is — and I know how much it hurts to have it forcibly extinguished by rules. So I cannot make this decision for the child; I have to hear what he himself has to say first.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is that the flame must not be snuffed out by someone else — even under the banner of 'doing what's best for you.' What I cannot accept is this: replacing a child's own wishes with a timetable, having everything arranged for him before he's even had a chance to speak.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to commit on the spot, and instead return the decision to the child rather than letting myself be swept along by the teacher's excitement or my own fear. I say to the teacher: 'Thank you — this means a great deal to us. I'd like to speak with him alone first, hear what he thinks, and then we'll make a decision.'

inner_consciousness.summary: The kid walks in carrying a glass of milk, eyes all lit up, going on about the commendation assembly, the speech, the math competition… every word like a needle. My palms are still a little cold — I can almost still feel the crumpled note between my fingers. 'Hard work pays off' — the moment those words come out of his mouth, something slams shut in my chest. I think about how I once tampered with my dad's documents when I was little, got chewed out so badly I couldn't lift my head; I think about all those nights I got kicked off teams, got called irresponsible… but those were my own messes, my own weight to carry. But him — he's going to stand up on that stage, say those words in front of the whole school, then go compete, using a result that isn't his to knock open that door. I'm not someone who follows plans, I've never played by the rules, but I have never taken someone else's thing and passed it off as my own. What's written on that note is too clear — clear enough to make me sick. But right now he's standing in front of me, so happy, looking at me with so much trust… I can't shatter his whole world in this one second. But I also can't let him walk up onto that stage.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Shock laced with nausea (the note is too specific — there's no room for self-deception); a heavy, aching tenderness (his eyes are so bright, it cuts like a knife); a gut-level refusal — not loyalty to any rule, but an instinctive revulsion toward 'glory bought with a lie,' a revulsion that arrives earlier and fiercer than any moral lecture ever could.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I've dodged too many rules in my life, blown too many deadlines, let too many people down — but I have never dressed myself up in someone else's skin, because that's how you end up not recognizing yourself anymore. I can't let him walk up onto that stage. Not because of the rules. Because it would turn him into someone who lies even to himself.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, chaos is fine, laziness is fine — but trading something fake for real glory is something I cannot accept. That's not freedom; that's selling yourself out. His eyes are still so bright. I can't let that brightness become the shame he sits alone with in the dark someday.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to confront him directly tonight — but I also refuse to play along. Instead, I let a weighted silence and a careful question hang the whole thing in the air, leaving room for a private conversation later tonight. I take the milk from him, glance down at it for a moment, and say quietly: 'Let's set the speech aside for now — did you actually review today? That problem you said you couldn't do before — can you work through it on your own yet?'

inner_consciousness.summary: The retired teacher stood in the doorway wringing his hands, and that look of helplessness stirred up something in my chest I couldn't quite name. Two months of digging, line by line, entry by entry — those numbers are real, I know they are. But what he said is real too — construction debris, connections at the neighborhood office, old bones that can't take a fight. My wife's face was pale today, and that assistant's smug little smile is still floating in front of my eyes. It's not like I haven't thought about just letting it go — two million split up comes out to a few thousand per household — but then I think about that elevator still humming away, that mottled outer wall, those fabricated projects that never existed… something presses against my chest like a splinter I can't pull out. The neighbors are willing to help quietly, just not willing to sign their names — I get it, I do. But I also know that if it's only my name on there, I'm the target. And yet, if I just swallow this whole, how do I face myself? I've already cross-referenced the numbers. The evidence is right there. This isn't a matter of instinct or inspiration — it's real, it's something I saw with my own eyes. I'm not good at following a plan, but this whole thing, from start to finish, was something I chose to do. Nobody pushed me into it. I decided to dig. So I'll keep going. One name is one name.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Indignation (an activated sense of justice, rooted in a lifelong resistance to being suppressed by rules, now crystallized into an instinctive reaction against injustice); Loneliness (that familiar feeling of isolation — the shadow of being left behind by the crowd, again and again since childhood, rising to the surface once more); Stubbornness (an inability to stomach the idea of just swallowing it down — this obstinacy has run through his entire life); Mild fear (his wife's pale face, the assistant's smile — he knows this is no small thing). Emotional intensity: high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since I was young that the person who follows their gut is always the one who ends up alone — but I also know that once I've decided something is real, I have no way of pretending it doesn't exist. Those fabricated projects, that elevator that's never had a single part replaced — that's my evidence, that's what I know to be true, and there's no pushing it back down.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is this: I cannot deny what I have seen with my own eyes. What I cannot accept is: knowing something is true and then looking the other way out of fear of the trouble — that suffocates me more than any rule or deadline ever could.

pairs.final_decision: I've decided to file a formal complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau under my own name, as the lead complainant on record. I'll accept the neighbors' quiet assistance but won't pressure anyone to co-sign. And I'll hold no resentment toward them for stepping back — I understand it. I told the retired teacher: 'I hear everything you're saying. You don't have to sign anything. But I have to go — I'm the one who dug up the evidence, so I'll carry it there myself. If you have any materials, pass them to me. That's enough.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 决定以个人名义实名牵头向住建局举报，接受邻居私下协助但不强求联名，同时对邻居的退缩保持理解而非指责。我对退休教师说：'您说的我都明白，您不用签名，但这事我得去——证据是我查的，我自己扛着去，您要是有材料就给我，够了。'

inner_consciousness.summary: The president just walked out like that? I stared at that phone screen and felt something ignite inside me all at once — not anger exactly, but that familiar feeling of blood rushing to my head. Three people in the activity room sat with their heads down, the atmosphere like stagnant water, and that kind of silence is harder for me to bear than any actual problem. My mom's words from last night were still circling in my ears — grades, summer camp, pressure… but all of that had retreated to the background now, because right in front of me was a more real battlefield. Next Friday, the main auditorium, the whole school — teachers and students alike — an event we'd been preparing for over a month. The moment that image flashed through my mind, I knew there was no way I could say cancel. My whole life, every time someone said 'just forget it' or 'I can't hold on anymore,' I was always the one who stood up and said no. Chaos is where I have the most energy; a mess is exactly when I shine brightest. Three club members sitting in the corner — what they need isn't someone to make the decision for them, what they need is someone to set this dead, airless room on fire. I felt that surge pushing outward from my chest, like that one second right before you walk onstage — the more pressure there is, the more I want to charge forward.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Elation (dominant), mixed with a thread of abandoned anger. The president walking out triggered his old sensitivity to being 'undercut' (echoing the time a close friend questioned him after the calisthenics competition), but that anger was quickly smothered by his habitual excitement at 'winning through chaos.' The silence of the activity room drains him, while the 'mess' is actually a summons — intensity: high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been through so many moments of stepping up in the middle of chaos and turning a scattered, directionless group into something worth watching, I instinctively believe: the more you shrink back in times like this, the more you fall apart — the only way to pull people back together is to charge straight in.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'having people watching' and 'having something to do' matter more than almost anything else — an event that took over a month to prepare, meant for the whole school, just getting canceled is something I cannot accept; sitting silently in the corner waiting for someone else to clean it up is something I'm simply not capable of doing.

pairs.final_decision: I chose to take full ownership of the situation — no cancellation, no hesitation — and pulled all three club members out of their slump on the spot to redistribute the work. I set my phone down on the table, swept my eyes across all three of them, and said: 'Cancel? Not happening. The president bailed, but the event is still on. Come on — pass me that task breakdown sheet. We're redividing everything right now. Everyone picks up two items, and we start tonight.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择全力接手，不取消活动，当场把三个社员从颓废状态里拉起来重新分工。我把手机往桌上一放，扫了一圈三个人，开口说：'取消？不行。社长跑了，但活动还在。来，把那张分工表拿过来，我们现在重新分，每人认领两件事，今晚开始动。'

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment the door clicked shut, I felt a jolt in my chest at the words 'accomplice' and 'delivered to your doorstep' — but what hurt more wasn't fear, it was being forced to pick a side in that room. The back of my head was still burning from earlier, and that low, strangled threat was still ringing in my ears, yet what really got under my skin was the whole class holding their breath, nobody saying a word, and somehow I'm the one they nail out front to carry it all. That old feeling came flooding back — being misunderstood, standing there while everyone stares, wanting to explain but the words just jamming up — and the one thing I absolutely cannot stand is being treated like someone too gutless to speak up. But shrinking down and playing dumb? That's even more pathetic. I'm not taking the fall for them, and I'm not going to look like a snitch either. I need to take the room back — the words come from me, the terms are mine: I state the facts, but not because I was scared into it.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Emotional tone — Irritation, agitation, pent-up frustration, wariness. The irritation comes from being threatened by a classmate and then cornered by the dean; the frustration comes from all those times I was misunderstood, stared at, and couldn't get a word in edgewise; the agitation comes from the way pressure actually makes me want to step forward and take control of the situation; the wariness sits at a moderate level, because I know that once I say something out loud, it changes things.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I know all too well that taking the fall for someone else only ever ends with you looking bad alone — so my gut instinct is to refuse to stay silent. But I'm also not letting anyone push my head down and make me pledge loyalty on command. If I'm going to say something, it's on my own terms: I lay out the facts, and I do it standing up, not begging.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is that I don't end up holding someone else's bag, and that I don't just stand there while everything falls apart with no one stepping in. What I absolutely cannot accept is being bullied and silenced into becoming a scapegoat — and then being expected to pretend I didn't see any of it.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to tell the truth about who was wearing the badge, but I won't frame myself as anyone's follower. I say directly who I saw wearing it, and I also state plainly, right there in the room, that he just threatened me — don't count me in as an accomplice.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择模糊周旋、不正面供出任何人，同时暗示主任自己"什么都看见了"但需要"想清楚怎么说"，用拖延换取主动权。我抬起头对主任说："老师，我确实看见了一些，但那个同学出去跟我说的话我现在还有点懵，您能给我两分钟让我把事情捋清楚再说吗？我不想说错。"

选择拒绝私下告密，将冲突公开化。
“主任，我不喜欢背后说人，既然他敢当众威胁我，您让我回教室，我当着全班的面指认他。”

inner_consciousness.summary: She's just sitting there whispering and laughing with the people in the back row, like nothing ever happened — but that line I saw a moment ago is still burning in my eyes: "We never should have let her hang out with us." My hand is clenched around my phone; the screen is practically warm from my grip. I know this feeling. I know it too well — it's like being a kid carrying my lunch tray over to a table and having everyone go quiet all at once. Not by accident. On purpose. I sat at that table for five minutes and not one person looked at me. Five minutes. I thought I could hold it together, but I couldn't, and my eyes are still red right now. She saw. She knows. And all she said was "don't overthink it," then turned back around and kept laughing. That "don't overthink it" cuts deeper than the screenshot itself. What I want most right now is to confront her directly — lay out the group chat, lay out that line, put it all on the table. I'm not the kind of person who can just sit on something like this; bottling it up only makes it worse. The more people around, the more clearly things can be said — saying it out loud in front of everyone is the only way it becomes real. But… the way she's acting right now, if I open my mouth, will she just deny everything? Will she make me out to be someone who's blowing things out of proportion? My mind is racing, there's something pressing down on my chest, and I can't figure out whether to sit or stand. I can't pretend I don't know. I can't do it. That's not who I am.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Searing sense of betrayal (from the moment I saw the screenshot — the deepest kind, because this is my closest friend); suppressed rage (the five minutes of silence, the dismissive "don't overthink it," all piling on top of each other); an intense, conflicting urge to confront (an instinctive reaction — the more people present, the more I want to get it all out in the open); a faint, uncertain fear (what if she denies it, what if I end up being the one who "takes herself too seriously"). Intensity: high — especially the first two, which are almost spilling over.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since I was little that the most draining feeling in the world is being shut out behind an invisible wall while everyone expects you to act like nothing's wrong. Silence never solves anything — saying it out loud, facing it head-on, even if it turns into a fight, at least it's real. At least I'm still in the room.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, being treated like I don't exist — being kept in the dark — is harder to accept than any argument. I'd rather have it out right then and there than sit and pretend I don't know a thing. That kind of pretending is exhausting to me. It's a humiliation.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to confront her directly — to put the screenshot and the side group chat out in the open, no holding back and no dancing around it. I turn toward her, lower my voice, but speak with complete directness: "I saw that group chat. I saw what you said. Can you tell me — what exactly is going on?"

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择当面质问，把截图和小群的事直接摊开说，不压着也不绕弯子。我转过头，压低声音但语气很直接地对她说：「我看到那个群了，也看到你说的那句话——你能不能告诉我，到底是怎么回事？」
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择当面挑明，不再装不知道。我压低声音直接问她：小群和那张截图我都看到了，你现在就跟我说清楚。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择直接对峙，将私下排挤公开化。我猛地站起来，拍着桌子大声喊：“别捂嘴笑了，朋友圈截图我看到了，既然嫌我烦，现在当着全班面说清楚！”

inner_consciousness.summary: I saw them. Those marks — new ones, old ones, deep and shallow — it was like something slammed straight into my chest, my mind going completely blank and racing at the same time. She was trembling, eyes already red, and the way she yanked her sleeve back down was so fast, so practiced… My throat tightened — not from fear, but because I suddenly didn't know where I was supposed to stand. She said "you're the only one I trust," and those words hooked into the softest part of me — the thing I've always been most afraid of is being needed and then pushed away, and right now both of those things are happening at once. She wants me to promise to keep this secret, but there's a voice buzzing in my head: this isn't an ordinary secret. This isn't who likes who or what grade someone got — this is her cutting herself with a blade. I think about all those times I was shut out, misunderstood, standing alone on the school grounds, and I know what that suffocating feeling is — but I also know that if I nod today and tomorrow she has a few more marks, what do I do? I can't pretend I'm capable of carrying this by myself. I can't make that promise — but I also can't just say "I'm going to tell a teacher" and turn and walk away, because then she really will be alone. I can't let her feel like I've betrayed her, but I can't let her carry all of this by herself either. I have to stay here. I have to make sure she knows first that I haven't run.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Shock and heartache (the moment of seeing the scars, every memory of being isolated and misunderstood piles on top of each other, giving him a genuine, visceral empathy for his friend's pain); the anguish of an impossible choice (the warmth of being needed and the terror of not being able to bear it alone surge up simultaneously); an impulse not to flee (intense, rooted in his bone-deep instinct that he cannot let someone be left alone).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through that feeling of being trapped alone in a corner with no one coming, I know instinctively that the most important thing right now is to make her feel that I haven't run — but it's also precisely because I know how deep that darkness goes that I can't bring myself to promise we'll keep this between just the two of us.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is "not letting someone carry things alone" — but what I cannot accept is trading a "I promise to keep your secret" for her continuing to sink further on her own. That's not helping her. That's just sinking alongside her.

pairs.final_decision: I refuse to give a clean, clear-cut promise of secrecy, but I insist on staying by her side — no running — trying to steady her first before we figure out what to do together. I take her hand, my voice coming out a little rough: 'I'm not going anywhere. I'm right here. But I can't promise you I'll do nothing — not because I don't care about you, but because I care too much. I can't carry this alone, and you shouldn't have to either. Let's figure it out together, okay?'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 拒绝给出干净利落的保密承诺，但坚持留在她身边、不逃跑，试图先稳住她再一起想办法。我握住她的手，声音有点哑：'我不走，我就在这儿。但我没办法答应你什么都不做——不是因为我不在乎你，是因为我太在乎了，我一个人扛不住这件事，你也不应该一个人扛。我们一起想，好不好？'

decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择表面妥协稳住对方，但实质上强势介入主导局面。我反手紧紧握住ta的手腕，大声说：“好，我不告诉大人！但我绝对不让你一个人扛，从今天起我每天盯着你，我们一起把这事解决掉！”

inner_consciousness.summary: Wow, that wave of agreement in the classroom — I'd barely sat down, the warmth of my presentation still in the air, and it hit me like a bucket of cold water. Her plan? KTV and food? Sure, easy, but what's the point! I spent three nights on this — three nights! A time-tunnel concept, era-themed segments, lighting choreography — that's something I made with real care. And now my friend's pulling me into the hallway, saying the vote's too close to call, asking whether I should talk to her about merging our plans… Merging? And who gets the credit? My mind started spinning — no, wait, this isn't even about credit. These are two completely different visions. I know she's popular — basketball team, arts committee, the whole thing — so what? A good plan is a good plan. But I also know that a good plan alone won't cut it; it's people's hearts that decide the vote. I've understood that since I was little. Holding your ground in a crowd doesn't come from hiding in a corner waiting to be discovered. I've got the whole weekend — I'm going to talk to every single person, let them see the sketch with their own eyes, feel what I was imagining. I'm not trying to beat her. I want everyone to genuinely see how cool this plan is. Merge? No — that would water down the best thing I've got. Time to go win some votes. Starting now.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fighting spirit (a lit-up competitive drive, rooted in years of being overlooked and ignored growing up — every moment of recognition from a crowd is a strike back against those memories); mild indignation (the sting of watching my plan get so easily overshadowed, moderate intensity); eager, restless excitement (the more pressure, the more charged he gets — it's pure instinct for him)
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since I was little that as long as you step up, speak up, and make yourself seen, you have a chance — silence and backing down are the only real losses; merging plans means handing over the best of what I've made, and I'd rather fight for the win outright than split the credit.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is having people truly see my idea and feel how good it is — that's not vanity, that's how I exist in the world; I can't bring myself to turn something carefully crafted into a watered-down compromise that satisfies everyone and excites no one.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to campaign hard for votes and stand by my original plan — no merger. I tell my friend: 'No merging — my plan is my plan. Come on, this weekend we go talk to everyone one by one. I'm bringing the sketch. Let them see it for themselves.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择全力拉票、坚持原方案，不走合并路线。我对朋友说：'不合并，我的方案就是我的方案——走，周末我们挨个去找人，我把草图带上，让大家自己看。'

decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview:
选择拒绝合并，周末全力拉票争取支持。
“合并多没意思，拉票才好玩呢！”我用力拍了拍朋友的肩膀，立刻掏出手机开始构思周末的拉票派对计划。

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment Mom's voice came through, it was like someone had grabbed my chest and squeezed — that feeling was so familiar, so real, nothing like the tension in a boardroom. This was something else, something soft, something that pulls you downward. Dad lying in a hospital bed, fluorescent lights, the ambient noise of a ward… I can't get that image out of my head. The overseas thing is still there — the contract, the visa, that unfamiliar street I once dreamed about — but all of it has gone distant and weightless, like it belongs to someone else's life. It's not that I didn't consider having it both ways, making a few calls, coordinating remotely, but I know that would just be lying to myself — Mom's voice was trembling, and she wasn't asking for my opinion, she was waiting for me to come home. The thing I've feared most my whole life isn't conflict, isn't being questioned — it's that feeling of being cut off, of a connection snapping. If I don't go back now, that break will be real, and I'll be the one who caused it. Career opportunities can be found again, fought for again, there'll be other stages to stand on — but if I'm not there for this moment, I will never have been there, not ever. I picked up my phone, hand still a little unsteady, but I already knew who I needed to call.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety and tenderness intertwined (the catch in Mom's voice triggered a deep need for belonging and family connection, intensity extremely high); a brief sense of being torn in two (the collision between professional ambition and family responsibility, quickly overwhelmed by emotional instinct); an urge to act (the greater the pressure, the more I need to do something to break the deadlock rather than just sit here).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because my whole life I've been afraid of that feeling of being cut off, of being left on the outside — so when my family truly needs me to show up, I have no way of choosing absence. It's not a rational calculation; it's something bone-deep, instinctive. And I believe that being physically present is what makes things real — problems like this have to be handled face to face. Dealing with something like this remotely doesn't count as dealing with it, not to me.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, being present in a relationship matters more than any opportunity. If I miss a project, I can find the next one — but if I miss this moment, I'll spend the rest of my life knowing I wasn't there that day. What I cannot accept is choosing absence at the very moment I'm needed most. That kind of absence becomes a crack, and no amount of noise or company will ever fill it.

pairs.final_decision: Choose to immediately set aside all preparations for the overseas project and return home as soon as possible to be with Dad through his medical care, putting the career decision on hold for now. Call Mom and say: 'Mom, I'm booking the ticket right now — I'll be there on the earliest flight tomorrow. Don't worry, I'll handle everything.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择立刻放下海外项目的筹备，第一时间赶回家陪父亲就医，将职业决策暂时搁置。拨通电话对母亲说：'妈，我现在就订票，明天最早的航班我就到，你别担心，我来处理。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 先回医院，暂缓海外决定并主动谈折中方案。我立刻订票回家，同时给公司打电话说明情况，争取延期或改派。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择接受海外机会，通过调动金钱和医疗资源来履行家庭责任。“妈你先别慌，我马上联系最好的专家和全职护工！海外项目我必须去，但我保证用翻倍的薪水给爸顶级的治疗，我这就去安排！”

inner_consciousness.summary: The conference room lights are still on, the whole city spread out in the night beyond the glass, and they're leaning back in their chair with this light in their eyes. "These past few months working with you have been the happiest I've felt in two years." That line went through me like a current — I felt it, of course I felt it. All those moments of eye contact over these months, that obscure novel, those nights we stayed until midnight — I kept telling myself it was just professional chemistry, but now they've said it out loud, just the two of us, the conference room lights, the city at night. My heart sped up. And then I thought of the light on at home, my partner hunched over their study materials, occasionally looking up to ask "this late again," and I'd mumble something back — that guilt is heavier now. But — the bookstore, that author, just the two of us, this isn't a work dinner, this is an invitation, and I know exactly what it means. It's not that I don't know where the line is. But I also know that if I say no, this connection gets severed — the project wraps up, they disappear from my life, and that feeling of being truly understood disappears with them. What I want most right now is to say yes, but I can't pretend this is just two friends going out, I can't lie to myself like that.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Attraction and guilt intertwined — the attraction rooted in the genuine emotional resonance and the satisfaction of feeling understood over these months, the guilt rooted in an awareness of the commitment I made to my partner; alongside that, a fierce reluctance to let go and a Panic at the thought of losing this connection — intensity is high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because my energy comes from people, from that feeling of being truly seen — and for these past few months they've given me exactly that — my instinct is to not let this connection break. But I also know that accepting this invitation means I'm actively stepping over a line, rather than letting things fade on their own.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, genuine connection and being understood are the most valuable things there are — I can't accept a state where I know something is there and pretend it isn't. But I equally can't accept becoming someone who is dishonest with their partner. Right now those two things are colliding head-on.

pairs.final_decision: Accept the invitation — but also decide to have a real conversation with my partner that same night, and face whatever has actually gone wrong between us. I say to them: "I'd love to go, I've been wanting to catch this talk too — but there's something I need to tell you first, about where things have been between us lately."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 接受邀约，但同时决定当晚回家后认真和伴侣谈一次，面对这段关系里真正出了问题的地方。我对ta说：'我愿意去，这本书我也一直想听这场分享——不过我需要先跟你说一件事，关于我们最近的状态。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择拒绝单独赴约，先把这段心动停在边界内。 我会对他说我很珍惜这段搭档时光，但周六不单独去，也该回去和伴侣认真谈谈。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择顺应本能接受邀约，将情感冲动转化为继续探索关系的行动。我迎上ta的目光，嘴角压不住地上扬：“当然去，我也正觉得项目结束得太可惜了，周六下午见。”

inner_consciousness.summary: My senior slid that number in front of me — 8%, 80 million, CTO — and my blood just started boiling. This is exactly the kind of moment I've been waiting for: real people, real stakes, an audience, the whole café turned into a stage. I could feel his eyes on me, that feeling of being truly seen — more clarifying than any classroom ever was. Something in my head was spinning at full speed — my advisor's messages, my parents' faces, that pile of grad school prep books — but all those voices were too faint, muffled like they were coming through a wall. I thought about all those moments standing at a mic, about that full-to-the-brim feeling every time I stepped up and made something happen. Silence and waiting have never been my rhythm. inner_consciousness.summary: The sound of that pill bottle rolling and bumping to a stop at my feet froze me solid. I thought about those muffled sobs in the middle of the night, the pile of takeout boxes, the curtains that never once got opened — I hadn't missed any of it these past two weeks, I just hadn't said anything, and neither had anyone else. Silence is already draining for me under normal circumstances, but this silence is different — this is the kind of silence where something goes wrong. He asked me why I hadn't gone out tonight, and that was the first thing he'd said to me on his own. That's not a coincidence. That's a crack in the door — even if it's no wider than a pinhole, he's the one who left it open. I can't pretend I didn't see it and kick the bottle back. I can't do that. That's not who I am. When someone is at their lowest and they ask you something — even offhandedly — they're testing whether anyone is there. I'm here right now, just me, the dorm so quiet I can hear water dripping, and this moment isn't one I chose, but it's happening. I have to say something. I have to walk over there — not because I know what to say, but because I know I can't say nothing.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Shock and alertness (an instinctive recognition triggered by the pill bottle — the awareness that this might be a crisis signal); a fierce sense of urgency (silence is already a drain on me, but this particular silence is something I simply cannot accept); a quiet, underlying fear (not for myself — fear that something will happen to him); an impulse pushing me forward, driven by something bone-deep in me: if you're present, you connect.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because the thing I've feared most my entire life is that feeling of being isolated in a corner with no one paying attention to you — so I understand better than anyone what it's like to shrink into the dark and not be able to make a sound. He is, right now, exactly the person I've always been most afraid of becoming. There's no way I can turn away.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, the most important thing in the world is that people can open their mouths and talk to each other — silence is a waste, and more than that, it's dangerous. What I cannot accept is this: there's someone right here, I'm right here, and yet we pretend nothing is happening and leave him alone in that darkness.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to speak up and step in — using the pill bottle as the opening to walk directly toward my roommate, without flinching from this crisis signal. I keep the bottle in my hand instead of giving it back, stand up, and walk to the edge of his bed. I say quietly: 'I didn't go out tonight — good timing, I'll keep you company for a bit. This bottle — can you tell me what's going on?'

inner_consciousness.summary: This room is too quiet — so quiet I can barely breathe. The fluorescent light hums, like something is boring into my ears. The doctor is watching me, waiting for me to speak, but my mind is a mess — not the electric kind of mess, the real kind, like someone has gone in and cut every single wire. My father is sitting beside me, and that line — 'whatever you two decide is fine' — he said it so lightly, so exhausted, and I suddenly remembered what he looked like when he was young, that man who never backed down from anything, and now he can't even be bothered to choose how he dies. For twenty years I've never been afraid of silence, never afraid of confrontation, never afraid of any boardroom standoff — but right now I don't know where to put all this energy. There's no audience to carry me, no crowd to give me strength, there's only this fluorescent light and this report and my father's closed eyes. That 20% figure keeps spinning in my head — I've bet on long odds my whole life, but that was business, that was something you could lose and come back from. But this is him. These are his last days. He said he's tired, and that look in his eyes was real — not giving up, just genuinely worn out. If I push him into chemo, is it for him, or is it because I can't bring myself to accept the outcome? I can't perform strength in this room. There's no crowd to light me up. There's only him, only this decision.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Heaviness and the edge of losing control (born from a vacuum that social energy cannot fill); deep tenderness and helplessness (his father's exhausted eyes have touched a vulnerability he has never let himself face); an undercurrent of fear (in a room with no audience, no applause, no rebound, his instincts have failed him). Intensity: extremely high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've seen too many people hold on under pressure, and what finally breaks them isn't the illness — it's the feeling of having their dignity stripped away. And that look in my father's eyes isn't fear; he is genuinely worn out. I have no right to make him bear the suffering inside that 20% chance just to satisfy my own unwillingness to let go.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: The things I've feared most my whole life are silence and endings — but in this moment I know that forcing him to fight a battle he can't win is only about making myself feel better. Whatever time he has left should belong to him, not to me.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to honor my father's exhaustion — palliative care, letting go of that 20% chance. I look at the doctor, my voice a little hoarse, and say: 'We'll go with comfort care. Let him go with some dignity.'

inner_consciousness.summary: Her words land, and I'm standing there with the dish towel still balled up in my hand, like something just knocked the wind out of me. The setting sun has turned the living room orange — a color that's supposed to feel warm, but right now all I feel is this strange burning heat — not anger, it's that heat you get when someone says something to your face that you weren't ready to hear. 'Flexibility.' 'Separate emotional lives.' These words are spinning around in my head and I'm trying to grab onto them but I can't, like water slipping through my fingers. My phone screen lit up for a second, I glanced at it and looked away — not now, not this moment. I need to say something, I need to hold this scene together, because silence right now is the worst possible thing, it'll crush us both. But I can also feel something tightening in my chest — not anger, it's that feeling I've known since I was a kid — being told that something you thought was solid isn't actually that solid. I don't want to run, I never run, conflict is real, it's something you can talk through, but this… this isn't ordinary conflict, this is her telling me that something inside her has already come loose. I have to speak, I have to crack this open, in this room, right now.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Shock and unease (triggered by a familiar relationship suddenly turning strange, layered over old wounds of childhood rejection and being pushed to the margins); a powerful urge to act (silence drains him — the more pressure he feels, the more he needs to speak); hidden vulnerability (the loosening of the relationship cuts straight to his deepest need for belonging); excitement tangled with fear (he wants to turn this scene into a negotiable conversation, but at the same time he's terrified that talking will only make things more out of control). Intensity: high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because the thing I've feared most my whole life isn't conflict — it's that feeling of being slowly pushed out in the silence — so my instinct is to drag this onto the table and deal with it face to face, right now, I can't let it just hang in the air and harden into a wall between us.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'being together' isn't a concept you can make flexible — a relationship is either real or it isn't — but more than that, what I can't accept is that she sat with this alone for a long time without telling me. That's the real isolation.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to speak up immediately, to turn this into a direct, face-to-face confrontation, refusing silence and delay. I set down the dish towel and look straight at her: 'You said you've been thinking about this for a long time — well, I want to know now, when did you start thinking about it, because when did there get to be so much between us that you weren't telling me?'

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment my phone screen lit up, my hands started shaking. The person in that photo was standing in their own studio, smiling so genuinely, surrounded by awards and finished work — that was exactly the life we'd sketched out together in a café twenty years ago, the path I'd pointed at and said 'I want that too' but never walked down. The engine was still humming, the traffic light cycled through its colors right in front of me, and I couldn't see any of it. That afternoon in the café came flooding back — we talked about living differently, I said 'I want that too,' and then I chose safety. Twenty years: promotions, family, a house, everything going according to plan — so why does all of it feel, right now, like an elaborately staged lie? I'm not used to this kind of silence, not used to sitting alone in a car and being swallowed by something like this. I need to say it out loud, I need someone to hear it, I need to turn this thing that's about to crack my chest open into actual words. My family is waiting for me, there are things to handle tomorrow, a deadline next week — all of that is real, but right now it sounds like distant noise. I can't just drive home and pretend everything is fine. That hollow feeling would suffocate me. I have to make a call, I have to find someone to talk to — even if I can't explain it clearly, even if saying it out loud won't fix anything — because the silence is worse than any of this.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Hollowness and shock (core emotion): twenty years of choices pierced through by a single photograph, that 'I want that too' moment resurfacing and bringing with it a hollowness he has never felt before; Restless agitation: solitude and silence drain him, and the loneliness of being trapped in this car is becoming increasingly unbearable — intensity extremely high; A low undercurrent of anger and resentment: not really directed at anyone else, but more at the version of himself who chose safety back then.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because my whole life I've drawn energy from people, from voices, from the moment something is spoken aloud — silence only makes this hollow feeling grow. I know it instinctively: I have to say this to someone, if only to let it leave my chest.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, connection and speaking out loud matter more than digesting things alone. I can't accept the idea of pressing this tearing feeling down by myself, driving home, sitting at the dinner table, and acting like nothing happened — that kind of silence is the real lie.

pairs.final_decision: He chooses to break the silence and reach out rather than suppress it alone or drive home and pretend everything is normal. He dials an old friend's number, his voice a little rough: 'I'm stuck at a red light right now — there's something I've been sitting on for a long time. Do you have a few minutes to talk?'

inner_consciousness.summary: Every time my phone lights up, my chest tightens right along with it. The hardest part isn't the possible result — it's how quiet this room is, quiet enough to feel like something is pressing me down into this chair alone. My instinct is to say nothing for now: wait for the follow-up, wait for confirmation, wait until I've got the situation sorted out — don't let the whole household explode at once, don't make everyone start tiptoeing around me with those careful, worried eyes. But I also know that the longer you drag things out, the less it feels like control, and in the end you just end up pushing people further away. All those moments I thought I could handle on my own, thought I could manage in real time — they all left cracks afterward. Family aren't clients, and they aren't a team to be managed; they're not there for me to keep the atmosphere calm. I don't want to cause a panic, but I can't keep them locked outside the door either. What finally pushed me over the line was this sudden realization: compared to the temporary chaos of telling them now, I couldn't bear the thought of them finding out later that I'd seen this coming all along and said nothing.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Oppression, unease, false bravado, and longing. The oppression comes from facing the unknown alone in the silence; the unease comes from all those past moments when I thought I had things under control and nearly lost them anyway; the false bravado is my old habit of shouldering everything myself first; and the longing — the thought of my family being kept in the dark — runs very deep.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I'm so used to steadying the room first and steering the conversation away, my instinct is to wait until things are confirmed before saying anything. But I've lived through too many moments of going it alone and ending up with relationships that hurt more for it — so this time I trust one thing more than anything else: the people who matter need to be brought in early, to face it together.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters more to me isn't keeping the surface calm — it's making sure we're still standing on the same side. I can live with a moment of panic. What I can't live with is using 'I'm doing this for you' as the reason I kept my family on the other side of the truth.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to tell my family as soon as possible, but I'll hold the rhythm steady — I won't frame it as a catastrophe. Tonight I'll bring my partner in, be honest that the doctor has asked for a follow-up and that I'm worried there may be something to it, and we'll go together tomorrow to find out for sure.

选择打破沉默，向家人坦白健康风险。

inner_consciousness.summary: She came and sat beside me, and the worried tenderness in her eyes made my chest tighten. I've been staring at this email for I don't know how long — those words feel like they're pulling me up from somewhere very, very deep. A researcher position, that field, something I genuinely burned for once. I remember the night I was sorting through old notebooks, hands trembling as I turned to those pages, heart beating strangely — not from excitement, but from a kind of ache I hadn't felt in years. She talks about stability and familiarity, and I get it, I really do — but stability has never been a source of energy for me. Stability is the thing that slowly drains me dry. This job I have now — every day is the same familiar routine, no one needs me to ignite anything, no new conversations, no real collision of ideas — and for me, that's harder to bear than loneliness. Academia? That's a different kind of people, a different kind of friction. Just imagine arguing, debating, sparking something with a room full of people who actually care about that field… my blood runs hot just thinking about it. But she's worried — she genuinely is — and I can't treat this like some solo adventure I push forward on my own. I need her to see what's in my eyes: not running away, but a real desire to come back to life.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Longing and suppression intertwined (a long-dormant passion reignited, triggered by old notebooks and a mentor's email); mild anxiety (fear that my partner's worry will become an obstacle, rooted in how much I care about the stability of our relationship); a quiet undercurrent of exhilaration (imagining the intellectual sparring and human connection of a new environment). Intensity: medium-high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many nights using busyness and noise to cover up the hollow feeling inside, I know instinctively that what's in that email isn't an impulse — it's a genuine, long-overdue signal that something is recharging in me. I can't keep pretending that a familiar track is the life I actually want.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, the feeling of truly being alive means having people to clash with and something worth burning for — and that matters more than any kind of stability. What I cannot accept is repeating the same motions every day until I can no longer feel myself at all.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to honestly express how deeply I want this academic opportunity, while actively inviting my partner to work through the possibilities together, rather than pushing ahead on my own. I turn toward her, tilt the laptop screen so she can see it, and say: 'I'm not running away — I just haven't felt like this in a really long time. Can you let me tell you what this opportunity means to me? And then we can figure out what to do together.'

inner_consciousness.summary: The phone screen is lit up, and that message is just sitting there. I stare at it, something rising in my chest — not the kind of high I get standing on a stage with everyone watching, but something else, quieter, hotter. Three days ago in the hotel lobby, when she talked there was a light in her eyes, she was actually listening to me — not waiting for me to finish so she could have her turn, actually listening. When was the last time someone heard me like that? I can't remember. More than a decade, and the person at home asks "how was the trip," I say "fine, good," and then we both scroll our phones, like two strangers sharing a room. It's not like I never tried — but that still surface of water, every time I jumped in I just got absorbed, not even a ripple. And now this message — she remembered the point I made, remembered the bookshop I mentioned, she said "I haven't felt this relaxed in so long." Me neither. Then my partner's message pops up — tutoring, overtime, that familiar cold, like air drifting out of a refrigerator. My finger hovers. I know this line is thin, I know. But I also know that silence is something that drains me, and she gave me a feeling I haven't had in a long time — being seen. I can't pretend nothing happened.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: The longing to be seen (intense): accumulated from being ignored and excluded in childhood, and from the long absence of emotional connection in his marriage; Excitement and a sense of danger coexisting (moderate): this new emotional connection has activated his habitual instinct to draw energy from other people, yet he dimly senses the boundary; A baseline of loneliness (a continuous low hum): the still surface of water in his marriage, and the echo of his therapist's words — "when the crowd disperses, what are you left with?"
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because my whole life I've been filling myself with crowds, and this person is the first one who made me feel like I didn't have to perform to be caught — not on a stage, but in a hotel lobby late at night, two people telling each other the truth. A connection like this isn't something I can just set down. Silence is the thing I truly cannot bear.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, being truly seen, being truly heard, matters more than any applause — that's what I've been searching for since I was six years old. What I cannot accept is strangling that feeling on a phone screen, pretending it doesn't exist, and then going back to that pool of still water.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to reply to this colleague's message — not keeping a deliberate distance, but taking one step forward along this genuine connection. I write back: "Made it home. I kept thinking about what you said during the meeting today — that bookshop really does sound quiet. We should go sometime. Good night."

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment the screen lit up, my chest seized — like someone had yanked me out of my chair right then and there. She'll ask for results the second she picks up, and I haven't even managed to swallow that line of red text myself yet. The hallway is packed with people, and my most familiar instinct is to find someone to talk to, to hold the atmosphere together — but this time my throat feels like it's blocked. All those feelings of being misread, being watched, holding myself together in public — they all came rushing back at once. What I'm most afraid of isn't this piece of paper; it's watching her expression change through a screen while I can't string a single complete sentence together. But not answering is worse — leaving her hanging will only make her mind spiral. I have to pick up. But I can't let the panic come out first. I need to steady her, and steady myself, and wait until the follow-up confirms things before we carry this together.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Emotional tone — Panic, a suffocating heaviness, and holding it together by force. The panic comes from that line of red text and the oppressive weight of the hospital hallway; the suffocating heaviness comes from clutching these results alone while my partner is nowhere near me; the forced composure comes from my long-standing reluctance to throw my most chaotic self directly at the person closest to me — the intensity of all three is very high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know all too well that one piece of bad news delivered without preparation can drag the whole situation into an even worse kind of panic — so my instinct is to not let the person I love most be the first to catch me when I'm falling apart. But I also know that not answering the phone only creates a bigger silence, and silence is the most frightening thing of all.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me right now is steadying the situation first — not letting someone who loves me spiral alongside me through a screen. But I also can't bring myself to lie outright, to keep her completely in the dark as if nothing has happened.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to answer the video call, but I keep things vague for now — I'm not going to drop the worst of it on her yet. Once I pick up, I tell her: two of my markers came back elevated, the doctor wants me to get a follow-up as soon as possible, and the moment those results are in, you'll be the first to know.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择接起视频通话，但含糊带过检查结果，暂时瞒住异常指标。接起电话后笑着说：'结果刚拿到，有几个数值还要再确认一下，等你周五回来我们一起去复查，没什么大事，你在那边别担心。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择接起视频，但先含糊带过，不把最坏的话现在抛出去。我接通后说有两个指标偏高，医生让尽快复查，等复查结果出来我第一时间告诉你。

inner_consciousness.summary: When she sent that message, I felt like my fingers were nailed to the spot. Three happy emojis, the guesthouse already booked, already talking about plans for Europe — she has no idea. Yesterday's two hours are still vivid in my mind: him sitting in my living room, voice so low, so solemn, saying "you're the only one I truly trust." I said yes. And now here she is, her excitement filling the whole screen, while I'm standing in front of her holding a live grenade. The worst part is I can't respond to her the way I normally would — all warmth and enthusiasm. Turning every get-together into a real, genuine connection is what I do best, and yet the moment I open my mouth this time, it's all a lie. Camping, a guesthouse, the four of us — I know that picture by heart, all the times we've laughed together. But he's already found a lawyer. If I say "sure, let's go," I'm helping her keep living inside a story that no longer exists. If I say "I can't" or make up some excuse, she'll ask why, and I won't hold up — I'm just not built for silence and deflection; the more someone pushes, the more I crack. And honestly, carrying this secret is already suffocating me. I need to talk, I need someone to know — and yet I'm the one person who can't say a word. All I can do is write back, keep up the pretense, get through this moment.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Suppression and discomfort (being forced into silence goes against every instinct I have; the weight of a promise I can't break); Guilt toward my friend (the sharp, tearing contrast between her happiness and the truth I'm sitting on); Mild anxiety (afraid of slipping up during the exchange, because I'm genuinely bad at sustaining a lie or dodging questions). Intensity: moderate-to-high — not a breakdown, but a relentless, suffocating pressure that makes this deeply painful.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I've been through too many moments of holding a smile in public while having nothing I'm allowed to say — I know that kind of pretending burns through a relationship from the inside. But this time I made a promise to him, face to face, and that promise is real. I can't betray it. All I can do is muddle through this moment and buy myself time to figure out what comes next.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me in any relationship is authenticity and genuine presence — pretending nothing is happening is just about the hardest state for me to be in. But I also can't turn my back on a trust that was handed to me in person, even if it's backed me into a corner of silence.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to accept the camping invitation and respond with surface-level enthusiasm, but leave myself a vague out — while quietly working to make sure the four of us never actually go. I write back: "That sounds amazing! The guesthouse must have such a beautiful view — let me just check my schedule on this end, you guys lock in the details first, and I'll get back to you as soon as I can!"

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择接受露营邀请、表面热情回应，但用含糊理由为自己留退路，同时暗中避免让四人真正成行。回复她："太好了！民宿风景一定超美，我这边先看看时间，你们先确认好，我尽快回你！"

decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择接受邀请，用极度热情的态度掩饰知情。我迅速回复微信：“太棒了！听到你们感情升温我真高兴！这周末必须安排上，我来准备烤肉和好酒，咱们好好庆祝一下！”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment this proposal was pushed in front of me, I felt the blood rush to my head — not anger exactly, more that familiar feeling of being lit up. I held it together in the all-hands meeting, all those eyes in the room bearing down on me, I bit my tongue and didn't say a word, but that breath has been lodged in my chest ever since. Now it's just the two of us, and he's laid it out in the most final terms possible — 'not here to discuss whether it can be changed' — fine, then let's not discuss it, let's talk. That process was built brick by brick by me, how many cohorts of new people have run through it, and he thinks he can just tear it down? It's not that I can't accept new things, but you have to show me where this proposal is actually better than mine — otherwise, on what grounds? He's younger than me, parachuted in from outside, and the first fire he lights is on me, right in front of everyone. I held back — that was for the team's sake, not a concession. Now this small conference room is actually my territory — no audience, that's fine, the person standing in front of me is the best opponent I could ask for. I want to lay everything out, put the problems on the table, go through them point by point — wherever there's a risk I'll name it, wherever there's room to adjust I'll offer a solution — not confrontation, a real exchange. This feeling makes me sharp, makes things feel real. Silence is what drains you; saying it out loud is what solves it.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Activated fighting spirit (rooted in the suppressed backlash from being publicly criticized, and a deep-seated instinct to draw energy from conflict); underlying resentment (the sting of having years of seniority and accumulated work dismissed in a single stroke); a strange exhilaration (the face-off between the two of them actually makes him feel real and in control); low-level vigilance (the other person is his superior — he has to read the line carefully).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been through countless moments of being questioned and shut down in public, and time and again it was by speaking up directly, by engaging in a real confrontation on the spot, that I ultimately earned respect — so my instinct is that silence and surface compliance only let problems fester inside, and that putting risks and objections squarely on the table is the only genuinely professional stance.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'saying it to someone's face' matters a hundred times more than 'stewing in silence'; I cannot accept having years of accumulated work cut off at the root with a single 'the overall direction is set' — even if you're my superior, you still owe me a reason that actually holds up.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to step up and engage directly, raising specific objections on the spot and working to embed my own judgment and buffer space within the execution framework — rather than offering surface compliance. I flip the proposal open, press my index finger against one of the timeline milestones, and say: 'I have an issue to raise here — not a question of whether it can be changed, but if this milestone goes the way you're describing, I can tell you right now exactly what gaps will open up in the transition period. Let's hear how you want to handle that.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择主动接招、当场提出具体异议，争取在执行框架内嵌入自己的判断和缓冲空间，而非表面顺从。我把方案翻开，食指点着其中一个时间节点说：'这里我有问题要提——不是能不能改的问题，是这个节点如果按你说的走，过渡期会出哪些具体漏洞，我现在就告诉你，你看怎么处理。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择正面接招，但争取缓冲和共担机制。我先认可改方向，再当场逐条指出过渡风险，要求加试运行节点和责任分工。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择正面迎击，反客为主将私下施压转化为公开讨论。我身体前倾直视他，笑着说：“既然话都在团队面前放出了，执行细节也别藏着掖着，我现在就把大家叫进来，咱们当场把新规矩盘透。”

inner_consciousness.summary: Whoa, a bug I've never seen before! I'm crouching here and there's this feeling inside me like I've just struck treasure — the green shell on its back is actually shimmering, and its antennae split into three separate tips, how weird is that?! I was about to get a really close look when suddenly someone comes running over yelling for me to come play soccer — they're short a player! My brain immediately gets pulled in two directions — over on the field there's a whole crowd of people, loud and alive, and I love that feeling more than anything; just picturing everyone running and shouting together makes me restless with excitement. But this bug… I've never seen it in any book, and here it is just sitting on this spiral flower, almost like it was waiting for me to find it. He said 'gross,' but honestly that just makes it more fun to me — what does he know about how rare this thing might be? Still, the laughter from the field keeps drifting over, and silence is genuinely hard for me — crouching here alone feels like such a waste… Wait, why does it have to be one or the other? I can just bring this little guy along! Get everyone to come look — wouldn't that be even better? A bug nobody's ever seen, plus a whole crowd of people — now that's what I call a good time!
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Excitement and inner tension coexisting — curiosity about this strange creature has lit up a real urge to explore, but the pull of the crowd is just as strong; rooted in countless experiences of drawing energy from being around people, crouching alone in a corner is a quiet drain on him; overall mood leans active and eager, intensity medium-to-high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every single time there are other people involved, everything becomes more fun — so my instinct is to merge the two things together: bring the bug to the crowd and turn the discovery into a shared spectacle.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: To me, something amazing only half counts if you're the only one who knows about it; the real fun is getting everyone to gasp together. I can't stand the idea of just quietly crouching here and letting this discovery go to 'waste.'

pairs.final_decision: I choose to accept the invitation and go play soccer, but at the same time I turn this strange bug into a 'surprise' I bring to the field — merging both things into one. I say to the kid: 'Let's go, let's go — but look at this first. Have you ever seen a bug like this? Its back is green and it actually glows! We'll come back and study it after the game — come on, let's go!'

inner_consciousness.summary: When the security guard held up that Optimus Prime figure and shook it, my mind went completely blank — I never touched it, so how did it end up in my bag? I turned to look at my friend and his face had gone white, lips trembling, and then he leaned in close, and that "please, I'm begging you" hit my ear like a needle. I could feel everyone around me staring, and that feeling of being ringed in by all those eyes — I know it too well, it's always like this, chest burning hot, ears starting to sting. He said his dad would send him back to his hometown, and in a flash I could picture that feeling of being alone in an abandoned factory with no way out — that kind of isolation, I get it, I really do. But I also know another feeling — being called a thief in front of everyone. What that's like. I'm not the kind of person who does something like that, and I can't let everyone think I am. He's begging me, but if I nod right now, I really will become that kid who steals things, and that's how everyone will remember me. I can't let that be who I am when I'm standing in front of people. I can't take the fall for him — not because I don't care about him, but because I can't let myself turn into that person.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Grievance and anger intertwined (the sharp sting of being wrongly accused, built up from countless past moments of being misunderstood and scrutinized by others); mixed with genuine heartache for his friend's situation, and an instinctive impulse toward self-protection — how he appears in front of a crowd is the thing he cares about most and the thing he guards most fragily. Intensity is fairly high, but because he's used to holding himself together in public, his outward composure will look steadier than what's happening inside.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been pinned in place by the stare of a crowd too many times — that shame I know by heart — I instinctively understand that the moment I admit to this in front of so many people, the label 'the kid who steals' will follow me for a long time. I can't accept trading away who I am in front of people just to buy my friend a moment of safety.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, who I am in front of others — how people see me — matters more than a lot of things. It's not vanity; it's that I've known since I was little what it feels like to be pushed to the outside of the group, so I absolutely cannot let myself get stuck with a label that won't wash off. I feel for my friend, I really do — but standing up in public and admitting to something I didn't do is something I simply cannot accept.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to tell the security guard honestly that I didn't put the toy in my bag, while not directly pointing the finger at my friend — instead I say I have no idea how it got there and ask the guard to check the security footage. I look up at the guard and say: 'Sir, I really didn't put this toy in my bag. I don't know how it got in there. You have security cameras — could you check them?'
