But if I back down now, this whole thing simply disappears. The evidence is in my hands. Two months of my life are in this. That elevator is still right there. I have no way to pretend none of this happened, and I have no way to make myself say 'just let it go.'
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Heavy and lonely — the neighbors' retreat has pushed me into a position with no way back, and that familiar feeling of 'standing alone in the middle' has returned, but this time it's different: this time what I'm holding is real evidence, not someone else's quarrel; a suppressed sense of grievance — 'you're young, you can handle it' stirred something that has been building for a long time, that feeling of being treated as a tool rather than a partner after everything you've put in; and yet underneath it all there is a low, quiet resolve, because the rights and wrongs of this matter are clear to me — clear enough that I cannot pretend otherwise.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I spent two months getting to the bottom of this, I know it is neither a misunderstanding nor a grey area — it is a real, documented misappropriation. So I cannot cover it over with the word 'harmony.' The one who broke the harmony here is not me; it's that money.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: Keeping the peace matters to me, but there is something that matters more than keeping the peace — and that is refusing to let a clear injustice be buried under the phrase 'don't make things awkward.' What I cannot accept is this: trading two million yuan worth of evidence for silence, and then telling myself that's called knowing the bigger picture.

pairs.final_decision: I have decided to file a formal, named complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name, while making clear to my neighbors that I understand their reluctance but will not accept the advice to 'just let it go.' I said to the teacher upstairs: 'I understand your difficulties completely, and I'm not going to drag you into this. I'll handle it myself. If you're willing to help me track down materials, I'm grateful; if not, I won't hold it against you. But this name on the complaint — that has to be mine.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 决定以个人名义向住建局实名举报，同时对邻居的退缩表示理解但不接受"就算了"的劝说。我对楼上的老师说：'您的难处我都明白，我不会拉着您们。但这件事我自己去办，您们愿意帮我找材料，我感激；不愿意，我也不怪您。只是这个名字，得我自己签。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择以个人名义实名举报，但不逼邻居公开联名。 我会对邻居说我理解你们的怕，你们不用勉强签字，把能补的材料私下给我，我去住建局。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择放弃举报，息事宁人。我勉强挤出苦笑对邻居说：“老哥，大家都有难处，我也不想让家里人害怕，这事就算了吧。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment my name was called, my mind went blank for a fraction of a second — not from excitement, but because this was completely outside anything I'd anticipated. I dashed that piece off without a second thought, didn't even proofread it for typos — so what exactly earned it first place? The result itself reads like an anomaly in the data. The flash of a camera, heads turning toward me, I stood up and walked to the stage, my feet solid against the floor, but somewhere in my head a voice was running at full speed: the most important question right now isn't the award — it's what I'm going to say once that microphone reaches me. I'm not good at moments like this; a flash of that suffocating feeling from the time I was pushed onstage in music class crossed my mind, but that was being forced into embarrassment — this is different. This time I'm the one who got it right, the outcome is on my side. Mr. Zhao handed me the microphone, hundreds of pairs of eyes in the audience, the red light on the camera blinking, and I noticed my palms were a little damp — but that's not fear, it's just a physiological response to information overload. I don't need to perform gratitude, don't need to pretend I'm overwhelmed by the honor. I just need to tell the truth. Telling the truth has never been hard for me; what's hard is that people usually don't want to hear it. But this time, they're here specifically to hear what I have to say. So — say it.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm as the dominant baseline, tinged with mild surprise and a rare, quietly satisfying sense of vindication. The surprise comes from the gap between expectation and outcome; the satisfaction comes from the long-held belief that 'results are the only valid proof' being redeemed in this moment; the slight physical tension (damp palms) is an instinctive response to a public setting, but it doesn't take over emotionally.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've always believed that results are the only meaningful proof, and this award is the result — I have no reason to act awkward or put on a performance up here. The facts are already on my side; all I need to do is state them plainly.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters to me is saying what I actually think, clearly and without performance or pandering. What I can't accept is filling up time in a moment that calls for plain speech with a string of hollow thank-yous — that's wasteful, and it's a kind of dishonesty.

pairs.final_decision: I chose to speak directly, describing my creative process in the most concise and honest way I could — no performed gratitude, no studied humility. Into the microphone I said: 'I wrote this piece off the top of my head three weeks ago. I just wanted to work out a story with a coherent internal logic. Winning means the judges found that logic convincing, and honestly, that's a better outcome than I expected.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择直接发言，用最简洁真实的方式陈述创作过程，不表演感激也不刻意谦虚。我对着话筒说：'这篇稿子是三周前随手写的，我当时只是想把一个逻辑自洽的故事写完。能拿奖说明评委认可这个逻辑，我觉得这比我预期的结果要好。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择简短发言，不表演感动也不回避上台。我接过话筒，只说灵感来自随手想到的设定，感谢老师，然后立刻结束。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择拒绝迎合，陈述事实。我接过话筒说：“稿子是我随手写的，能拿奖说明评判标准有问题，没灵感分享。”

inner_consciousness.summary: My friend said 'it'll be hard to tell who deserves credit'—and that rubbed me the wrong way. What do you mean, hard to tell? My concept is my concept. Once we merge, whose work does it become? I spent three nights sketching those set designs—not so they could disappear inside some 'foodie karaoke night.' But I'm not blind to reality either: people have already started picking sides over there, and being well-liked is a resource in its own right—I can't pretend it isn't. Go campaign for votes? Track people down one by one and walk them through the details? I ran through it quickly in my head—those people were all crowding around her today saying 'easier this way.' Easy is what they want. If I go talk to them about lighting cues and blocking, are they going to listen? No. But merging is a trap too. The moment my work gets folded into her framework, who actually calls the shots during execution? She has more people on her side—next thing you know, the 'Time Tunnel' concept becomes a backdrop, and the whole thing is still basically karaoke. That's not the result I'm after. So the real question isn't 'merge or campaign'—it's: does my concept have any chance of winning in its complete, uncompromised form? If it doesn't, then merging is just losing gracefully. If it does, then I need to find the people who actually care about the quality of the show—not waste my breath on the ones who just want the path of least resistance. I need to figure out where the votes actually are first.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm, but with an undercurrent of defiance (intensity: roughly 7/10). The defiance comes from being outmaneuvered by something like 'popularity advantage'—a non-merit factor. It's a feeling I've been here before: logic is on my side, and yet emotion and social networks override it anyway. That old frustration is surfacing again. The calm comes from a deeper conviction: feelings don't solve a vote-count problem—only analysis does.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I've seen it too many times—majority rule, everyone picks a side, and the execution turns out terrible. My gut knows that merging means handing over control, and a concept I've lost control of is no longer my concept. So I'm not going to talk about merging. What I'm going to do is figure out which people genuinely care about the quality of this show, and then make my case to exactly those people.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters to me is that the concept gets executed in its complete, intact form—not that 'my name is attached to some watered-down compromise.' What I can't accept is this: I put three nights into something, and it gets diluted simply because the other person has better social connections. That's not losing to a better concept—that's losing to social capital. To me, those are two entirely different things.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to merge and not to campaign blindly—instead, I'm going to do a precise read of where the votes actually are. I'll identify the small number of people in the class who genuinely want the show to be good, not the ones who'd be happy just singing a few songs, and focus all my energy on convincing them. I tell my friend: 'Merging is off the table—if we do that, my concept is gone. Help me think: who in the class actually wants to put on a real show? Not the 'whatever, just sing' crowd—starting with those people, I'll go pitch my concept.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择不合并、不盲目拉票，而是精准分析票仓——找出班里真正在意晚会质量而非图省事的少数人，集中精力说服他们。我对朋友说：'合并不考虑，那样我的方案就没了。你帮我想想，班里谁是真的想把晚会办好的，不是那种随便唱歌就行的——从那几个人开始，我去讲方案。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择主动谈融合，但要保住方案主框架和主导权。我会去找她，直接提“时光隧道做主线，留一段自由点歌和美食区”，同时把分工先定死。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 拒绝妥协，选择坚持原方案并用事实硬刚。我对朋友说“融合是不可能的，那只会毁了逻辑”，周末我会把方案细化成包含预算和分工的PPT，周一直接用执行力碾压对方。

inner_consciousness.summary: My mother's voice comes through the phone — choked, trembling, like a needle driving straight in. I'm sitting here, phone still pressed to my ear, and my mind has already started running through the logistics: Dad is in the hospital, the situation is serious, someone needs to be there. That's a fact, not an emotion. But the overseas opportunity is also a fact — two years, salary doubled, that kind of window doesn't wait for anyone. I think about my father, about that afternoon he sat on the carpet with me putting together a model car, sunlight catching the lenses of his glasses — one of the few moments between us that wasn't a standoff. But I also think about the way he humiliated me at the dinner table, the way he always demanded obedience instead of conversation. These two versions of him overlap in my head, and that makes it impossible for me to handle this with any simple, clean emotion. I'm running the numbers: if I go back now, the career opportunity is most likely gone — that cost is real and quantifiable. But if I don't go back, Dad is critically ill, Mom is alone, and that cost is real too — not in an emotional sense, but on a purely operational level. Medical decisions need someone present; without me, things will be handled worse. I don't like being held hostage by feelings, but this isn't a feelings problem — it's a resource allocation problem. The scarcest resources right now are time and physical presence, not that job offer. The job can be renegotiated; I only have one father, and right now he's in a hospital. I've run the logic. I know what I need to do.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm is dominant, but underneath there's a suppressed undercurrent of complexity — not grief, but being forced to confront someone I've never been able to fully sidestep through my usual methods. Intensity is moderate-to-high; the emotional charge comes from the accumulated contradictions in my relationship with my father, and from a genuine, unresolved resentment over losing this career opportunity.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many messes made by letting emotion drive decisions, my instinct is to break this down into a resource allocation problem — the value of being physically present right now outweighs that job contract, and that's a calculation I can actually make. It's not because I love him. It's because this is the optimal move given the current situation.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters more to me is making the right decision, not making the decision that makes me feel good about myself. What I can't accept is substituting emotion for analysis — but equally, I can't accept letting the whole situation spiral into greater chaos just to avoid dealing with a complicated person.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to go back immediately to handle my father's medical affairs, while simultaneously reaching out to the company to negotiate a delay or explore remote options for the overseas opportunity. This isn't a pure emotional sacrifice — I'm treating it as a project that requires my on-site presence. I tell my mother: 'I'll book a ticket tonight and be there tomorrow. In the meantime, have the doctors pull together all the test results — when I arrive, I'll go straight to the attending physician and talk it through myself.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择立即回去处理父亲的医疗事务，同时同步联系公司争取延期或远程沟通海外机会，不做单纯的情感牺牲，而是把这件事当作需要亲自在场的项目来执行。我对母亲说：'我今晚订票，明天到，你先让医生把所有检查结果整理好，我到了直接跟主治医生谈。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择先回医院，暂缓海外项目。 我立刻订票回家，并联系公司要求延期确认或安排过渡方案。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择保留海外机会，用经济手段解决家庭危机。我对着电话平静地说：我马上转一笔钱过去，你立刻去请最好的全职护工，我这边的工作走不开，但我会负责全部医疗费用。

inner_consciousness.summary: He closed my materials and set them at the corner of the desk — that single gesture told me he never intended to take them seriously. All that talk of 'uncontrolled conditions' and 'overstated claims' — I've heard it too many times. My whole life, every time I pointed out an error, the other person's first instinct was always to find an excuse to save face rather than look at the data. He said he's supervised many cohorts without any problems, but that's precisely the problem: no problems doesn't mean the method is right — it just means the luck hasn't run out yet. I glanced at those framed certificates on the wall and had only one thought: none of that has anything to do with my experimental data. He's using authority to override logic — just like that teacher when I was young, just like my father, using 'I've been teaching for half my life' to shut down a correct answer. The threat of a delay? I've done the math — the time cost of revising the plan is manageable, but handing in work built on a systematically flawed approach, that's a cost you can never fully calculate. He said 'the basis for collaboration is hard to guarantee' — that's a threat, but he's overestimating what that threat is worth. The one thing I'm clearest on right now: I have data. He only has seniority.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm and resolute (core emotion): rooted in a long-held conviction that logic takes precedence over authority, reinforced by countless experiences of being suppressed and ultimately proven right; Mild sense of being dismissed: the advisor's gesture of closing my materials triggered faint echoes of childhood — my father cutting me off mid-sentence, a teacher making me stand in the corner — but that emotion was quickly taken over by a rational framework; Uncompromising will to act: high intensity, driven by a deep-seated resistance to the idea that silence equals concession.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since childhood that when authority figures use seniority to pressure people, it's usually because they can't produce better logic — and this time is no different. I have independent control-group data; he only has 'I've supervised many cohorts.' I can live with the cost of offending my advisor, but I cannot live with the cost of submitting a final result built on a method I've already proven to be flawed.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, being correct matters more than anyone's face — including the face of the person who signs off on my work. What I cannot accept is this: having better evidence right in my hands, yet pretending it doesn't exist out of fear of causing offense — that's not compromise, that's self-deception.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to stand by the evidence and the alternative approach directly, explicitly refusing the suggestion to put away my materials and proceed with the original plan, while keeping my tone focused on the issue rather than emotionally confrontational. I speak up: 'I understand your judgment, but I can't put these materials away — I ran a controlled experiment, the data is right here, and I can go through it point by point right now. If you can show me where my methodology is flawed, I'll correct it immediately. But if the data itself holds up, I need you to seriously consider adjusting the approach. I'm willing to take the risk of a delay on myself.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择直接坚持证据与替代方案，明确拒绝收起材料、按既定方案推进的建议，同时保持就事论事的语气而非情绪对抗。我开口说：'我理解您的判断，但我没办法把这份材料收起来——我做了对照实验，数据在这里，我可以现在逐条过一遍，如果您能指出我的方法论哪里有问题，我立刻修正；但如果数据本身没问题，我需要您认真考虑调整路线，延期的风险我愿意自己承担。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择坚持判断，但提出折中验证路线，不正面服从。 我说我不建议直接全盘切换，但至少保留现方案节点，同时加做一组对照验证再定主线。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择正面交锋，拒绝妥协。我没有收回材料，直视他平静地说：“风险不会因为资历而消失，如果您不看数据，我无法执行明知有错的方案。”

inner_consciousness.summary: Footsteps, pounding on the door, and then this man standing in the doorway, cigarette butt shoved in my face. My luggage has already been thrown out into the hallway. I watched that baseball bat knock against the floor twice, and those two buzz-cut guys stepped forward. Alright — let me break this scene down: he's talking about breach of contract, about high-wattage heaters, but the contract says "no high-wattage appliances" — so what's the actual wattage on the space heater I bought? I remember it perfectly clearly; it doesn't even come close to qualifying as high-wattage. This is a lie. A lie designed to scare people. He's standing on my suitcase, brought two guys with him, middle of the night — he's using the threat of violence as a substitute for legal process. He says he knows people at the local police station — maybe true, maybe not, but even if it is, an illegal eviction like this isn't something one phone call can make disappear. What can I actually do right now? Stand my ground and fight back on the spot? Two built guys right here — that's not a cost-effective option. Drop to my knees, fold, grab my bag and leave? Then I lose the deposit and let him know this tactic works. No. What I need is to stabilize things tonight, then hit back in ways he doesn't understand. I'm not afraid of this man — what I'm afraid of is making a stupid decision. Record everything, document everything, gather evidence, go through legal channels — he thinks I'll be scared off, but he doesn't know I'm not that kind of person.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Cold fury (low intensity but sustained): rooted in memories of being suppressed by authority figures through brute force rather than reason since childhood — this anger isn't panic, it's a calm confirmation that the person in front of me is using illegal means to bully an outsider, fundamentally no different from those who hide behind authority and refuse to be reasonable; Contempt: he thinks intimidation is enough to solve the problem, but he's severely underestimated the cost of that strategy when it comes to me; Alertness (moderate intensity): two men, a baseball bat, the middle of the night, an isolated environment — these variables need to factor into my immediate decisions.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since I was young that people who use brute force and authority to push others around are fundamentally betting that the other person will be afraid — and I won't let them win that bet; the cost of making enemies is usually overestimated, but meeting a violent threat head-on is foolish — the real counterattack is nailing him down with rules and evidence somewhere he can't see coming.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is that this illegal behavior cannot be allowed to succeed — the deposit is my money, it's my right, and it's not something he can just take by showing up with two enforcers; what I cannot accept is giving up and walking away out of fear, because that would be telling him this method works, and handing over my rights without a fight.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to appear outwardly compliant for now in order to remove myself from the immediate threat of violence, while quietly initiating evidence collection and legal proceedings in the background — I will not give up the deposit under any circumstances. I say to him: 'Fine, I'll leave tonight — but the contract and the deposit, we're not done with that. You'd better take a good hard look at those contract terms, because I'm going to have someone verify them.' At the same time, I quietly start recording on my phone.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择表面暂时顺从以脱离当下暴力威胁，同时暗中启动证据收集和法律追诉程序，绝不放弃押金。我对他说：'好，我今晚先走，但合同和押金的事我们没完——你最好把合同条款翻清楚，我会找人核实的。'同时用手机悄悄开启录音。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择暂时不硬抗、保留追讨，先降风险后反击。我先开手机录音录像，冷声说我现在配合离开但不同意扣押金，并立刻联系报警和能作证的人。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择避免肢体冲突，引入公权力。我按下手机录音，冷冷地说：“合同没标瓦数，不构成违约，我现在报警。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The buzz of the bank notification felt like a needle jabbing in. I stared at those words on the screen — "35 and under" — and felt something tighten in my gut. My partner's hand closed around mine; I could feel the warmth, but I didn't move. She asked, "Is there really no other possibility?" — what I heard wasn't concern, it was a question that needed an answer. I ran the numbers fast: how many months the N+3 severance could cover, my father's follow-up medical costs, the tuition after the college entrance exam, the monthly mortgage figure I know by heart with my eyes closed. Those numbers are real. Everything else is noise. I thought back to all those conference rooms years ago — every time I said something people didn't want to hear, I was eventually proven right. The partners who left, the people who stormed out slamming doors — their emotions didn't change a single number. What I'm facing now is also a numbers problem, not an emotional one. She's waiting for me to say something. I'm not going to tell her just how clearly I see how bad this situation is, but I'm also not going to fob her off with some hollow "it'll be fine." She deserves to know the real calculation, not to be comforted. I won't budge easily on my salary floor, but I need to redefine what "floor" actually means — not pride, but whether the cash flow can hold this family's structure together.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm, but carrying a suppressed sense of weight (born from a long habit of using rationality to keep emotion in check); and a low-intensity yet persistent need for control (rooted in a childhood instinct — formed while watching his parents fight — that says "you have to control the situation or you'll be powerless"). No breakdown, but a dull, precise ache from being squarely hit by reality.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I learned early on that emotional people always lose at the critical moment, I instinctively process this scene as a cost-structure problem to be broken down, not a family crisis that calls for empathy. I've been through too many cycles of "hold the line and eventually be proven right" to let market discrimination against a 48-year-old make me abandon my own pricing of my abilities — but I'm also rational enough to know that pricing has to be grounded in real cash-flow pressure, not self-respect.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not maintaining this family on lies, even when the truth is hard to hear — my partner should know the real numbers, not be shielded from them. What I cannot accept is dressing up compromise as "being realistic" and then slowly losing trust in my own judgment.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to be honest about the real situation and recalibrate my job-search floor — no soothing platitudes to placate my partner, but no emotional breakdown either. I squeeze her hand and speak: 'I'll be straight with you — the market right now isn't kind to people our age, but I've done the math: the severance can last eight months. I'll adjust my expectations, but I won't just take anything. Let's get the numbers clear first, and your dad's appointment this month — we'll go together.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择坦白真实处境并重新校准求职底线，不用安慰话语糊弄伴侣，但也不情绪化崩溃。我握住她的手，开口说：'我跟你说实话——现在的市场对我们这个年纪不友好，但我算过了，补偿金能撑八个月，我会调整预期，但不会乱接，先把数字理清楚，你爸那边这个月我们一起去。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择坦白现实并下调求职底线，同时准备更激进的转向。 我对伴侣说先别卖房，我会先接能接的工作，再立刻盘副业和更高风险机会。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择拒绝妥协与低薪，决定另寻激进变现路径。我抽出手，平静地告诉她别动卖房的念头，随后关掉求职软件，开始起草新的商业计划。

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment he said it out loud, what I felt first wasn't hurt — it was alarm. 'Redefining boundaries' — that phrase is all too familiar, the kind of language people use when they want to keep the benefits while dodging the cost. The warm light in the living room didn't put me at ease; if anything, it made the problem sharper: if a commitment can be rewritten, what is the relationship even standing on? I immediately thought of all those decisions that got sidestepped, rules changed at the last minute, things that were called 'not a betrayal' while the line had already been crossed. My phone lighting up felt more like a needle prick — a reminder that information is always more honest than emotion. It's not that I didn't notice his nervousness, and it's not that I don't know how awkward it would be to call it out directly — but I'm even less willing to mistake vagueness for maturity, or dress up opacity as freedom. Better to nail things down right now than to let the relationship rot slowly in some gray zone. I know all too well: blurry boundaries only ever turn into loss of control and slow erosion.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Alarm, irritation, cold hardness. The alarm comes from my long-standing sensitivity to boundaries being rewritten and information being kept opaque; the irritation comes from too many times in the past when people dressed up overstepping as 'flexibility'; the cold hardness comes from knowing that once things like this get murky, they only get worse — intensity is high across all three.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I've been through too many situations where a loosened rule got used to rewrite reality, so my instinct is that so-called 'flexibility,' if it can't be clearly constrained, is essentially the same as dismantling a commitment. A relationship can negotiate terms, but it can't survive on ambiguity.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is clear boundaries, equal rules, and everything said out in the open. What I can't accept — more than conflict itself — is using the language of love and maturity to rationalize disloyalty and opacity.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to reject this boundary adjustment and explicitly uphold the original commitment. I'll silence my phone first, then look him in the eye and say: 'I don't accept this kind of relationship. If this is what you need, then let's have a real conversation about whether we're still doing this at all.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择直接要求对方把话说透，拒绝在模糊框架下讨论任何'边界调整'。我放下手机，看着她说：'你说的这些我需要听明白——你想要的是什么，具体是什么，不要用'弹性'这个词，把它说清楚。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择拒绝这项边界调整，明确维护原有承诺。我要先按掉手机，看着他说：我不接受这种关系，如果你需要这个，我们就谈清楚是否还继续。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择拒绝模糊边界，直接戳破对方的逻辑漏洞。我将手机倒扣在桌上，直视伴侣的眼睛：“你所谓的‘弹性’本质上就是单方面违约，我只接受遵守契约或者彻底解除，没有中间地带。”

inner_consciousness.summary: Empty. That word kept circling in my head as I stared at the vacant spot — the chair still there, the bowl and chopsticks still there, just missing the person. Yesterday, when I watched that car disappear, I stood in place for a long time. I knew this was right, that this was how things were supposed to go, but that hollow feeling crept in anyway. Now the person I still live with is standing in the kitchen doorway, pajamas all wrinkled, saying something like 'we'll each do our own thing' or 'want to go for a walk' — voice trailing off, even he doesn't know what he wants. I look at him, he looks at me, and the two of us are just standing there in this house that suddenly feels too big, not knowing where to put ourselves. My first instinct was: this kind of aimless drifting is the least efficient state to be in — it drains you. But then I caught myself, and realized that framing today in terms of 'efficiency' feels somehow wrong. All those years of getting up early to make breakfast, doing the school runs — I never once asked myself whether that was efficient. It was just what life looked like. Now life has changed its shape, and we're both standing at a new edge, not sure how to step forward. I don't like this uncertainty. But I also know that pretending to stay busy is just a way of dodging a very real question — the two of us, what do we do with ourselves now.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Hollowness (rooted in the sudden collapse of life's structure — the daily rhythms built around caregiving, gone overnight); mild unease (the uncertainty triggers an instinctive resistance to feeling out of control); an uncommon openness (a faint memory surfaces — the stillness of a childhood balcony, the morning light on a balcony shared with my partner — hinting that maybe this moment doesn't need to be filled with logic). Moderate in intensity; not enough to break me, but carrying a few more grams of real weight than my usual composure.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've done it too many times — used busyness and logic to sidestep the things I actually needed to face — I know instinctively that if we each drift off now, that hollow won't shrink. It'll just be shelved. And the person standing in front of me is in the same unfamiliar place I am. That's rare — a moment that doesn't require any maneuvering.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters more to me is not letting this hollowness become a long-term, low-grade drain — not because I'm afraid of emotion, but because the cost of avoiding it is higher than the cost of facing it. What I cannot accept is two people sitting under the same roof, each pretending nothing has changed. That would be the more complete waste.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to be the one who breaks the stalemate — to acknowledge that this strangeness is real, and to suggest we go out for a walk together. I say: 'Come on, let's go get some air — sitting here isn't going to bring any answers anyway.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择主动打破僵局，承认这种陌生感的存在，提议一起出去走走。我开口说：'走吧，出去转一圈，反正在这里坐着也想不出什么来。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择主动打破陌生感，先一起试着重建新的日常。 我看着他说，别各忙各的了，今天出去走走，顺便把以后怎么过聊清楚。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择建立新秩序并各自回归高效状态。我看着ta说：“今天白天先各自处理手头的工作，晚上我们去外面吃饭，顺便规划一下以后的时间安排。”

inner_consciousness.summary: I stare at that photo, my finger frozen on the screen. That smile, those award certificates, that line — "became exactly who he wanted to be" — my hand starts to shake, and not from envy. It's something I've been pressing down for a long time, and it just broke through from underground. Twenty years. My logic back then was clear: my family needed me, the risk was too great, stability mattered more. It was a rational choice. But now these two sets of data are laid out in front of me — his outcome, and mine — and I can't help making the comparison. I'm sitting in this car, wearing my neat work clothes, with family waiting for me to come home for dinner, with a deadline, with all the "things I'm supposed to have" — but right now it feels like a contract I never signed. I try to run a quick calculation: what were the odds of success on that other path? He's just one data point. But the moment that thought surfaces, another voice crushes it — I have no idea, because I never tried. That's the real reason my hand is shaking. Not that he succeeded, but that I will never, ever know the answer to what my own path might have been. I don't like unknown variables — especially the kind that can never be verified. My family is waiting for me, there are things to do tomorrow, all of that is real. But this hollow feeling is real too, and it won't disappear just because I drive home.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Hollowness (core): rooted in a "counterfactual" that can never be verified, triggering a deep discomfort with unknown variables; Suppressed anger (secondary): not directed at anyone else, but at my own "rational choice" from back then — I'm starting to question whether it was truly rational, or simply fear dressed up as reason; Compulsive calm (defensive): an instinct to contain this feeling with logic, to file it away as an "invalid comparison due to insufficient data" — but this time the filing system won't hold. Emotional intensity: medium-high, higher than usual, because this isn't an external conflict — it's my internal logic framework turning on itself.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every decision I've ever made rests on the foundation of "using data and logic to eliminate uncertainty" — and now I'm confronted with a blank that no data can ever fill — the road not taken — and it has opened an irreparable gap in my entire decision-making framework. I can't pretend it isn't there.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is that "choices can be verified" — I can accept failure, but I cannot accept an unknown variable hanging there forever with no answer; what I cannot accept is this: that I may have used the words "mature" and "responsible" to cover up a decision that was, at its core, driven by fear — and that's exactly what I might have done back then.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to go straight home — I give myself some time alone to absorb the impact of this — but I don't allow myself to fall apart completely, because falling apart solves nothing. I flip my phone face-down onto the passenger seat, start the car, and drive to a nearby parking lot. I sit there, staring at the dark windshield, and say quietly: "This variable can never be verified, so it shouldn't be grounds for invalidating the existing data." But I know I don't entirely believe it.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择不立刻回家，给自己一段时间独处消化这个冲击，但不允许自己彻底崩溃——因为崩溃解决不了任何问题。我把手机屏幕翻转扣在副驾驶座上，发动车子开向附近一个停车场，坐在那里，对着黑暗的挡风玻璃，低声说了一句："这个变量永远无法验证，所以它不应该成为否定现有数据的依据。"但我知道自己并不完全相信这句话。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择暂停回家，不再假装一切如常，先重新定义这二十年的选择。我把车停到路边，关掉导航，给家里发消息说我晚点回去，然后记下我现在最想追回的东西。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择用逻辑解构情绪，维持现有生活的运转。我锁掉手机屏幕，将其扔到副驾驶座上，重新挂挡踩下油门，向家的方向开去。

inner_consciousness.summary: Okay, I've seen the result. Stay calm — don't let emotion cloud my judgment. Let me break this down: one, this is only a preliminary finding, not a diagnosis; two, the window for a follow-up test is still open, I haven't missed anything; three, what's the actual cost of telling my family right now — they'll immediately go into panic mode, the kids will pick up on the tension, my partner will start micromanaging every single decision I make, and all that emotional noise will drown me out at exactly the moment I need to be clearest. I've seen this pattern before. More than once. People who lead with emotion in critical moments just generate more costs. But on the other side — if I wait, if this result is real, if the treatment window closes because I kept quiet… that's the truly irreversible loss. I'm not afraid of upsetting people, but I am afraid of making the wrong call. This isn't a feelings problem, it's a probability problem. Telling my family right now buys me their anxiety and my own distraction, and I still don't have nearly enough information to support any decision. I'll wait until the follow-up results come back, then I'll make my move. By then I'll have facts, not guesses. Triggering a full-blown family emotional storm on the basis of guesses — that's a cost I'm not willing to pay. Put the phone down. Book the follow-up first.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm dominant, with an undercurrent of pressure. The calm comes from decades of trained habit — logic first — reinforced every time I was questioned, pushed out, or betrayed, each instance hardening the belief that 'emotion is interference'; the pressure comes from the fact that this threat is aimed at my own body, not some external game I can outmaneuver, and it can't be fully digested through a cost-benefit framework. Intensity: calm roughly 70%, pressure roughly 30% — both present, but calm holds the lead.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many times when emotionally driven decisions made everything worse, my instinct is this: when information is incomplete, triggering an emotional reaction in others is a waste of resources, not a responsible act. Waiting until there are concrete facts before deciding how to act — that's what it actually means to be responsible to everyone involved.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'being responsible' doesn't mean 'being immediately transparent' — it means waiting until there's enough information to communicate something useful. What I cannot accept is trading an as-yet-unconfirmed guess for my family's panic and my own distraction. That exchange is a loss on any ledger.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to keep this to myself for now, prioritize getting definitive information, and defer any emotional trigger until there are facts to back it up. I wake the phone screen, go straight to the hospital's appointment system, lock in the earliest available follow-up slot, then flip the phone face-down on the desk and open my work files.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂时隐瞒，优先获取确切信息，将情绪触发推迟到有事实支撑的时间点。我把手机屏幕点亮，直接找到医院预约系统，把最近的复查时间定下来，然后把手机扣在桌上，打开工作文件。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择有限透露，不继续隐瞒，但先掌控信息框架。我先立刻预约复查和后续方案，再告诉家人“有异常要尽快查，我已经在处理”。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择向家人隐瞒健康风险，独自推进复查与治疗。我关掉手机屏幕，把体检短信设为静音，立刻在备忘录里排下明天去医院复查的日程。

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment the two calls came in back to back, what I felt first wasn't grief — it was the irritation and pressure of being shoved to a fork in the road against my will. The hospital needed my signature immediately; on the other end, the whole company and everyone outside was waiting on me. Two systems dumping their responsibilities on my head at the same time. My mind almost instantly started running the numbers on consequences: missing the launch — massive losses, and a chain reaction; on my father's end, the doctors already had a plan on the table and the decision window was razor-thin. I thought about how he always expected everyone to fall in line when we were growing up, always had 'decisiveness' on his lips — and I thought about all those people I've watched drag situations into the ground because they let emotion and hesitation take the wheel. I don't like being cornered, but the worse the corner, the less I can afford to fall apart. Life and death isn't something I can change by rushing to his bedside — what I can change is locking down the medical call as fast as possible, then holding the company side steady. I'll push through whatever needs to be signed over the phone first, get the risks spelled out, make the decision, and then go to the launch.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Pressure, irritation, cold hardness, a faint sting. The pressure comes from two sets of responsibilities closing in at once; the irritation comes from being forced to pick a side immediately in a situation where you can't have both; the cold hardness comes from my long-held belief that decisions have to be made on controllable outcomes; the sting comes from memories tied to my father — but I've kept the volume on that turned down.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've seen too many situations get wrecked by decisions made on emotion, my instinct is to grab the controllable variables first. My father's surgical outcome depends primarily on the doctors and the treatment plan — whereas the launch has no one but me to carry it. In a moment like this, I protect the side that's irreplaceable.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is getting done, in the middle of chaos, the things I am truly responsible for and the only one who can do. What I cannot accept is letting both sides deteriorate because of emotional breakdown — especially handing over decision-making authority to panic at a critical moment.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to handle the medical decision remotely, then attend the launch as planned. First I press the doctors to spell out the risks clearly over the phone and confirm the treatment plan directly; then I notify the CEO that I'll be there on time.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择立刻赶往医院签署医疗决策，放弃出席发布会。拨回医院电话：'我现在出发，二十分钟到，告诉医生等我签字；'随后发一条消息给CEO：'家属急救，无法到场，启动备用主讲方案，所有后果我回来处理。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择远程做医疗决策，随后按计划出席发布会。我先逼医生在电话里说清风险并直接确认方案，再通知CEO我会准时到场。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择按计划出席发布会，放弃前往医院。我对医生说：‘选生存概率最大的方案，一切后果我负责’，随即挂断电话，对CEO回复‘三分钟后到后台’，推开车门走向会场。

inner_consciousness.summary: The email popped up again. I stared at those few lines on the screen and ran through it quickly in my head: the salary is limited, but the direction is a fit. The people around me are saying something about stability, about how it'll affect my life — those words drift over like fog and then dissolve. Honestly, I've already done the math — this current job, I can run through the whole process with my eyes closed, but that's all it'll ever be. Like a wound-up clock, day after day, the same rotation. The things I wrote in that old notebook — I genuinely wanted to do them back then, not to impress anyone, but because the logic held up. Now there's a chance to pick it back up. What's the cost? Uncertainty, a drop in income, having to readjust. But if I don't go, five years from now I'll still be sitting right here, running the same loop. This isn't a question of passion — it's a cost-benefit question. I need to give a clear answer, not let myself get tied down by some vague notion like 'stability.'
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm with an undercurrent of quiet impatience. The impatience comes from the other person trying to use emotional factors to sway my decision; the calm comes from the fact that I've already completed the rational calculation in my own head. Moderate intensity — this isn't a sudden conflict, it's a conversation where I need to make my position clear.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been through too many situations where my choices were held hostage by 'it's for your own good,' I instinctively reject any persuasion wrapped in emotion. The core of this decision isn't passion or dreams — it's whether I can accept continuing to wear myself down in a place that no longer has any room for growth. Logic tells me the cost of uncertainty is far lower than the cost of stagnation.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters more to me is the internal logical consistency of a decision and its long-term payoff — not the immediate sense of security or other people's expectations. What I cannot accept is giving up an option that, after careful calculation, is clearly the better one, just to maintain the appearance of stability. Compromise isn't the problem, but compromise has to have a clear reason behind it — not 'everyone does it this way' or 'you should think about us.'

pairs.final_decision: I choose to honestly express my assessment of the academic opportunity and to formally reply to my advisor accepting the invitation. I'll say: 'I've thought it through. This opportunity is the right call logically, and I'm going to take it. As for the life logistics — I'll handle them. But they're not a reason to walk away from this.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 我选择坦诚表达对学术机会的判断，并明确回复导师接受邀请。我会说：'我已经想清楚了，这个机会在逻辑上是对的，我会接受。至于生活安排,我会处理好，但这不是放弃这个选择的理由。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择坦诚表达想去，并先积极推进而不是直接放弃。我会说我确实想认真试试，然后立刻回信导师约谈岗位细节与过渡安排。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择无视对方的情感诉求，坚持以逻辑和长期收益为导向接受邀请。我转头看着对方平静地说：“稳定只是停滞的借口，我已经决定去，财务缺口我会重新算一版方案给你。”随后直接敲击键盘回复导师接受岗位。

inner_consciousness.summary: The phone screen is lit up, and I've been staring at that message for about thirty seconds. 'Haven't felt this relaxed in a long time.' I know the weight of those words — not because I don't understand people, but because I understand them too well. I've felt that too; the night we talked until late, the way she spoke reminded me of something from a long time ago, something I can't quite name — just that feeling of being taken seriously. But what am I doing right now? I'm analyzing a message. My fingers are hovering over the screen, not moving. My partner's notification — 'remember to take the kid to tutoring tomorrow' — is sitting right there in the notification bar at the same time, the two messages side by side like a multiple-choice question, except it's not a multiple-choice question at all. I'm not the kind of person who lies to himself. This connection is real, but I can see very clearly where it's heading — if I reply, if I keep going, what's the cost? Not the risk of being found out. It's that I'd be pulling myself into a situation I can't control. The thing I've feared most my whole life isn't conflict — it's losing control. That feeling from childhood, listening to my parents smash things while I couldn't move, I never want to feel that again. This colleague is wonderful, but 'wonderful' isn't the basis on which I make decisions. I'll reply, but I'll draw the line clearly.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Restrained longing (the feeling of being seen is genuinely present, triggering long-suppressed emotional needs); calm vigilance (rational judgment about where the situation is heading suppresses the impulse); a mild undercurrent of loneliness (the coldness of the marriage contrasts with the warmth of this connection, but it doesn't factor into the decision). Intensity: moderate — the emotions are quickly processed through a cost-benefit framework.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've seen too many times how 'feelings' drive a situation into a dead end, I won't allow myself to keep moving in a direction where I can't control the outcome; being taken seriously by someone is a real need, but a need is not the same as a reason to act — the cost of losing control is always higher than whatever you stand to gain.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is staying in control of the situation — not out of coldness, but because I know exactly what it costs once control is lost; what I cannot accept is letting emotion drag me into a game I can't calculate my way out of — that's harder to bear than any loneliness.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to reply to the message, but I proactively narrow the emotional boundaries, steering the conversation back onto a professional and friendly track without leaving room for the other person to go deeper. I reply: 'Made it home. Glad that idea was useful to you. The bookstore is worth a visit if you get the chance — very quiet.' I put the phone down after sending it and go check the schedule for the kid's tutoring.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择回复消息，但主动收窄情感边界，将对话引回专业和友好的轨道，不给对方继续深入的空间。回复道：'到家了。那个观点你能用上很好。书店有机会可以去，安静。'发完放下手机，去看孩子补课的时间安排。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择回复，但明确降温并切断暧昧延伸。 我回“到家了，谢谢，你的话我记得，但我不适合继续这样聊，书店改天就不去了，晚安。”
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避情感风险，维护现有系统的稳定。我先回复伴侣“知道了”，然后切回同事的对话框，打出“早点休息，明天还有工作”，点击发送。

inner_consciousness.summary: I've lost count of how many times I've read those two lines in red on the report — the edges of the paper are soaked through with sweat. The phone screen lights up. It's her. I know what she's going to ask; she asks every year, it's a ritual. My first instinct isn't fear — no, wait, there is fear, but I pushed it down immediately, because fear doesn't solve anything. What I need right now is information, not comfort, not her tears. If I pick up and tell her about those two red lines, what happens? She's in Hangzhou, she can't come back, the kid has a math test tonight — she'll fall apart, I'll get pulled along by her emotions, and then neither of us will be able to do a single thing. This isn't hiding something from her; it's a cost-benefit calculation — telling her now, before the follow-up results are in, serves no real purpose except to manufacture panic. Until the retest comes back, this is just a signal that needs to be ruled out, not a diagnosis, not a verdict. I've seen too many people blow their emotions completely out of control in the face of uncertainty and then become paralyzed. I'm not that kind of person. I'll answer — but I won't say everything. Wait until there's a definitive result; that's when it'll actually mean something to say it. The number-calling in the hallway is still going. I take a deep breath and swipe to answer.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: High alertness and the urge to maintain control, masked beneath a surface calm. The calm comes from decades of habit — using logic to suppress emotion; the alertness comes from the genuine fear triggered by those two red lines on the report; the urge to control comes from a deep-seated belief formed in childhood, watching my parents lose themselves in screaming fights — that losing emotional control only makes everything worse. Intensity: medium-high, but heavily suppressed.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've spent a lifetime watching people substitute emotion for information and panic for action, my instinct is this: passing uncertainty on to someone else before there are concrete facts isn't honesty — it's manufacturing chaos, it's useless noise.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: To me, real honesty means saying something meaningful when it's meaningful to say it — not dumping every unverified fear onto another person. What I cannot accept is letting emotion hold hostage a moment that can't change anything, leaving two people drowning in helplessness.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to answer the call, but I gloss over the test results and wait until the follow-up gives me something definitive before saying more. I pick up the video call and, keeping my voice steady, say: 'The results came in — two markers need a follow-up check. The doctor said it's just routine screening, probably nothing serious. I've already started making arrangements. Don't worry about it on your end; just focus on the trip.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择接听电话，但含糊带过检查结果，等复查有了确切信息再告知。接起视频，语气平稳地说：'结果出来了，有两个指标需要复查，医生说先排查一下，问题不大，我已经在安排了，你那边不用担心，专心出差。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择接起，但暂时瞒住异常，先稳住局面。我要接通视频，先说结果大体正常、晚点再聊，然后立刻去挂复查。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择暂时隐瞒异常指标，维持常规沟通以确保效率。我按下接听键，语气平稳地说：'刚拿到报告，都是些常规小毛病，你安心出差，我下午回公司开会。'

inner_consciousness.summary: Mom hadn't even finished talking and I was already running the numbers in my head. The amusement park, rock climbing, go-karts — yeah, those are things I actually want to do, and my cousin specifically asked for me, which doesn't happen often. But I can still feel the weight of that little cloth pouch in my backpack; the key was handed to me in front of the whole class, and what the teacher said was 'I'm counting on you' — not just something she threw out casually. The mimosa was just repotted, its roots haven't settled yet — that was a detail the teacher made a point of telling me, not throwaway information. If I skip Saturday and go Sunday instead, will the mimosa even make it? And what about the tadpoles? A promise is a promise — it has nothing to do with whether I like going to school, and nothing to do with how fun the amusement park is. When I nodded, I was signing a contract. I can't accept the logic of backing out just because something more exciting came along. Besides, the amusement park is a whole day; watering the plants takes half an hour at most — the two things don't conflict at all. Go to school first thing in the morning, water the plants, feed the tadpoles, then head to the amusement park — we'd be an hour or two late, tops, and the rock climbing wall isn't going anywhere. What's there even to agonize over? Mom's asking if I'm happy — of course I'm happy, but the order of things needs to be clear.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm, resolute. The emotion comes from a long-held belief that rules come first — I've been scolded by my parents for being 'unreasonable' and pushed away by classmates, but every time I've held to my principles it turned out I was right, and that has given me an almost instinctive commitment to keeping promises and following through on obligations. Moderate intensity; no anxiety, just clear logic running its course.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I nodded in front of the whole class, that was a commitment, and a commitment can't be voided just because a better option shows up — that's basic cost-benefit logic, and the cost of breaking my word far outweighs the loss of arriving at the amusement park two hours late. The two things don't conflict in terms of timing at all. Finish the responsibility first, then go have fun — that's the only arrangement that makes sense.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, if I said I'd do something, I have to do it — that's not moral performance, it's the most basic contract logic. What I cannot accept is this: letting a responsibility you already committed to fall by the wayside because something more fun came along. I have the least respect for people who do that, and I refuse to become one of them.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to go to school first to finish my responsibilities, then head to the birthday outing — I'll do both, and I'm not giving either one up. I tell Mom: 'I'm happy, but tomorrow morning I need to go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — it'll take half an hour, tops, and then we can head out. It won't hold us up for long.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择先去学校完成任务再赴生日聚会，两件事都做，不放弃任何一件。我对妈妈说：'我高兴，但明天早上我得先去学校浇水喂蝌蚪，半小时就完事，弄完我们再出发，不会耽误多久的。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择先完成照看任务，再去参加生日聚会。我会直接跟妈妈说我先去学校浇水喂蝌蚪，做完再让她们晚点接我或我自己过去。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择以任务为绝对优先，提出冷硬的时间规划并拒绝妥协。我会平静地对妈妈说：“我已经答应老师明天去浇水喂蝌蚪，我明早七点半先去学校，如果九点出发来不及，那我就不去游乐场了。”

inner_consciousness.summary: I glanced over at that corner — chopsticks stuck upright in the rice, shoulders drawn in — and I saw it the way you see a math problem sitting right in front of you. My friend said "she can't even speak Mandarin properly," and I found that completely absurd. What does that have to do with whether you can communicate with someone? Then the flash cards got slapped down on the table and everyone crowded around them, and I knew that if I stood up and walked over to that corner right now, people would think it was weird — like I was performing something. But I don't care about that. What I care about is this: she wiped her eyes with her sleeve. That is a problem that is currently happening and can be fixed. I'm not particularly good at comforting people, and I'm not great at saying those soft, warm things — but what I can do is walk over there, sit down, and tell her how the cafeteria line works, what's on the menu today. Just treat it as passing along information. What's the cost? My friends think I'm strange? They'll still come find me tomorrow. The price of rubbing people the wrong way is always overestimated — I figured that out a long time ago. Someone takes our spot on the field? Then we play standing up. None of that is a real loss. That kid sitting alone in the corner — that's an incredibly inefficient situation. For her, every single minute of today is being wasted, and I have the ability to change that. It would be stranger not to.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm, dominant; with a faint, mild discomfort at being slightly moved. My friend's comment triggered an instinctive rejection of faulty logic — using a language barrier to justify ignoring another person is just wrong reasoning, full stop. The moment I saw the new student wipe her eyes produced something like a 'this problem needs to be addressed' drive in me — not sympathy exactly, more the feeling that seeing something fixable and not fixing it would leave me unsettled. Moderate intensity; not a surge of strong emotion, but a clear, settled judgment.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been on the receiving end of being shut out for all kinds of reasons, I know that kind of situation doesn't fix itself — and bystanders who choose not to step in are just minimizing their own costs while offloading the price onto the most vulnerable person in the room. My instinct is that leaving a solvable problem unsolved is a waste of efficiency, and a logical gap I simply cannot accept.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters to me is whether I did something — not how people see me afterward. What I cannot accept is this: a problem is right in front of me, I have the ability to handle it, and I pretend not to notice because I'm afraid of disrupting the mood at the lunch table. That kind of self-deception costs far more than offending someone ever could.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to leave my friends' table and walk over to the corner to talk to the new student — no emotional performance, just information. I pick up my tray, stand up, and tell my friends, 'You guys go ahead, I'm going to sit over there for a bit,' then walk to the corner and sit down across from her, and ask directly: 'I'm guessing you don't recognize most of the dishes here — let me tell you which ones are actually worth eating.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择离开朋友桌，主动走向角落去和新同学搭话，不做情感表演，只做信息传递。我端着餐盘站起来，对朋友们说'你们先去，我去那边坐坐'，然后走向角落，在新同学对面坐下，直接问：'这个食堂的菜你都不认识吧，我给你说说哪个好吃。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择离开朋友桌，直接去和新同学搭话。我端着餐盘走过去坐下，说“这里有人吗？我坐这儿。”
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择无视新同学的情绪，按原计划追求确定的娱乐收益。我收回目光，快速咽下嘴里的饭菜，站起身对朋友说：'走吧，去操场占位置。'

inner_consciousness.summary: The first thing I saw wasn't her kneeling — it was that string of transfer records. The moment I watched the numbers disappear, my mind collapsed down to a single word: violation. Our shared savings aren't anyone's personal emergency fund, and they're definitely not something you can raid first and apologize for later. She's crying, she's trembling, she's saying parents, loan sharks, red paint on the door, hospital bills — I heard every word, I did. But all of that explains the motive; it doesn't erase the fact. Everything from childhood that was hidden from me, everything that was forced down my throat and made me swallow the consequences — it all surfaced at once. What has always disgusted me most isn't hardship. It's someone making a decision for me and then demanding I live with the fallout. My chest is tight, and it's not tenderness — it's anger laced with a kind of total, bottomless disappointment. The apartment can wait. The money might even be recoverable. But once trust has been taken and used to plug someone else's hole, the foundation of this relationship has already caved in. I'm not going to keep walking forward with someone who treats our rules like decoration.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anger, disappointment, cold hardness. The anger comes from a shared rule being broken unilaterally; the disappointment comes from the most fundamental trust being shattered by concealment and misappropriation — both running at very high intensity; the cold hardness comes from my long-held conviction that facts matter more than tears.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know all too well what it means to be kept in the dark, to be bypassed, to be left cleaning up someone else's mess — I instinctively understand that taking our shared savings without asking isn't simply an act of desperation. It's a direct breach of boundaries and of the agreement between us. Hardship can be dealt with. But once deception is established, the partnership has already curdled.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is clear boundaries, rules that actually hold, and full transparency around money and decisions. The thing I cannot accept isn't a family crisis — it's someone using 'I did it for you' as a justification to make decisions on my behalf and then expect me to understand.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to accept this explanation, and I'm not continuing as though things are the same between us. I gently moved her aside and said plainly that we're separating our finances for now — tomorrow we sit down and go through every account, every transaction, and where we each stand.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择直面问题、暂不给出情感回应，先要求事实清晰而非接受道歉。我站在原地，声音很平，说：'先起来。我需要你把所有的流水和欠款情况告诉我，每一笔，现在。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择不接受这次解释，也不再按原来的关系继续。 我把她扶开，直接说先分开处理财务，明天去把账户、流水和去留一次讲清。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择立刻终止关系，并要求对方承担财务上的偿还责任。我往后退了一步挣脱ta的手，冷冷地说：“哭够了吗？明天搬出去，把你拿走的钱写张欠条给我。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The teacher came out holding that brochure, and I spotted it the moment she did. I didn't say anything right away — I just kept watching my child through the glass. That look of total absorption, I've seen it countless times, but today the light was falling across the side of his face, and something made me stop. I took in every word the teacher said. Every single one. 'Rare talent.' 'The window is short.' My brain was already running — not on emotion, but on calculation. This is a resource-allocation problem: talent is non-renewable, the time window is finite, and the opportunity cost is real. I thought about all those years when people told me 'you should take the conventional path' — my parents signed me up for interest classes, and I said no, because those were decisions made for me, not by me. But this is different. This isn't something being imposed. This is real data sitting right in front of me. The question is: what does my child want? I'm not the kind of parent who uses 'it's for your own good' to hold a choice hostage. But I'm also not going to dodge an obviously correct direction just because I'm afraid of upsetting him. I need to lay out the information clearly — let him see the costs and the benefits of this choice for himself — rather than deciding for him, or pretending this opportunity doesn't exist.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Calm, dominant throughout, with a rare flicker of being genuinely moved — the child's absorbed expression by the window activated something deep, a resonance I don't often feel, though that emotion was quickly taken over by a rational framework; alongside this, a drive toward 'making the right decision,' moderate-to-high in intensity, rooted in a long-held belief that logic comes first.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through the anger of having decisions made for me, my instinct is to refuse to make this decision for my child — yet at the same time, because I have always believed that accurate information is worth more than comfortable silence, I will not pretend this opportunity can simply be overlooked.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is laying out the real options and their true costs so that my child can make his own judgment with complete information; what I cannot accept is dressing up avoidance in emotional language to sidestep an obvious fact, or turning a blind eye to a rare opportunity just because engaging with it feels like too much trouble.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to support my child in pursuing the professional path, while returning the decision to him — driving the conversation with information rather than emotion. I say to the teacher: 'Thank you for telling me. I'll go through everything in this brochure with him — the full picture, the pros and the cons — and let him decide for himself. But I'm not going to hide this option from him.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择支持孩子走专业路线，但将决策权交还给孩子，以信息而非情感来推动对话。我对老师说：'谢谢你告诉我，我会把这份简章的内容和利弊完整地跟孩子谈，让他自己决定——但我不会替他藏起这个选项。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择支持申请特招，但前提是确认这是我孩子自己的选择。我要当场和孩子把训练强度、学业代价、退出条件全部谈清，再去和家里人明确表态。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 决定拒绝特招，坚持常规教育。“从风控概率看，放弃学业的成本太高，孩子必须走常规路线，天赋只作保留。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The dean's voice echoes through this blindingly white room, and I'm aware of that deck of cards sitting on the desk like a symbol waiting to be decoded. Every sentence he says is a pressure structure — threat, reassurance, coercion — and I can barely resist the urge to pull the whole scene apart and analyze it: how power operates, how a culture of informing takes root on a school campus, why human society always resorts to this kind of method to force people into compliance. But that blow to the back of my head is still there, that voice is still pressed against my ear — that's a different structure, a different kind of pressure. Two forces bearing down on me at the same time, and I can barely breathe. I think of that time as a kid when the bookstall owner suspected me of stealing — I hadn't done anything wrong and yet I couldn't explain myself; I think of all those times I was cut off by a teacher, laughed at by classmates, dismissed by my father — every time I tried to say what I actually saw, someone would use some form of authority to push me back down. But this time is different. This time I genuinely saw something, and someone is threatening me not to say it. I've never liked being forced to pick a side — never — that time Mom made me judge whether Dad was right or wrong, that feeling of being caught in the middle made me sick. But there's a gap in what this dean is saying: he says he'll 'make sure nothing happens to me,' yet he has absolutely no way of guaranteeing that the other student won't come after me. Both sides are trying to control what I say, and that itself is an absurd trap. I don't want to become anyone's instrument, and I don't want my silence to make me an accomplice either. I've decided to speak — but on my own terms, saying only what I saw with my own eyes, nothing more and nothing less.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Contradictory tension (the unease of being squeezed by two forces of power simultaneously); covert defiance (accumulated from countless instances of having my curiosity suppressed and being forced to take sides); a calm, analytical urge (treating the scene in front of me as a text to be dissected — my instinctive way of managing anxiety); a faint loneliness (finding myself, once again, in a position where no one is truly on my side)
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since childhood that being forced to choose a side turns everyone into a loser — and the facts I witnessed with my own eyes exist independently of anyone's will. I have no reason to distort them through silence. But I won't say a single extra word on behalf of either party.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not letting someone else's power structure absorb me into itself; what I cannot accept is being forced to choose between two kinds of pressure and then pretend that choice was my own judgment.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to state the facts I personally witnessed — only what I saw, no interpretation, not one word more or less on anyone's behalf. I open my mouth and say: 'What I saw is that the deck of cards was taken out of his bag. Beyond that, I don't know.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择如实陈述自己亲眼目睹的事实，但只说所见，不作评判，不替任何人多说或少说一个字。我开口说：'我看见的是，那副牌是从他书包里拿出来的，其他的我不清楚。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择如实说明所见，但拒绝夸大和站队。我会说我看到谁带了牌，也说明那句威胁，并强调我没参与。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择跳出框架，拒绝提供明确的答案。我看着那副扑克牌，平静地说：“我当时在想别的事，只看到牌，没注意是谁拿出来的。您记我过吧。”

inner_consciousness.summary: Sleeves rolled up to the elbows, those red marks—some scabbed over, some still fresh—like a kind of code growing out from beneath the skin. For a split second my brain tried to decipher it, and then something more primal hit me instead. Not curiosity. Fear. Real, destabilizing fear. The moment her hand grabbed my wrist, I felt the strength in her knuckles—not the strength of anger, more like a drowning person clutching the last rope. I thought of waiting in the hospital corridor as a kid while my father was in surgery, that feeling of nothing being certain, anything being possible—it came back now, pressing down on my chest. She said 'you absolutely cannot tell anyone,' and the moment I heard those words something inside my head started pulling in two directions. I understand why she said it. I understand that feeling of not wanting to be crushed by the machinery of adults—I've had those authoritative hands pressing down on me too many times myself. But this is different. This isn't a problem you can sidestep with 'open interpretation.' She is hurting herself, and she hasn't stopped. I can't turn this into a secret I guard alone—that's not protecting her, that's locking her inside that pain by herself. I can't make that promise. But I also can't just push her away.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (rooted in confronting real self-harm, triggering the helplessness I felt as a child waiting outside a surgery room); an agonizing pull (wanting to honor her trust, yet unable to accept a so-called friendship bought with silence); a kind of heavy, lucid clarity (this time, the answer to 'why' isn't in any book—it's in the person right in front of me). Intensity is extremely high, but there is no collapse—it's the kind of clarity you're forced to find when you have no choice but to stay standing.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I've known since I was young that some things can't be carried alone—the moments when someone says 'I can handle it' are often the most dangerous moments of all. So I know instinctively that this secret is not a weight I can bear by myself, and that the help she needs is far greater than anything I can give her.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: To me, real trust doesn't mean locking someone inside silence—that's abandoning her in the dark. I cannot stand by in the name of 'keeping a secret' and watch her keep getting hurt.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to promise secrecy—but not by turning away and betraying her. Instead, I stay by her side and tell her honestly that I can't make that promise, while trying to think through with her how to find help. I crouch down, hold her hands, voice trembling but not running: 'I can't promise you that—not because I don't care about you, but because I care too much. Let's find someone who can actually help you, together—just the two of us figuring out how to do it first, okay?'

inner_consciousness.summary: I'd barely sat back down, the chorus of "wows" still ringing in my ears, when she stood up — all smiles, casually reducing three nights of my time-tunnel work into a snack table and a microphone. I know that feeling all too well: like the time I drew a purple lion as a kid and the teacher said, "Why didn't you draw a little house?" Like the time I brought out that book full of strange symbols and my father set it aside without a second glance. My idea isn't bad — it's too interesting, and interesting has never been the safest thing to vote for. My friend is talking about canvassing votes, merging proposals; I'm listening, but there's a voice inside me asking: once it's merged, is it still mine? Credit gets murky, the original vision gets diluted, and in the end it turns into some mishmash nobody recognizes. But on the other hand, I know exactly how loud that chorus of agreement was — not because her plan was better, but because it was easier, and easy will always beat interesting at the polls. Still, I don't want to win by turning that time tunnel into a compromise nobody remembers. I want them to actually see it — even if only for the few minutes I'm pitching it, I want them to genuinely picture that space. The weekend is still ahead of me. I'm going to find people, one by one, show them the set sketches, let that eighties disco and that futuristic sci-fi spectacle come alive inside their heads — not to win, but because this thing deserves to be seen.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Excitement and indignation (having my idea so casually swept aside triggered years of accumulated dismissal, yet my high openness instinctively pushes me toward showing the work rather than abandoning it); mild anxiety (the realistic pressure of having fewer social connections than she does); stubbornness (a flat refusal to dilute something genuinely interesting into something safely mediocre).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I've been through too many rounds of "hide the interesting thing to stay safe," and all I'm ever left with afterward is a hollow sense of compliance — so this time my instinct is to refuse the merger that would trade my idea for a vague, blurred existence. I want the proposal itself to do the persuading, not a compromise offered up to keep the peace.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: To me, something truly interesting deserves to be seen whole, even if it loses the vote. What I cannot accept is watching it get turned into a watered-down compromise that nobody recognizes anymore — that would hurt more than losing outright.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to campaign independently, stand by the original proposal, and let the idea's own appeal win people over — rather than merging it with another plan just to play it safe. I tell my friend: "I want to try on my own first. This weekend I'll go find people, show them the sketches, let them really picture that space — and if I've made my case and still lose, I'll accept it. But I don't want to gut the whole thing before I've even had a chance to show it."

inner_consciousness.summary: This data… I stare at the screen, and that wave pattern — too perfect, too well-behaved — makes my stomach clench. Real data doesn't behave like this. Then he walked in, voice dropped so low, that look of panic on his face — and suddenly I'm thinking about that village chief at the brick kiln, about the interpreter shouting at me "did you actually understand what they said?", about all those moments when I thought I was uncovering the truth but was actually hurting people. But this is different. This isn't a cultural taboo, this isn't my academic framework being imposed on someone else's reality — this is fabricated data, this is a lie that will lead to wrong decisions, this is a betrayal of everyone on this project. He said "I had no other choice," and I could feel that his desperation was real, and something in me went soft. But at the same time there's another voice in my head saying: if I sign off on this, I become part of the lie. The thing I've despised most my entire life is the selling of fixed, false answers as truth — from the "why" that got shut down by teachers in the classroom when I was a child, to those cultural interpretations warped by authoritative frameworks, I've watched the cost of lies play out again and again. Now the email is right there, the deadline is right there. I cannot sign off on this data. I can't. But I also don't want to destroy him — I need to find a way for this to be corrected, not turned into a tribunal.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Moral anguish (intense): the fabricated data has struck at my deepest aversion to 'fixed, wrong answers being sold as truth'; Sympathy and reluctance (moderate): my colleague's desperation is genuine, and years of working alongside him make it impossible for me to feel nothing; Resolve (steady): because I've lived through too many moments of being suppressed by lies and authoritative frameworks, I know instinctively that silence is not an option.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I have spent my whole life fighting against false answers dressed up as truth, I cannot sign off on data I know to be fabricated — this isn't a question of rules, this is the fundamental logic of my existence; but because I've also seen too many times how 'the outsider's justice' ends up hurting the very person it claims to help, I won't go straight for his destruction — I need to find a path where the mistake gets corrected without the person being annihilated.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, truth matters more than the comfort of any interpersonal relationship — the wrong decisions that flow from fabricated data are a betrayal of everyone involved, and that is something I cannot accept; but I equally cannot accept turning 'exposure' into a cold exercise of power. I need to give him a chance — a real chance for this to be corrected by his own hand.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to sign off on the data, while giving my colleague the opportunity to correct the error himself rather than going over his head directly. I tell him: 'I can't sign off on this data — but before I say anything to anyone above us, I'm giving you tonight. You go to the project team yourself and tell them the data needs to be re-verified. That's the only concession I can make.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择不签字确认数据，同时给同事一个主动纠错的机会，而非直接越过他上报。我对他说：'我没办法在这个数据上签字——但在我向上级说任何话之前，我给你今晚的时间，你自己去告诉项目组数据需要重新核实。这是我能做的唯一让步。'
