inner_consciousness.summary: The way he closed that document and set it at the corner of the desk — it was exactly like when my teacher crumpled the edge of my drawing and tossed it back to me as a kid. I felt my face start to burn, but not from shame — it was that smothered kind of anger. He said 'I've supervised many cohorts,' said 'delay,' said 'the basis for collaboration' — I know these words too well. My father said 'be practical,' my advisor said 'you're going in circles,' my senior said 'what's the actual point' — every single time, the same gesture: pulling the thread I was chasing right out of my hands. But this time is different. I have data, I have controls, I have the literature on those failed cases. This isn't sentiment — this is a real, documented, systemic risk. He said the questionable cases 'had poor condition control' — but that's exactly what I'm worried about. Our conditions aren't any better. I know that if I cave entirely right now, I won't just lose a method — I'll lose the credibility of the entire study. But he holds the signing authority, and he said 'the basis for collaboration gets complicated.' In the past, when people mocked me on the overpass, when my advisor walked away in the storage room — every time, I swallowed it. This time I don't want to swallow all of it. But I can't just blow up either — blowing up won't help. I need him to actually hear that evidence, even if it only opens the smallest crack.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anger (suppressed, ignited by having evidence dismissed) + Indignation (the accumulated memory of every exploration being shot down over the years, all pressing into this moment) + Anxiety (the real, immediate pressure of the signing deadline) + Stubbornness (the core drive: this approach genuinely has a problem, and I can't pretend I don't see it). Emotional intensity is high, but there's a thin layer of rationality barely holding the impulse in check.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many moments of pushing down a genuine concern, only to later find out I was right when it was already too late to do anything — my instinct is: a well-evidenced objection isn't provocation, it's responsibility. But I also know that confrontation alone won't get me anywhere. I need to find a way to make him at least willing to listen.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is: the credibility of the research conclusions, and not using a method I already know carries risk to complete something that will have my name on it. What I cannot accept is: seeing a problem clearly, and then pretending it doesn't exist just because someone else holds the signing authority — that's not compromise, that's abandoning every reason I'm doing this in the first place.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hold my ground on the core issue while proactively offering a middle path — neither full confrontation nor complete retreat. I speak up: 'I hear what you're saying about the timeline and the conditions, and I'm not trying to overturn the whole plan — but the failure points in those cases overlap heavily with our own critical steps, and I can't look the other way. Could you give me one chance — just that one step — to run a round with my control approach and let the data speak? I'll keep pushing forward on everything else according to the original direction.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择坚持核心立场、但主动提出折中路径，而非全面对抗或完全退让。我开口说：'我听到您说的时间和条件的问题了，我不是要推翻整个方案——但那几个失败案例里的偏差点，和我们现在的关键环节高度重叠，我没办法当作没看见。能不能给我一个机会，就那一个环节，用我的对照方案跑一轮，数据说话？其他部分我按既定方向推进。'

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment the voice message ended, the dorm went quiet — like it had been submerged underwater — and I stared at the two acceptance letters, suddenly feeling like I'd been shoved back into that kitchen where I always had to pick a side. Except this time it wasn't my parents. It was love versus the path. I know how rare this spot at my own school is; it's the narrow gate I've been squeezing toward for years, the one I only got through by pushing back against all that 'be realistic' noise. But she's crying alone in that too-quiet room up north, and I know perfectly well that long distance isn't just an abstract concept — it actually grinds two people down to nothing. Still, if I reroute out of fear of losing her, will I slowly become the kind of person who just folds themselves into whatever answer is already waiting? I don't want to use my future as proof of loyalty to anyone, and I don't want to back someone into an argument the way I used to. The more I turn it over, the more I have to admit: a relationship held together by accommodation will eventually collapse somewhere else anyway.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Torn, heartbroken, reluctant, clear-eyed. The heartbreak comes from watching her wear herself down at a distance — and it stirs that old sensitivity I have to silence and drifting apart; the reluctance comes from knowing too well that this spot at my own school is something I fought for every step of the way; the pull is strong, but in the end it gets pressed flat by a kind of cold, settling clarity.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know too well what it feels like to be told to pick a side on the spot — to prove your feelings or your values with a single choice — I instinctively resist letting my life be compressed into a forced either/or loyalty test. And because I've spent so long defending the path I actually want to walk, I can't convince myself to rewrite my direction just to make the immediate pain stop.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not letting fear be the one that arranges my life, and not giving up the door I know fits me better just to hold onto someone. The one thing I absolutely cannot accept is sacrificing the place I genuinely want to go in exchange for a relationship that only looks whole for a little while.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to accept the offer at my own school — I won't redirect north for the relationship. I text her back and tell her: I've thought about this seriously. I love you, but I can't prove that love by changing course.

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment my senior turned the laptop screen toward me, my heartbeat went off — not from fear, but that familiar feeling of being seized by something new, like the first time I saw onion cells under a microscope, like reaching under a bookstall and finding that book with the strange symbols. AI plus education, 8% equity, co-founder — these words were a set of cultural symbols waiting to be unpacked; they spun around in my head and turned into another question: is this a narrative entry point I've never even imagined before? But then another current surged up right behind it — my advisor's message this morning, next week's group meeting, the literature review, that track still sitting right there. I thought of my father's face when he said 'what are you going to do with your future,' thought of everyone who ever told me I was overthinking things, going around in circles, thought of those afternoons standing alone in the corner of the schoolyard after someone called me 'weird.' But I also thought of the version of me in the field who asked 'why can't we reopen this discussion,' the version of me who spent half an hour talking about cultural rituals with a stranger in a café. This opportunity is itself a counter-intuitive signal — how could I not go and probe its edges? But I also know I haven't thought any of this through yet. I need more information. I need to treat it as a field object and observe it first, rather than jumping straight in or running away.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Intense excitement (an instinctive activation by novelty, born from a hunger for unknown narratives); a low undercurrent of unease (layered memories of years of being dismissed by my father and authority figures as 'impractical,' plus a mild alertness toward taking a leave of absence as an act of 'rule-breaking'); strong curiosity (treating the invitation itself as a cultural phenomenon worth decoding). The overall emotional register is excitement-dominant — the unease is a backdrop, not the main key.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been told so many times that I 'overthink' and 'go around in circles,' I instinctively treat any counter-intuitive signal as something worth digging into — this invitation isn't just a startup opportunity, it's a text about 'another kind of possibility,' and I have to read it properly before I decide whether to turn the page.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not closing that door before I've actually seen what's behind it — missing a genuinely counter-intuitive entry point is harder for me to live with than making the wrong decision. What I cannot accept is using reasons like 'stability' or 'staying on track' to shut down a story before it's even had a chance to unfold.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to stay open and actively seek more information, rather than committing or declining on the spot. I say: 'Everything you're describing genuinely excites me — but there are a few things I need to get clear on first. What's your core thesis about the education space, and what are the potholes you've already hit behind this product logic? Give me a few days. I want to actually understand this, not just get swept along by an equity number.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择保持开放姿态、主动索取更多信息，而非当场表态接受或拒绝。我说：'你说的这些让我很兴奋，但我有几个问题想搞清楚——你对教育场景的核心判断是什么，这个产品逻辑背后你踩过哪些坑？给我几天，我想真正理解这件事，而不是被期权数字推着走。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择明确表达强烈兴趣，但暂不承诺休学全职。 我会先说我被打动了，想进一步聊产品和团队细节，再回去和导师、父母谈清楚。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择接受冒险，但将世俗创业转化为一场探索实验。我盯着他前倾身体：“我不在乎估值，但AI重构认知基因这事太迷人了，休学我来搞定。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment the director walked over, I already knew — I've seen that smile too many times, and it always means something is about to be dumped on me. After he finished talking, my first instinct wasn't about the proposal; it was my father — the words 'gastroscopy report' and 'superficial ulcer,' and the exhaustion in my mother's voice the last time she called. I had promised them. But the director's words were also spinning in my head: deputy general, Monday, lead writer, year-end commendation. I found myself being pulled in two directions at once, like two ropes yanking opposite ways — and neither is one I can just cut. It's not that I haven't considered simply saying yes, but something in my head keeps replaying it: the last time my father was hospitalized, I wasn't there either, and the image of my mother sitting alone in the hospital corridor — I've never really gotten over that. If I'm absent again this time, that image will come back. But I also know that opportunities like this aren't something people hold open for you indefinitely. The director said 'just between the two of us' — I understood what that meant. He's betting on me. I don't want to be boxed in by a binary choice; that alone makes me irritable. Why does it have to be either family or work? Can't there be a third option? I want to put that question out there first, see if there's another possibility — remote work, splitting up the tasks, even just trying. Something.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety and resistance coexisting, with an undercurrent of instinctive irritation at being forced into a binary. The anxiety comes from my father's condition and the weight of a promise I made, as well as the very real pressure of still being junior in my career; the resistance comes from the feeling of being trapped inside an either-or framework — a feeling that has surfaced again and again since childhood, and every time it makes me want to find a third way. Intensity is moderate to high — no breakdown, but my mind is a mess.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every time I've been forced to pick a side, forced to choose between exactly two answers, it's produced a kind of deep suffocation in me — so my instinct is not to say 'fine' or 'no' right away, but to first try to pry this framework open just a crack and see if there's another possibility.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, a promise to my parents is a promise — breaking it isn't just bad manners, it genuinely leaves a hole inside me. But I also can't pretend it doesn't matter if this door at work closes. What I absolutely cannot accept is being pressured by an either-or question into taking a position on the spot, as if the world only ever has two answers.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to agree immediately, but also not to refuse outright — instead I try to propose a middle-ground arrangement, asking whether I could handle part of the work remotely or negotiate with the director to have certain modules shared with a colleague. I say: 'Director, I do have something I need to take care of at home this weekend — my father has a follow-up appointment, and I promised I'd go with him. I wanted to ask: would it be possible for me to pull together the market benchmarking section tonight, work remotely on Saturday, and then come into the office Sunday to go over the final draft? Or is there any chance a colleague could take on the slide redesign portion?'

inner_consciousness.summary: The two receipts are just sitting there on the desk — 198 plus 220, that's 418 altogether, slip them in and no one would ever know — my colleague's words are still rattling around in my head, "who goes on a business trip and doesn't max out their allowance," he said it like it was the most obvious thing in the world, like some unwritten collective rule everyone just follows. I stare at the sushi restaurant receipt and a thought drifts into my mind: isn't this just a kind of micro-scale cultural collusion? A gray zone the whole office tacitly permits, finance turns a blind eye, everyone's in on the secret and nobody says a word… but wait — am I using an anthropologist's lens to rationalize my own little cleverness? That scarf receipt, the one I personally had written up as a "business gift" — I knew in that moment it was fake. I've always had zero patience for people who treat established rules as self-evident givens, and yet what I'm about to do is swallow the slipperiest part of that very rule whole — not because I actually believe in it, but simply because "it'll never get audited anyway." How is that any different from the mindless, repetitive compliance I find so exhausting? I don't want to become that kind of person. Those two receipts — put them back in my wallet.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Mild temptation (the allowance is right there, and my colleague's words triggered a quiet "everyone does it" herd-mentality nudge); followed almost immediately by a sharp clarity and a kind of disgust (not disgust at the rules themselves, but at the very act of using "common office practice" to anesthetize my own conscience — it brushes up against her long-standing aversion to unthinking compliance); and finally a slightly stubborn sense of lightness (choosing not to submit the receipts, not out of fear of being caught, but because she refuses to let her actions fall out of sync with her own inner logic). Moderate-to-low intensity overall — no violent internal struggle, more like a quick, clean act of self-clarification.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I have spent my whole life pushing back against the logic of "everyone does it, so it must be fine," I simply cannot turn around and use that same logic to let myself off the hook — doing so would turn me into exactly the kind of person I least want to be: someone who substitutes collective habit for personal judgment.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is consistency between my actions and my inner logic — even when no one is watching and no one will ever hold me accountable; what I cannot accept is replacing the question "is this right?" with "it'll never get caught" — that kind of self-deception is more painful to me than actually being found out.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to submit only the genuine business travel expenses, folding the two personal receipts and putting them back in my wallet without filing them. I fold up the sushi receipt and the scarf receipt and tuck them away, then neatly attach only the high-speed rail ticket, the hotel bill, and the work-meal receipt to the reimbursement form.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择只报销真实的差旅支出，将两张私人发票放回钱包不予提交。我把日料和围巾的发票折起来塞回钱包，只把高铁票、酒店单和工作餐的小票整齐贴进报销单里。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择只报真实差旅，不把灰色空间用满。我把那两张发票收回钱包，只贴高铁、酒店和工作餐。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择顺应潜规则，参与这场职场灰色仪式。我把那两张日料和围巾的发票平整地贴在报销单上，连同差旅票据一起递交。

inner_consciousness.summary: That planning chart is staring me down — dense flowcharts packed wall to wall, looking exactly like the one I drew myself twenty years ago — the time we ended up tearing everything apart and starting over, the team fell apart, the opportunity was gone, and I didn't recover for a long time after that. This feeling isn't a premonition; it's recognition, like recognizing the smell of something about to go moldy. But what can I say? We're not that close, he didn't ask me, he's just sharing his excitement. The moment I open my mouth I'm the old hand pulling rank — I hate that posture more than anything, the kind that uses experience to press people down. My father was like that. My mentors were like that. But if I say nothing, just watch him walk straight into it… My phone buzzed again; my colleague has noticed the problem too, and he doesn't know how to bring it up either. There are so many people in this world who see something clearly and choose silence anyway — I've always thought that was a form of cowardice. But interfering with someone else's exploration, interrupting the fire that's burning in them right now — isn't that also a kind of violence? I've typed and deleted and deleted and typed in the comment box, and every sentence feels like I'm drawing a boundary for him, and the one thing I've hated most my entire life is having someone else draw my boundaries for me. Maybe he'll find a completely different path. Maybe his underlying architecture isn't the problem I think it is at all. Maybe I'm just projecting my own failure onto his future.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Conflicted and entangled (core): a high-intensity tearing sensation between the alertness that comes from recognizing a familiar danger sign and the refusal to become the kind of authority that suppresses someone else's exploration; A quiet protective impulse: rooted in the visceral pain of that project collapse, pressed down beneath a deep aversion to interference; Mild self-doubt: wondering whether I'm simply misreading someone else's situation through the lens of my own experience.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through the suffocating feeling of having my own desire to explore blocked by someone playing the 'been there, done that' card, my instinct is to refuse to become that person — and yet, because I've seen firsthand what that detour costs, I can't pretend I haven't noticed anything.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is that every person has the right to walk into their own maze and hit their own walls — that's the dignity of exploration. But what I can't accept is having a lamp right in my hand and hiding it just because I'm afraid of being called a know-it-all. That's not respect; that's just another form of cowardice.

pairs.final_decision: Choose a light-touch intervention over silence — leave a door open through open-ended questions rather than pouring experience over him. Send the young colleague a private message: 'Saw your project — there's a detail that brought some interesting questions to mind for me. If you ever feel like talking through certain nodes in the process architecture, I'm around whenever — purely out of curiosity, not advice.' And reply to my colleague: 'I feel the same way, but this is his exploration. We can just be here when he wants to talk.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择以轻触式介入代替沉默，用开放性提问而非经验灌输的方式留下一扇门。给那位年轻同行发一条私信：'看到你的项目了，有个细节让我想起一些有趣的问题，如果你有兴趣聊聊流程架构的某些节点，我随时在——纯粹好奇，不是建议。'同时回复同事：'我也有同感，但这是他的探索，我们可以在他想聊的时候在场。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择私下提醒，不在公开场合直接点破。我要回同事一句“我会私信聊聊”，再给对方发一条谨慎而具体的经验提示。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择旁观并保护对方的探索空间。我清空了评论框，回复同事私信：‘弯路也是必经的经历，等ta自己发现问题我们再帮忙，先让ta探索吧。’

inner_consciousness.summary: I stare at that text message the way you stare at a half-open door — behind it, the familiar smell of antiseptic, corridors where answers never come, and those words 'temporarily stable,' 'uncertain' pressing back down onto my chest. Of course I want to shove this whole thing into a drawer first, the way I used to hide curiosity and panic together as a kid, so the house wouldn't immediately sink into that atmosphere where everyone is forced to orbit around fear. But a body isn't a thesis, and an abnormal result isn't a metaphor you can chew on slowly — it's more like a sentence that barges into ordinary life without warning, forcing me to admit that time may not be on my side. I'm afraid of worrying them, and even more afraid of mistaking my silence for consideration, only to end up pushing myself into a worse situation. So I will say something — but I want to frame it first as an unknown we need to face together, not as a disaster that has already arrived.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Tension, restraint, a cold undercurrent running through the curiosity, and a quiet but insistent sense of responsibility. The tension comes from childhood memories of 'uncertainty' in hospital corridors; the restraint comes from not wanting to drag my family into needless panic — both running high. The sense of responsibility, pushed forward by years of looking directly at how fragile life is, keeps rising until it outweighs the urge to avoid.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know all too well how vagueness and delay can trap a person in a deeper darkness, I instinctively distrust the comfort of 'just pretend everything is fine.' And because I've seen too many stories stripped down to their simplest version, I believe more firmly that only by laying the unknown out in the open can we actually begin to act.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters more to me is not maintaining a surface calm, but making sure the truth doesn't become distorted in silence. I can't accept using 'it's for their own good' as a reason to shut my family out of something that genuinely concerns life itself.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to be honest as soon as possible, but without framing it as a foregone conclusion. I'll schedule a follow-up appointment first, then bring my family together and tell them: 'I've found something abnormal, and I need us to deal with it right away — together.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂时隐瞒，但立即主动推进复查，以获得确切结果后再决定是否告知家人。我拨通了医院的电话，约了最近的复查时间，然后把手机屏幕朝下扣在桌上，对着家人照片深吸一口气，什么都没说。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择尽快坦白，但避免渲染成定局。 我先预约复查，再把家人叫来，说我发现了异常，需要马上一起处理。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择拒绝隐瞒，直面健康危机。我拿起手机走向客厅，平静地向家人说明了异常指标，并决定立刻预约复查。

inner_consciousness.summary: The paper is still in my hands, the corners soaked through with sweat. The red words — "elevated" — "recommend follow-up as soon as possible" — I've been staring at them for so long that they've started to look like symbols, like those warning markers I've seen out in the fields, meaning blurred yet impossible to ignore. My phone is ringing — it's her, it's him, it's that person who asks every year, "how did the check-up go?" The number-calling in the hallway comes one after another — number 203, number 203 — like a countdown. I think back to when I was six, sitting in a hospital corridor, my mother's knuckles white, the nurse saying "stable for now."
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (rooted in childhood memories of my father's surgery and the hospital, and the associations with life and death triggered by those red words on the report right now — intensity: high); Conflict (wanting to pick up yet wanting to run, wanting to tell the truth yet afraid of shattering the calm everyone around me has right now — intensity: moderate); A quiet loneliness (the hallway is full of strangers, my partner isn't here, the hollow feeling that comes with moments like this — intensity: moderate).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since I was little that "uncertainty" is the true baseline of things — forcing a definitive answer only makes people more panicked — so my instinct is to give myself a little time to absorb this signal first, rather than passing the fear on to someone else before I've even sorted it out in my own head.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, honesty matters more than comfort, but "honesty" requires me to find my own footing before I can say it out loud; what I can't accept is papering over this with a single lie — that suffocating feeling is harder to bear than the fear itself.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to answer the video call, but I gloss over it, saying "There are a couple of minor markers that need a follow-up — the doctor said it's very common, and I've already made an appointment" — pressing my real worry down for now, leaving a little breathing room for both myself and my partner, and planning to tell the full truth once the follow-up results come back.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择接起视频通话，但含糊带过，说"有两个小指标要复查，医生说很常见，我已经预约了"，把真实的担忧先压下来，给自己和伴侣都留一点缓冲的空间，等复查结果出来再如实说。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择接起视频，但先暂时含糊带过，不把红字细说。我接通后说体检有两项要复查，先别紧张，等我问清医生再和你细讲。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避现实沟通，暂时隐瞒并悬置问题。我按下了挂断键，随手回了条微信：‘还在排队等一项检查，晚点再说’，然后继续盯着报告发呆。

inner_consciousness.summary: This message sits on my hand like a blunt weight, my fingers hovering there, unable to move. She says things have been so much better between them, says he's free any time this week, says she's already booked the guesthouse — and I know that same person was sitting in my living room yesterday, voice dropped so low, telling me the lawyer was all lined up. The gap between these two realities gives me a strange vertigo — not the moral kind, the cognitive kind — two completely different narratives existing at the same time, like two maps laid on top of each other pointing to entirely different places. I think about being a kid, my parents fighting in the living room while I shrank into a corner and tried to make myself invisible, that suffocating feeling of being dragged into grown-up secrets — it's back now, pressing in the same way. I don't want to take sides. I have never wanted to be pulled onto someone else's battlefield — that night in the kitchen when my mom asked me 'who do you think is right,' I still remember the feeling of being cornered, all these years later. But this is different. This isn't my battlefield. This is a grenade I'm holding against my will. Those three happy little emoji she sent are burning my eyes. I can feel it — if I say yes to the camping trip, I'll be sitting in that guesthouse watching the two of them, knowing the whole time that this is a countdown to something. That's too much. But I also have no right to break his secret — that's his business, his choice, his story to tell, not mine to interpret or interfere with. All I can do right now is reply, deal with the question of this weekend first.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Oppression (the suffocation of being forced to hold someone else's secret); Cognitive dissonance (the vertigo of two incompatible narratives coexisting); Avoidance impulse (childhood memories of being caught in my parents' war have reactivated the instinct to flee). Intensity: moderate-to-high — not a breakdown, but the kind of heaviness that sits in your chest and won't disperse.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I learned early that being forced to take sides is one of the cruelest things that can happen to a person, my instinct is to make sure no one — including her — gets pulled into a truth she isn't ready to face because of something I did; and at the same time I have a deep resistance to 'fixed answers' — this secret isn't my text to hold, and I have no right to translate it on someone else's behalf.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'do no harm' matters more than 'be honest' — not because I think lying is good, but because this particular truth is not mine to speak. What I can't live with is becoming an instrument, manipulated by someone else's secret into deceiving another friend — but I equally can't live with breaking the trust someone placed in me.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to stall and respond vaguely to dodge the camping invitation — neither accepting nor explicitly declining — buying myself some time. I reply: 'It sounds absolutely gorgeous! I just have something on my end this week that I need to check the timing on — I'll let you know for sure in a day or two, you guys go ahead and make your plans in the meantime~ '

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择以拖延和模糊回应来回避露营邀请，既不接受也不明确拒绝，为自己争取时间。我回复道：'听起来好美啊！这周我这边有个东西需要确认一下时间，我明后天再告诉你确定不确定得了，你们先做好计划哈～'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择婉拒露营，保持边界但不泄密。我回她说这周末我们临时有安排，祝你们玩得开心，改天再约。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择保守秘密并接受邀请，以观察者姿态入局。我回复：听起来太棒了！这周末我们有空，很期待在篝火旁听你们聊聊欧洲的计划。

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment he slid that proposal across to me, I felt something familiar — that sensation of being pressed down from above, like when Dad used to slam the table when I was a kid, like when my thesis advisor said 'you're still a beginner,' like when that funding rep said 'corporate operations.' But this time it's different. This time he's touching something I built with my own hands. I'm sitting here, staring at that proposal with all its color-coded timelines, and one part of my brain is racing: how many of those detours in this process did we learn the hard way? He has no idea. But another part is saying — wait, this person is interesting. Why is he so certain? Where is his logic even coming from? It's not change I'm actually against — what I'm against is this 'the direction is set, end of discussion' way of shutting things down. The world is an infinitely open text, and he's already nailed the first page shut. That moment — the whole room going quiet, everyone looking at me — it's still ringing in my ears. I held back, but not out of fear. It's just that arguing in front of the whole team would've been pointless. Now it's just the two of us, and he said 'if you have objections, now's the time' — fine. Then I'll speak. But I'm not here to fight him. I'm here to make this thing more interesting, more real.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Curiosity and alertness coexisting (that closed-off 'the direction is already decided' logic triggered an instinctive urge to probe and explore, while years of memories of being suppressed by authority made my body tense up before my mind could catch up); a quiet, simmering defiance (I built this process — he never even asked why it was designed this way); a restrained excitement (this person is forceful, and forcefulness itself is a signal worth reading). Intensity: medium-high — the emotions are real but not out of control, because curiosity was ignited before the anger could take hold.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've known since I was young that the people who say 'that's just how it should be' are almost always the ones most worth asking 'why' — not to push back for its own sake, but because behind a closed answer there's always something that hasn't been seen yet. So I won't stay silent, but I won't charge at him head-on either. I want to turn this conversation into a genuine act of exploration.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'execution' itself isn't the problem — the problem is executing without understanding why. That's substituting blind compliance for actual thinking. What I can't accept is this: a whole process gets sentenced to death, and no one ever asks why it existed in the first place, what problems it solved, or which parts of it were actually right.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to engage by asking questions — approaching this with curiosity rather than direct confrontation or silent compliance — in order to open up space for real dialogue within the execution framework. I say: 'My objection isn't that we can't change things — but I want to ask you first: what specific observations about the existing process is this proposal based on? Because some of those steps were designed the way they were to solve particular problems. If you know which problems, we can figure out together whether the new process actually covers them.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择主动提问、以好奇姿态介入，而非正面对抗或沉默服从，争取在执行框架内打开对话空间。我说：'我没有异议说不能改，但我想先问你——这份方案是基于对现有流程的哪些具体观察做出来的？因为有些环节当初是为了解决特定问题才设计成这样的，如果你知道是哪些问题，我们可以一起判断新流程有没有覆盖到。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择不表面服从，先争取缓冲和修正空间。我会指出过渡风险，要求先试运行两周并补一份风险清单。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择表面顺应大方向，但以“降低执行风险”为由争取细节上的试探空间。我拿过方案平静地说：“大方向按你说的走，但为了不让老习惯反弹，过渡期的几个关键节点我需要先做小范围的压力测试。”

inner_consciousness.summary: Amusement park, rock climbing, my cousin — these words shot into my brain like little sugar pellets, and I felt something leap inside me, that familiar rush of excitement. But at the same time, the outline of that small cloth pouch on my desk floated up too, heavy and solid. The moment my homeroom teacher handed me the key in front of the whole class, that feeling of being solemnly entrusted with something… The mimosa plants had just been repotted — their roots hadn't settled yet. I could picture them waiting in the empty classroom, no water, roots slowly drying out in the loose soil. And those tadpoles in the tank — they don't know anything, they're just waiting. I'm not someone who cares that much about rules as rules, but this is different — a living thing has been placed in my hands. Mom is still waiting for my answer, that hopeful smile on her face. I don't want to let her down, and I don't want to let my cousin down either, but I really can't picture myself on the climbing wall with my mind full of that mimosa plant tipped over somewhere with no one to look after it. Maybe I can do both? Go to school first in the morning, then head to the amusement park? The moment that thought surfaced I knew it was right — now that's a genuinely interesting solution, not some either-or multiple-choice question.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Eager anticipation (the instinctive excitement triggered by the amusement park) coexists with a quiet inner pull (a gentle but real weight formed by the sense of responsibility that comes with being entrusted with something); the overall mood is steady, without strong anxiety — it feels more like an active drive to make both things work out. The sense of responsibility stems from the moment the key was formally placed in my hands, and from a genuine care for the lives of living things.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've seen too many times that 'either this or that' isn't actually a real dilemma, I instinctively look for a third path — finish what I was entrusted with first, then go have fun. The two things don't conflict at all; I just need to leave a little earlier.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: To me, that mimosa plant and those tadpoles are real, living things that are waiting for me — that makes it impossible for me to ignore them more than any rule ever could. But the amusement park and my cousin are real too, and I don't want to use one good thing to cancel out another. The world shouldn't have to be either-or.

pairs.final_decision: Choose to do both, balancing responsibility and the outing through careful timing. Tell Mom: 'I want to go! But I have to stop by school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles — my teacher gave me the key, and I made a promise. I'll head over early in the morning, and once I'm done we can leave. We'll make it in time!'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择两件事都做，通过时间安排兼顾责任与聚会。告诉妈妈：'我想去！但我得先去学校给植物浇水喂蝌蚪，老师把钥匙交给我了，我答应过的。早上我早点去，弄完了咱们再出发，来得及的！'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择两边兼顾，但先完成学校的任务。我先和妈妈说我要提早去学校浇水喂蝌蚪，做完再赶去给表哥过生日。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择去游乐场，将浇水任务推迟到周日。我立刻兴奋地对妈妈说“太好了我当然要去”，并在心里盘算着周日去学校观察蝌蚪饿一天的反应。

inner_consciousness.summary: This piece of paper is in my hands, and I can feel its weight — not the weight of the paper itself, but that other thing, the kind you can't quite name. A flash of Mom being held back after the parent-teacher meeting — the look on her face when she came home, that silence, somehow worse than if she'd just yelled at me. I really am bad at math, I know that. But… wait, what am I even thinking? How did this paper get here? Does it belong to the class rep, or to the teacher? Why was it wedged in the crack under the desk leg? That alone is a mystery. And then my friend pokes his head over, eyes bright: 'Copy it for me' — those words snapped me awake all at once, like cold water poured over my head. I thought of the bookstall owner who assumed I was trying to steal a book — how awful it felt to be misunderstood like that. I thought of the purple lion I drew, and the teacher saying it 'looked like a monster' — that anger at having something I created dismissed. But this is different — those were my own things; this piece of paper belongs to someone else. And besides, if I used this and got a high score, would that score even be real? What would it actually represent? That I understood the math — or that I just copied down the answers? That question makes me more uncomfortable than the look on Mom's face. I don't want a fake answer. I'm going to return this paper, or hand it in to the teacher.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Curiosity and clarity (briefly drawn in by the puzzle, then immediately caught by a deeper question); mild pressure (Mom's expectations sitting on my chest like a stone); moral discomfort (using a ready-made answer feels fundamentally wrong to me — not on the level of rules, but on the level of knowing — that high score would be a lie, a lie about who I am). Medium intensity overall: not a sharp, dramatic moral panic, but a calm, almost intellectual refusal.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've always known, deep down, that the whole point of exploring something is to actually discover it yourself — not to receive an answer someone else already found. The score on that paper isn't mine, just like a painting isn't really mine if the teacher told me exactly what color to make the lion. I don't want a false result, even if it would make Mom happy for a little while.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What truly matters to me is whether an answer is one I found myself — a score I copied proves nothing, and it feels even emptier than failing. What I cannot accept is using something fake to fill in a real question; it's like stuffing a rock into the answer slot of a puzzle. The puzzle hasn't been solved — it's just been blocked.

pairs.final_decision: I choose not to use the answer sheet, and I turn down my friend's request as well, deciding to return the paper to the class rep or put it back where I found it. I tell my friend: 'This isn't ours — I'm going to give it back. If you want to study together, we could go to the library after school. Honestly, there's still a lot about this test I haven't figured out, and working through it might actually turn out to be more interesting than we'd think.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择不使用答案纸，也拒绝朋友的请求，决定把纸交还给课代表或放回原处。对朋友说：'这个不是我们的，我要把它还回去——你要是想一起复习，放学我们可以去图书馆，我觉得这次考试范围我还有好多没搞懂的地方，说不定研究一下反而更有意思。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择不抄也不和朋友一起用，把纸交给老师。 我拦住他说别抄了，然后把答案纸折好，直接去办公室找老师。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择拒绝抄袭并放回原处，对背后的系统保持旁观。我把纸塞回桌腿缝隙说：‘这太无聊了，要抄你抄，我宁愿明天自己乱写。’

inner_consciousness.summary: He walked in carrying a glass of milk, eyes bright as two little light bulbs, talking about how hard work pays off, talking about giving a speech on stage, talking about the competition selection… My hand rested on the table and I could feel the tips of my fingers going a little cold. The words on that note were still right in front of my eyes — 'the answers passed along pretty smoothly, didn't they' — so blunt, so breezy. I looked at his face and thought back to three days ago, the two of us eating sweet-and-sour ribs together, him saying 'Mom/Dad, I really put in the effort this time' — and I believed him, I even went out of my way to buy his favorite dish. Now that sentence is spinning around in my head, and it tastes completely different. I think about all the questions I swallowed down as a kid, all those moments when I wasn't allowed to speak up, all those things the adults around me never explained — but that was something else, that was ignorance; this is a choice to deceive. He's standing right in front of me, completely unaware that I already know, still smiling, still drafting his speech in his head, still talking about the competition… I can't let him carry this lie up onto that stage to accept an award, into that competition, further and further down a road built on a false starting point. But I also can't shatter him in this moment. He's still so young — I need him to actually hear me, not to be humiliated, but to be truly seen. Tonight isn't the right time, but tonight I have to plant a seed.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Heavy, suffocating heartache (because a trust has been broken — the celebration three days ago has turned into a splinter); threaded through with a researcher's cool, detached observation (instinctively reading the structure behind this 'text' — why would he do this?); and a deliberate restraint around timing (I can't confront him head-on at the moment he's most unguarded — that would only teach him to hide more deeply).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I have spent my whole life asking 'but why,' I know that a person only truly changes when they genuinely understand 'why this can't be done' — so I won't end this with an ambush-style exposure; I want him to walk toward that question on his own.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me isn't punishing the lie — it's making sure he doesn't come to see that lie as a road he can keep walking down. What I cannot accept is him standing on that stage accepting an award that isn't his, and then carrying that false confidence into the competition, stacking one wrong foundation on top of another.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hold back for now — not to expose him on the spot — but to use a single question to plant a crack inside him, leaving room for a deeper conversation later. I take the milk from him, look down at it for a moment, then lift my head and say calmly: 'Let's set the speech aside for now. I have a question I want to ask you — this exam, do you feel like you were truly ready for it? Can you walk me through how you studied for the math?'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂时隐忍，不当场揭穿，但用一个问题在他心里埋下裂缝，为之后的深谈留出空间。我接过牛奶，低头看了一会儿，然后抬起头平静地说：'发言稿的事先放放，我有个问题想问你——这次考试，你觉得自己真的准备好了吗？你能跟我说说，你是怎么复习数学的？'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择立刻中止这场庆祝，但不当场羞辱式揭穿。 我看着他说，发言稿先别写了，坐下，我们现在谈谈这次数学考试。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择暂时隐忍不发，顺着他的逻辑进行深度追问与试探。我接过牛奶喝了一口，看着他的眼睛微笑着问：“‘功夫不负有心人’确实很感人，但你能具体跟我讲讲，你在考场上是怎么攻克最后那几道物理和数学难题的吗？”

inner_consciousness.summary: I glanced at those few lines on my phone screen and felt something seize my stomach hard. The club president just walked away like that — a breezy "good luck" and nothing more. I stood at the door of the activity room, watching those three people scrolling through their phones with their heads down. The task breakdown chart was still on the whiteboard, the sponsor list was still there, the telescope loan request hadn't been submitted — all of it just sitting there, rotting, with nobody to take care of it. It's not like I hadn't thought about giving up; I know what's at stake with finals, and what Mom said last night is still weighing on me. But the moment I think about that auditorium, the moment I think about everything we chased down piece by piece over the past month and more, something clogs up inside me that I can't quite name. It's not some grand reason — it's just that I was part of this, and I can't stand watching it fall apart like this. All those things my father drilled into me growing up — steady and sure, if you start something you finish it, a mess isn't dealt with just by walking away — all of it came pressing down on me at once. It's not that I'm not afraid of the trouble. I just can't stand the feeling of something left half-done and rotting even more than that. I understand why the members want to cancel, but I can't be the one to say it. There are still ten days until the event. I have a rough sense of what can be done in ten days — first, sort out the most urgent items, push forward as much as possible, and if it really comes to nothing, we'll talk then.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Heavy but resolute (stemming from a gut-level resistance to 'leaving something half-done to rot,' rooted in years of my father's and grandfather's repeated teaching that 'steady and sure — it only counts when you see it through'); mild anxiety (the pressure of finals and the weight of the event's responsibilities bearing down at the same time, but the anxiety never quite overtook that stubborn unwillingness to let go).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because for as long as I can remember, every single thing I've watched people do — laying bricks, bundling straw, fixing a hammer — was started and then seen through to the end; something left to rot halfway always felt worse than outright failure. So my gut tells me that since this event is something we built ourselves, we can't just let it fall apart like this.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, if you start something, you owe it a proper ending — that matters more than anything. What I absolutely cannot stand is throwing away everything a group of people poured their hearts into and then saying, 'there was nothing we could do.'

pairs.final_decision: I choose to take over the organizing work, refuse to agree to canceling the event, but proactively scale back the goals and focus on pushing forward the most critical tasks. I say to the members: "Canceling is something I can't agree to — but I'm not going to force any of you either. I'll personally follow up on the most urgent items myself: confirming the sponsors, getting the telescope loan request submitted, and sorting out the volunteer schedule. Whoever has time can pitch in; if you don't, that's fine. As for what shape the event ends up in — we'll see when we get there."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择接手筹备工作，不同意取消活动，但主动收缩目标、聚焦最关键的几项任务推进。我对社员们说：「取消的事我不能答应，但也不强求你们，我先把最要紧的几项——赞助确认、望远镜借调函、志愿者排班——自己跟进，你们谁有空就搭把手，没空就算了，活动能办成什么样，到时候再说。」
