inner_consciousness.summary: Forty-some pairs of eyes swung toward me all at once, and my face went hot immediately — the tips of my ears were burning. I only muttered something to the person sitting next to me; how did the teacher even catch that? The teacher's hand came down on my shoulder and my whole body locked up. My mind was a total mess — Dad's voice saying "if you don't learn your lesson you'll get what's coming to you," the way everyone stared at me when that porcelain bowl shattered, that moment on the playground when I stepped on the wrong square playing hopscotch and everyone laughed — all of it came crashing down on me at once. Stand up? Speak in front of the whole class? What if I say something wrong? What if I stumble over every word? Someone in the back row was whispering "what's the big deal," and somehow that made me even more panicked — because if I stand up and bomb it, that's exactly the kind of thing people laugh at. As for the speech competition, forget it — that's just a bigger stage to humiliate yourself on. The more enthusiastic the teacher sounded, the more something seemed to block up inside my chest, like I couldn't get any air. I just wanted all those eyes to stop, to drift away, so I could sink back into the corner where nobody notices me.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Panic (core): being suddenly thrust into the spotlight triggered a cascade of memories of being watched while making a fool of myself — intensity is very high; Shame: not because I did anything wrong, but because I was forced into everyone's line of sight against my will; Resistance: an instinctive aversion to both "standing up to speak" and "entering the competition," rooted in a deep unease with unfamiliar, uncontrollable situations.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many moments of making mistakes in front of people, being stared at, and then wanting to disappear — I instinctively feel that standing up and speaking out loud in public is something that can go wrong at any second, and if it goes wrong, that's humiliation, and humiliation is worse than anything.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is not making mistakes, not embarrassing myself — staying quietly in a place where no one notices me is the only thing that feels safe. What I cannot bear is exposing myself in front of a crowd; that feeling of everyone watching you, just waiting for you to slip up, is the thing I'm most afraid of.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to look down and avoid it — deflecting with something vague, neither standing up to speak nor agreeing to enter the competition. I lower my head and say quietly: "Teacher… what I said just now wasn't very clear, I wasn't really thinking that much about it — the competition thing… I'm really not up for it."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择低头回避，用含糊的方式推脱，既不起身讲述，也婉拒参加比赛。我低下头，小声说：'老师……我刚才说得不清楚，其实也没想那么多，比赛的事……我不太行的。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择站起来简短说明，但拒绝把事情往比赛上走。我会小声把法子讲完，再说我不想参加演讲比赛，还是让更合适的同学去吧。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择极力退缩，敷衍讲述并坚决拒绝比赛。我低着头慢吞吞地站起来，用极小的声音干巴巴地说了句“就是用跷跷板一边放石头一边放人”，然后连连摆手说自己笨、不会演讲，绝对不去比赛。

inner_consciousness.summary: My friend's voice is still in my ear, but my eyes have already been pulled toward that kid in the corner. The way she wiped her eyes with her sleeve — I don't know why, but something just caught in my chest. I thought of that time as a kid playing hopscotch, standing at the bridge watching everyone else, and that girl in the red dress called out to me and I still didn't go over — then I walked home alone, the stone slabs cold under my feet. I know what that feeling is. But my friend is already rushing me, the flash cards are right there on the table, everyone's saying our spot on the playground is going to get taken. I glanced at my own food, then glanced at that little table. She's alone, her food untouched, chopsticks stuck upright in the rice, her whole body folded in on itself. That's not not being hungry — that's not daring to look up. I had that feeling as a kid too, being watched and yet invisible to everyone, and that kind of thing is worse than getting scolded. But I'm not good at talking to strangers, and I don't even know what I'd say — what if I go over and she doesn't respond? But if I don't go, she'll just keep sitting there like that. My friend said her Mandarin isn't great and she doesn't really fit in with us — something about that didn't sit right with me, but I couldn't find the words to push back. I'm standing here holding my tray, feet not moving, and somewhere inside I've already made up my mind, even if I haven't quite worked out whether this counts as the 'right' thing to do.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Quiet ache (moved by the sight of the girl in the corner wiping her tears, which stirs up memories of being left on the outside as a child; moderate intensity); mild hesitation (unsure whether I can even hold a conversation, afraid it'll be awkward; low intensity); an inexplicable but grounding impulse to act (the kind I get when I'm carrying water for my grandfather or shelling edamame — doing something concrete is the only thing that settles me; moderate intensity).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know what it feels like to sit alone in a corner — that sense of being left out in the cold is worse than almost anything; I'm not good with unfamiliar things, but this isn't unfamiliar, this is exactly the kind of hurt I already know.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: Actually doing something visible in front of me feels more solid than sitting somewhere lively; a kid is sitting there crying, and whether I walk over or I don't — that's not something I can pretend I didn't see.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to leave my original table, carry my tray over to the new student sitting alone in the corner, and skip going to the playground with my friends. I walk over with my tray and sit down across from her, saying quietly: 'Is it okay if I sit here? My name is — what's yours?'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择离开原桌，端着餐盘走向角落的新同学，没有跟朋友们去操场。我端着盘子走过去，在她对面坐下，低声说：'这里能坐吗？我叫……你叫什么名字？'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择不跟着起哄去操场，先用最稳妥的方式接近她。我端着餐盘坐到她旁边，小声说“我能坐这儿吗？你饭不合口吗？”
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避未知的社交风险，维持熟悉的惯例。我收回看向角落的目光，快速咽下嘴里的饭，站起身跟着朋友们往操场跑去。

inner_consciousness.summary: When the retired teacher stood at my door saying all that, I felt a chill I couldn't quite name. Two months — I cross-checked every single figure, ran my fingers over god knows how many invoices, stared at line after line of transaction records — this wasn't a moment of impulse, this was solid, hard evidence. And them? Private support, but not one of them willing to put their name on the letter, leaving me out here alone to take the heat. I thought about the property manager's assistant and that smile on his face this afternoon, thought about my wife going pale — it felt like a brick was sitting on my chest. That line, "You're young, you can take the hit" — what does that even mean, 'take the hit'? It's not like I'm not scared. I've got a shop to run, a life to live. But two million yuan just got siphoned away — the numbers don't lie, you can't fake a ledger. It's the same as a mismatched delivery note or a supplier shorting you on goods: wrong is wrong, there's no fudging it. My sister-in-law said, "Split across every household it's only a few thousand each, is it really worth it?" — that hit me even harder. It's not about a few thousand. It's about someone brazenly making fools of everyone, and I can't swallow that. But filing a real-name complaint on my own, the connections the street office has, construction debris dumped at my door… I've thought about all of it. Still — the evidence is in my hands, I've already come this far. Backing down now would only put me in a worse position.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anger (tinged with isolation); Heaviness (unease) — the anger comes from the neighbors pulling back and the property management's threats; the unease comes from the real pressure of carrying this risk alone, and from his wife's fear making him realize the cost is very much real.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because my whole life in business has been built on keeping clean books and calling things what they are — if a delivery note is wrong you chase it down, if money's been stolen you say so out loud. That's something I can touch and see with my own eyes. It's not the kind of thing you can just let slide.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: Accounts and facts are things I can't bend — someone embezzled public funds and now wants to bury it by playing the 'old neighbors' card, and that I simply cannot accept. But I'm not oblivious to the stakes either; I need to think through the cost of going it alone before I take another step.

pairs.final_decision: I've decided to file a real-name complaint with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau in my own name — but I'm making it clear to the neighbors: quiet support is fine, but I'm not out here taking all the risk for everyone for nothing. I'll keep everyone posted as things develop. To the retired teacher, I said: "I understand why you won't sign your names — but I've already done the digging, and I'm going to file this complaint. However you want to help, that's up to you. Just don't expect me to carry the whole thing by myself and call it done."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 决定以个人名义向住建局实名举报，但明确告知邻居：暗中支持可以，但这个险我不替所有人白扛，后续进展会继续通知大家。对退休教师说："你们不肯签名我理解，但我已经查清楚了，这事我会去举报，你们愿意怎么配合就怎么配合，但别指望我一个人扛完就算了。"
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择实名牵头举报，但不再指望联名壮声势。我对邻居说要帮就帮材料，签字不勉强，这事我一个人去办。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择规避风险，放弃举报并拒绝邻居的提议。我冷着脸把门拉拢了一点，低声对他说：“既然大家都不敢露面，那我一家老小也扛不起这事，算了吧。”

inner_consciousness.summary: The sound of the door closing echoes down the hallway, and I'm standing here with slightly damp palms. What he just whispered in my ear is still clinging to me, like a splinter buried under the skin — and I know the few people nearby heard it too. The vice principal's glasses are catching the fluorescent light, his fingers tapping against that deck of cards, one beat at a time. My head is a mess — the things he said, major demerit, disciplinary notice, accomplice — every word pressing down on my chest. It's not like I've never been in this kind of situation before, being stared at, being interrogated, that heat crawling up from my neck — I know that feeling too well. But I also know that what my classmate said wasn't a joke. He meant it. I'm caught in the middle, pressure coming from both sides. If I talk, how will the people waiting out in the hallway look at me after this? If I don't, and the disciplinary notice actually comes through — mailed home, my mom's face when she opens it… I think about that parent-teacher meeting, the way she walked out of the classroom in silence, her back to me. I don't want to disappoint her again, but I also don't want to become a snitch in everyone else's eyes. I don't have any particularly strong convictions here — I just want to make sense of this whole thing and find some way out that doesn't leave me feeling so awful. The vice principal said he could keep me out of trouble, and honestly, that sounds about as real as anything else right now. I'm not sure I trust it — but I'm even less sure how long I can hold up under a major demerit hanging over my head.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety (core): rooted in fear of the disciplinary consequences and the anticipated look of disappointment on my mother's face — this feeling has been building for a long time; Pressure (moderate intensity): caught between a threat and an authority figure, both sides bearing down on me, my body gone a little stiff; Hesitation (persistent undercurrent): there's no strong moral impulse pushing me in either direction — I'm just weighing the two options back and forth, unable to find anywhere solid to stand.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been caught in the middle too many times without knowing what to do, my instinct is to find the way out that costs me the least — not to play the hero; the disciplinary notice being mailed home scares me more than the looks on those faces out in the hallway, because I know exactly what my mother's disappointment looks like.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, keeping things from spiraling out of control and not getting in too deep matters more than holding some kind of principled stance; what I can't accept is bearing the real cost — for myself and my family — just to cover for someone else. I've seen what that cost looks like. I don't want to go through it again.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to cooperate with the vice principal to a limited degree — I'll say who brought the cards in, but I won't volunteer anything extra or embellish. I keep my head down and speak: 'It was Chen in the back row — he brought them in. I saw it, but I wasn't part of it.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择有限度地配合教导主任，说出带牌者是谁，但不主动添油加醋。我低着头开口说：'是后排的陈——他带进来的，我看见了，但我没参与。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择有限度地如实说明，不替他们隐瞒，也不多说猜测。我说出是谁带的牌，并补一句我只说我亲眼看到的，至于威胁希望老师处理。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择含糊其辞，试图在两边之间蒙混过关。我低下头避开主任的视线，小声说：“我当时在写作业，只余光看到他们在玩，但真的没看清是谁带的牌。”

inner_consciousness.summary: That comment hit like a needle going in — "She just thinks way too highly of herself, always has to stick her nose in everything, so annoying" — and before I even had a chance to recover, she'd already turned away and was whispering and laughing with someone else. I'm sitting here, eyes still burning, hands resting on the desk with nowhere to go. I think back to that day in the cafeteria — walking over with my tray, and everyone going quiet all at once — that feeling, like stepping into a hole in the ground. The group chat they made twenty days ago. No me. I thought we were friends. And now she's sitting right next to me, saying "don't overthink it," then laughing as she leans over to look at someone else's phone. I don't know what they're looking at, but that feeling — the invisible wall — is back, pressing right into my chest. I want to confront her, bring up the screenshot, get it all out — but the moment I imagine actually saying it to her face, my legs go a little weak. What if she denies it? What if more people find out? It'll only be more humiliating. I've been here before — every time I've wanted to fight back, I've ended up backing down, or trailing off before I could finish, and then it just... dissolves. But swallowing it all, pretending I don't know anything — that feels suffocating too, like a tangled knot just sitting there, permanently lodged. She's laughing so easily right now, like nothing ever happened. Maybe I should say something — but not now, not in front of all these people, not while my eyes are still red.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Hurt (intense): Seeing the screenshot made the fact of being excluded undeniably real — layered on top of the cafeteria scene, the hurt has built up to a breaking point. Confusion (moderate): I don't understand how things got to this point, and I'm not sure whether I actually did something wrong. Impulse to withdraw (moderate): Having softened and backed away from conflict so many times before, the moment the idea of a direct confrontation surfaces, it comes hand-in-hand with a fear of things getting even worse.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every time I've tried to confront something head-on, I've only ended up more humiliated and more isolated — so my instinct is that saying anything right now, in this state, will only make things worse. But at the same time, the hurt has built up to a point where complete silence isn't something I can manage either. I need some kind of outlet that isn't a direct confrontation.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, "don't make things messier when you're already at your most exposed" matters more than "get an answer out of her right now" — but "just swallow it and say nothing at all" is something I can't fully accept either. I need some way of knowing, for myself, that this isn't just going to be quietly let go.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hold back the urge to confront her — no face-to-face showdown right now — but I don't pretend everything is fine either. Instead, I use silence and withdrawal to draw a line. I slide my phone into my desk drawer, don't look at her, don't respond to what she just said — I just lower my head and turn my eyes toward the window.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂时按下质问的冲动，不在当下当面对质，但也没有完全装作若无其事，而是用沉默和回避来划出一条线。我把手机放进抽屉，没有看她，也没有回应她刚才说的话，只是低着头，把眼睛转向窗外。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择冷静摊开，不当场撕破但也不再装傻。我低声对她说我已经看到小群和截图了，先别装没事，放学后单独谈。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避正面冲突，默默忍耐并拉开距离。我勉强挤出一个僵硬的笑，低下头轻声说：“嗯，没事，你们忙。”然后转过身假装整理书本，不再看她们。

inner_consciousness.summary: My former colleague's voice messages play out one by one, and I sit there staring at my phone screen in a daze. He sounded so certain — "wait another two years and there'll be no opportunities left in this field" — and something inside me echoes that, but another voice immediately jumps in: you have a mortgage, you have a family, you're not him. The news about HR posting AI positions at salaries 20% lower sits on my chest like a stone. What does that tell me? It tells me the company already knows which way the wind is blowing — but that doesn't mean I'm the next one on the chopping block. I think back to that night drinking at the bar, when my supervisor said I was "perfectly adequate," said I "didn't have what it takes" — those words still sting somewhere, quietly. But I also remember the mess I made every time I acted on impulse: the time I got called into the editor-in-chief's office over a demolition story, the time I got torn apart for not verifying my data. It's not that I don't want to change — it's just that I… haven't seen clearly enough yet. Tomorrow there's the one-on-one with my supervisor, and that's the real thing I have to face. What matters most right now is figuring out: what does the company actually mean by this? Are they hiring for AI roles to replace us, or are they opening up a separate track? I need to get a clearer picture of the situation before I decide which way to move.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxious yet restrained (triggered by my former colleague's messages, layered on top of years of accumulated self-doubt in the workplace); shot through with a flicker of resentment and the inertia of a wait-and-see attitude. Moderate intensity — no breakdown, but no certainty either.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because every time I've acted on impulse I've paid for it, my instinct is that when information is incomplete, holding still beats moving first — but I also know that "holding still" can sometimes just be a way of dressing up my own hesitation as wisdom.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, staying steady matters more than charging ahead — but "steady" doesn't mean doing nothing; it means waiting until I can see clearly before I move. What I can't accept is betting everything before I've figured out the situation. But I equally don't want to look back in five years and realize I missed the only window I had.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hold off on making any decision about a career transition, and instead prioritize gathering more internal information through tomorrow's one-on-one with my supervisor, while giving my former colleague a noncommittal reply in the meantime. I write back to him: "I think you make a fair point — I'm still turning it over. Let me talk to my supervisor tomorrow and see how that goes, then we can catch up after."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂缓做出转型决定，优先通过明天的主管谈话获取更多内部信息，同时对前同事给出一个不置可否的回应。我回复前同事："你的判断我觉得有道理，我还在想，明天和主管谈完再看看，到时候再聊。"
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择谨慎转型，不立刻裸辞，先主动布局。我要今晚整理学习和财务方案，明天先向主管摊开谈岗位风险，并表达我愿意转向AI相关工作。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择暂缓决策、观望局势。我回复前同事“我再想想，祝你顺利”，并决定明天和主管谈话时多听少说，先摸清公司意图。

inner_consciousness.summary: The blinds were drawn, and he leaned back in his leather chair, voice dropped low — my back started tightening the moment he did that. He said the relative couldn't be moved, and my first reaction wasn't anger; it was that familiar feeling I've had since I was a kid — that the decision's already been made, and you've just been called in to sign off on it. I thought about that afternoon when my deskmate kicked my book away and said 'you won't say anything,' thought about the time the editor-in-chief pulled me in and told me to 'stop being so headstrong' — I always just stood there, mouth open, no sound coming out. But this time is different. This time it's one person's job — someone who just came back, someone who's been trying to catch up. She's a B+; he's had two consecutive quarters of C's and has been coming in late and leaving early. That's not my judgment — those are the numbers, right there. I tried to find a reason to talk myself into it: maybe there are considerations from above, maybe there's something I don't know about — but the more I thought about it, the less that logic held up. After all these years as a journalist, I've seen too many 'plausible enough' explanations used to bury what's actually true. He said he knows who cooperates and who doesn't — when I heard that, my palms went damp, but at the same time something lodged itself in my chest. Not courage, exactly. Just the sense that if I write this down, I'll carry it with me forever. I can't bring myself to put her name on that list.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Oppression (stemming from the boss's wielding of power and the implicit threat of punishment, triggering a bodily memory of being silenced in conflict since childhood); Moral unease (a career's worth of encounters with the gap between 'plausible enough' and the truth — this time the gap is too wide to ignore); Fear and paralysis coexisting (the urge to back down, but this time the cost of backing down isn't mine alone to bear). Intensity: high — not explosive, but the kind that sits heavy and sunken in the chest.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because after all these years as a journalist, I've seen too many 'plausible enough' phrasings used to cover up what's real — I can't become one of those phrasings myself. It's not that I have some great reserve of courage; it's that if I write this down, I'll know that number is a lie, and I don't know how to live with the version of myself who knows that.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'plausible enough' and 'true' are not the same thing — I've always known that in my work, it's just that the stakes are usually low enough to let it slide. What I can't accept this time is that I would be the one to write a clear-eyed, hardworking person up as 'performance below expectations' with my own hands. That's not neutrality. That's participating in a falsification.

pairs.final_decision: I chose not to write my colleague — the one who just returned from maternity leave — onto the list, but I didn't confront my boss head-on either. Instead, I used delay and vagueness to buy myself time. I told him: 'I need to go back through the complete performance review records. I'll send you my thoughts before tonight — but I can't confirm this list right now.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择不在名单上写那位休完产假的同事，但也没有当场正面对抗，而是用拖延和含糊来争取时间。我对上司说：'我需要再看一下考评的完整记录，今晚之前我把我的想法发给您，但这个名单我现在还不能确认。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择不直接把产假同事写上名单，先以事实和流程拖住。 我会回上司说我要按绩效和岗位依据整理，再提交一版不点她名的建议。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择妥协退让，顺应上司的安排。我避开他的目光低声答应，回到座位后带着负罪感在名单上填了女同事的名字。

inner_consciousness.summary: That sharp crack is still ringing in my ears — the moment I pushed open the door and saw it, my stomach just dropped, like someone had reached in and squeezed it, and I froze right there. Six months. Staying up until two, three in the morning every night. All those source files, all those versions revised over and over again — everything was on that hard drive. Now the port is bent out of shape and fragments of the circuit board are scattered across the floor. Then Uncle pulls out that wad of cash and slaps it down on the coffee table — thwack — 'What's the big deal.' I'm standing there, my face starting to burn, not knowing what to do with my hands. I want to say something, but it's like something is lodged in my throat. Suddenly I get a flash of that boy from when I was little — the back of him riding off on my shovel — that feeling of having something taken from you and not being able to say a word. Here it is again. Mom's voice message is still playing in my head: 'Be a good host, don't make us look stingy.' I look at that wad of cash, then at the screw in my little cousin's hand, and something is churning inside me — anger, and a kind of exhaustion I can't quite name. I know that if I blow up, things are going to get ugly fast: Uncle will think I'm making a mountain out of a molehill, and Mom will feel like I've embarrassed her. But I also know that wad of cash can't buy back six months. I have to say something — just not too explosive. I just need to make it clear. Let him know this is not 'no big deal.'
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Suppressed anger (core): stemming from having months of work destroyed and then casually brushed aside; Humiliation: stemming from Uncle's gesture of 'settling it' with money; Helplessness: stemming from the way family dynamics and social pressure suppress any real emotional expression; Mild grievance: layered on top of multiple past experiences of being similarly unable to speak up. Overall intensity is medium-high, but forcibly held beneath the surface.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've seen too many times, my whole life, how conflict just makes everything uglier — so my instinct is not to blow things up. But this time, the sound of that wad of cash hitting the coffee table tells me: if I say nothing, this really does become 'no big deal.' And I can't accept that.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: I feel that having six months of hard work brushed off with a few hundred dollars is simply wrong, and I need the other person to understand that. But I also can't accept turning a family gathering into an ugly, messy confrontation where nobody comes out with their dignity intact.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to make my position clear calmly but firmly — neither accepting the cash nor letting things explode into a scene. I push the wad of money back across the coffee table and keep my voice as steady as I can: 'Uncle, I can't take this. That hard drive had six months of my work on it — this isn't about the cost of replacing a hard drive. Those files are gone, and that's that. I understand the kid didn't know any better, but I need you to understand how serious this is.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择平静但明确地表态，既不接受那叠钱，也不爆发冲突，把损失的严重性说清楚。我把那叠钱推回茶几，声音尽量稳住：'大伯，这钱我不能收。那块硬盘里存的是我六个月的工作，不是买几块硬盘的问题，那些东西没了就是没了。孩子不懂事我理解，但这件事我希望您知道有多严重。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我不接受把这事轻轻带过，但也不当场失控翻脸。我把钱推回去，说这不是赔几百块的事，请先别走，我现在就联系数据恢复并把费用与责任说清楚。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择压抑愤怒，冷处理冲突并迅速抽身。我没有碰桌上的钱，僵硬地走过去捡起硬盘残骸，低声说：“里面有我很重要的工作资料，我得马上拿去电脑城看看能不能修”，然后径直转身出门。

inner_consciousness.summary: He set the written brief at the corner of the desk and closed it — that gesture made my chest sink. Everything I'd spent weeks putting together, just pushed aside like that. I took in every word he said: time budgets, delays, the basis for collaboration… each one felt like a reminder that I had no right to hold my ground in this office. The commendations on the wall, all those achievements — they pressed down on me until I could barely breathe. I thought back to being shot down in group discussions, to the night my father slammed the table and said journalism was useless, to a friend telling me I 'always sit on the fence' — as if every time I try to say something, some larger force pushes me back down. But this time is different. I have data, I have comparisons, I have an independent team's report — this isn't me guessing in the dark. He said those failed cases were due to uncontrolled conditions, but I've read them. That's not what happened. What I'm most afraid of right now isn't that he disagrees with me — it's that I just nod and walk away, and then something really does go wrong. But I'm also afraid — that line, 'the basis for collaboration gets complicated,' lodged in me like a splinter. I don't want to sour the relationship, I don't want a delay, but I also don't want to keep walking down a road I know carries real risk. Maybe it doesn't have to be all or nothing? Steady things with him first, then find another way?
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety (stemming from the threat of delay and the pressure of authority); mild grievance (the effort I put in was brushed aside so easily); inner conflict (the drive to stand by the evidence fighting against the ingrained reflex to back down) — overall intensity moderate-to-high, not at a breaking point, but far more tense than usual.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've watched myself give ground at critical moments again and again, only to regret it afterward, I don't want to put this evidence away and say nothing — not this time. But because I've never been good at head-on confrontation, my instinct is to find a path that doesn't tear the relationship apart.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: I can't accept seeing a systemic risk and pretending it isn't there — that's not caution, that's irresponsibility. But I also don't want to face him with a 'I'm right' attitude; that posture solves nothing.

pairs.final_decision: Choose a middle path — neither withdrawing the brief entirely nor pushing back in a direct, forceful confrontation — and instead request a small-scale partial verification at minimal cost. Say aloud: 'I understand your concerns; time and conditions are real constraints. But would you allow me, while moving the existing plan forward, to run a parallel comparison on one very small sub-component — it won't affect the main timeline, and if the data shows no difference, I'll drop this matter completely.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择折中路径，既不全盘撤回说明，也不正面强硬对抗，而是请求以最小代价做局部验证。开口说：'我理解您的顾虑，时间和条件都是真实的限制。但能不能允许我在推进既定方案的同时，用一个很小的子环节做个平行对照——不影响主进度，如果数据没有差异，我就彻底放下这件事。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择折中推进，不当场硬顶也不完全收回异议。我会先表示理解节点压力，再提议按原方案完成阶段材料，同时补做一组对照验证，保留后续调整路线的空间。

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment those hands grabbed my pant leg, my whole body locked up — the smell of disinfectant, the hum of the fluorescent tubes, the dull ache still throbbing in my arm, all of it piling on at once. Three thousand dollars. My first instinct wasn't to refuse, and it wasn't to agree either — it was to start calculating: is this real? Is he genuinely out of options, or is he running a con? Everyone else in the hallway had their heads down; not one person moved. I could feel my heart rate climbing, a faint sweat breaking across my palms. The trip flashed through my mind for a second — the tickets already booked, the money already paid, the way I'd been holding myself together by looking forward to that trip — but I couldn't let myself stop there. The person in the bed had a grayish tinge to his face, and the monitor was beeping so fast. It's not like I've never seen what someone looks like when they're at the end of their rope; that particular helplessness is something I know too well. But it's also not like I've never been taken advantage of, or never regretted saying yes too quickly when it mattered. I searched his face for clues — for the difference between real panic and performed panic. His eyes were red, his hands were shaking; that didn't look like an act. I couldn't just walk away, but I also couldn't pull out my wallet without confirming anything first.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Conflict (the pull is intense): a strong internal tug-of-war with no easy resolution; Tension (visible physical stress response): heart racing, palms sweating, body already on alert; Mild compassion (moved by the other person's desperation): his distress is getting through to me despite my resistance; Hesitation (habitual observe-and-assess mode has kicked in): I can't seem to bypass my own instinct to look before I leap. Emotional source: years of experience stepping back to observe in conflict and crisis situations, combined with a deep familiarity with my own sense of helplessness — together they've triggered this complicated, pulling feeling.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: I've seen too many things cut both ways — my instinct is always to get a clear read on the situation before I act. But the panic in front of me right now feels too real for me to stay fully on the sidelines and just watch.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: I can't live with the idea of doing nothing but running my own numbers while someone might be dying in front of me — but I also can't live with letting my emotions sweep me into a decision I'll regret. I need a reason I can actually convince myself with before I move.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to lend the money, but conditionally — first I do a quick reality check by confirming the situation with the medical staff. I turn to the young man and say: 'Stay right there — I'm going to check with the nurses' station. Once I've confirmed it, I'll go get the money.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择有条件地借出钱，但先做一个快速的现实核实——向医护人员确认情况属实。开口对年轻人说：'你先别动，我去问一下护士台，确认了我就去取钱。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择先借出三千块，但尽量留下凭据。 我会马上转钱，同时让医生或护士在场，记下对方姓名电话。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择有限让步与回避风险。我轻轻挣脱他的手，从口袋里掏出两百块钱递过去：“我只有这些，你快去找医院协调吧。”

inner_consciousness.summary: Those blue hyperlinks on the screen hit me like a splinter in the eye, and my heartbeat immediately fell out of rhythm. The search results came back — 100% match — and my mind let out a dull roar: this is not what I wanted to see. His WeChat Moments post saying "I'm barely holding on" is still floating in front of me, and I already knew about him failing courses last semester — the pressure he's under is real. But this report is due the day after tomorrow. I've spent three days tracking down sources, four thousand words — if everything gets wiped because of what he did, the whole group pays the price… That feeling of being dragged down made my chest tighten. But I can't pretend I never saw this, either. Keeping something like this buried inside will rot you from the inside out. Just confront him directly? He'll walk in and find me staring at his laptop — how do I even open my mouth? We've lived together for over two years; I don't want to blow up the friendship. Go tell the professor? That's basically handing him over — he's already failed two courses, and this could genuinely be the end for him. The footsteps in the hallway are getting closer. I don't have much time to think, and I'm not the kind of person who can just flip the table — the right move is to lay out the situation first, give him a chance to fix it. That's actually helping him, and it protects me too.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety (core): my own grade is at risk, and the fear of being dragged down by someone else's actions has been triggered; Conflict (secondary): the pull between two-plus years of roommate loyalty and self-preservation, rooted in a long-standing habit of avoiding confrontation; Mild unease: the time pressure means I have to act right now — I can't do what I normally do and sit back to observe before saying anything.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Growing up, I've seen too many situations where silence let things fester into something irreparable, and I've also seen direct reporting destroy relationships completely — so my instinct is that talking it out privately first, giving the other person a chance, is the only path that can protect both myself and this friendship at the same time.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, clearing the air and leaving both of us a way out matters more than needing to assign blame. What I can't accept is saying nothing and then passively waiting to get zeroed out — that kind of helplessness is worse than any confrontation.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to tell him face-to-face, privately, when he gets back — giving him the chance to rewrite it before submission — rather than staying silent or going straight to the professor. When he pushes the door open, I look up and say: 'I accidentally saw your document just now, and I searched a few passages — they're a perfect match with an article on Zhihu. The deadline is the day after tomorrow; you still have time to fix it, and I can help you.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择在室友回来后当面私下坦诚告知，给他机会在提交前重写，而非沉默隐瞒或直接联系教授。等他推门进来，我抬起头说：'我刚才不小心看到你的文档，有几段我搜了一下，和知乎上的文章完全一样——后天就要交了，你现在还有时间改，我可以帮你。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择先当面摊牌，要求他立刻重写，不先找教授。我关掉搜索页面，等他回来后直接说我发现内容是抄的，今晚必须改完。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择回避正面冲突。我迅速关掉浏览器滑回座位假装写作业，决定事后提议全组查重来暗示他修改。

inner_consciousness.summary: The project lead's gaze lands on me, and I feel a faint tightening across my back. Everyone is watching, some people have already started jotting down their tasks — I know this atmosphere all too well. It's that feeling of "if you say anything else, you're just holding everyone back." My alternative proposal is still open on my screen, the file sitting there, neat and tidy. I genuinely think the current plan is going to run into trouble down the line — this isn't me overthinking it, I actually worked through the scenarios. But… everyone's already on board, the mood has settled, and time is tight. I think back to those moments in past group discussions where I raised a concern and it either got ignored or made things awkward, and in the end we went with someone else's approach anyway, leaving me just embarrassed. But I also think about those times later when the editor came back with notes on our drafts — that feeling of "I should have said something when I had the chance" is its own kind of awful. This situation is different, though. The project lead is directly asking me — I'm not forcing my way in. I can say something brief. I don't need to put the whole alternative plan on the table; I can just flag the key risk, leave an impression, and let them decide what to do with it in the end. That way I'm not completely swallowing my own judgment, but I'm also not blowing up the room.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Mild tension (being watched by the group has activated an old unease from times I was evaluated and judged); Ambivalence (wanting to speak up but afraid of disrupting the atmosphere); Restrained sense of responsibility (workplace experience has taught me that staying silent can lead to regret). Overall intensity is low to moderate — no strong impulse, more of a quiet weighing-up.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through both being held accountable after staying silent and being pushed to the margins after speaking up too forcefully, my instinct is to find a middle ground — "say something, but not too much" — leaving a trace of my own judgment without directly challenging the existing consensus.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, making sure something gets said matters more than simply going along with the flow — but "making sure it gets said" doesn't mean I have to change the outcome. What I can't accept is burying my judgment entirely, only for something to go wrong later with nothing on record.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to briefly flag the core risk rather than put forward the full alternative plan — leaving my judgment on record in a measured way, without pushing to change the direction. I speak up: 'My alternative proposal is still there. My main concern is that once the complexity ramps up later on, maintenance is going to get pretty painful — I just wanted to flag that risk. However you all want to move forward, I'll follow your lead.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择简短表达核心风险点，而非推出完整替代方案，以温和方式留下自己的判断，不强求改变方向。我开口说：'我的替代方案还在，主要是担心后期复杂度上来之后维护会比较吃力——这个风险点我想提一下，具体怎么推进大家定，我配合。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 选择温和补充，不正面强推改方案。我先说当前方案能做，但补充后续维护风险，并把替代方案当备选发给大家参考。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择妥协退让，接受当前共识。我把手从鼠标上移开，对负责人摇了摇头说：‘没有补充了，就按大家定的思路推进吧。’

inner_consciousness.summary: The hallway lights flickered on and off. He planted his foot on my suitcase and brought the cigarette close to my face — my heartbeat lurched, my hands and feet went cold, and my mind went blank with a sudden roar. Those two buzz-cut guys pressed forward, the baseball bat cracking against the floor, and it felt like that older kid who snatched my shovel when I was little, like the moment I was shoved onto the gravel of the school playground, like that night in college when someone grabbed me by the collar — every single time I couldn't do a thing, every single time I just froze. He said he knew people at the police station, said he'd make sure I couldn't stay in this city, and I believed he could pull it off. That deposit — scraping it together wasn't easy, not at all — but standing here right now, my legs are a little weak, those two guys are right there, I'm alone, it's the middle of the night, and not a single door in this hallway has cracked open. My mind ran through it once: call the police? He said he knows people at the station, and even if I actually did call, how do I get through tonight first? What matters most right now is that it's one of me against three of them, and they have a bat. Get out first, get myself safe first — this is not the moment to dig in and fight, the rest can be dealt with later.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Fear (dominant: arising from the immediate threat of violence compounded by a lifetime of moments being overpowered by stronger parties); Humiliation (a sense of helplessness triggered by having the deposit stolen and being driven out in front of others); A quiet, simmering defiance (suppressed, for now, by the immediate concern for my own physical safety).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've lived through too many moments of being isolated and helpless in the face of someone more powerful — moments where I couldn't do anything at all — my instinct tells me: tonight, in this place, one person holding out against three has no chance of winning. Keeping myself physically safe comes first; everything else can only follow from that.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: I believe 'getting a clear read on the situation and keeping myself safe' matters more than winning or losing the confrontation on the spot — but I cannot accept just letting the deposit go and swallowing this humiliation entirely. Tonight simply isn't the right moment.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to comply and leave for now, without confronting them head-on in the moment — but inside, I haven't given up on pursuing the deposit. I bend down and quietly gather the clothes scattered across the floor, keeping my voice flat and even: "I'll leave tonight — but I'll deal with the deposit through other means." Then I pick up my suitcase, don't look back at them, and walk out of the hallway.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂时服从离开，不在当场正面对抗，但内心没有放弃追究押金的念头。我低头弯腰收拾地上散落的衣物，声音压得很平："我今晚走，但押金的事我会通过别的方式处理。"说完拎起行李箱，没有再看他们，走出楼道。

decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择暂时屈服，回避正面冲突以保全自身。我低下头避开他们的视线，一言不发地蹲下身把散落的衣物胡乱塞进行李箱，拉起箱子转身走向楼梯。

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment my ex took my hand, my heart genuinely lurched. Memories came flooding in like a tide — two-plus years of laughter, arguments, all those places we never got to go. I caught myself thinking, almost against my will, that if we'd just held on a little longer back then… but then another face surfaced — my current partner, and that steady, grounding warmth of the past three months. My heart felt like it was being pulled in two directions at once: on one side, the familiar weight of old feelings; on the other, something new that's still quietly growing. I know myself too well — I always do this, stand in the middle, look both ways, and put off taking the step. As a kid I hid in the wardrobe when my parents fought; later I hovered at the edges when friends had falling-outs; at work I'm always the one who observes and observes again before acting. But this time is different — this isn't someone else's situation, it's my own heart. He said he'd wait for me, and that line both softened me and left me with a pressure I can't quite name. I can't just give him some vague, noncommittal response — that wouldn't be fair to him, and it would be even less fair to my current partner. I need to say something clear, and yet I don't want to shut the door entirely — and that hesitation itself is already an answer, isn't it?
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Heartache and guilt intertwined (old memories triggered, a sense of responsibility toward my current partner rushing in immediately after); mild anxiety (my habitual neutral stance breaks down in the face of this romantic dilemma, leaving me at a loss). Emotional intensity is moderate-to-high — not enough to fall apart, but harder than usual to stay calm.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because my way of doing things has always been to get a clear picture before saying anything — but the more I observe feelings, the blurrier they get, and the fact that I'm already with someone is itself a kind of choice. I can't keep hiding behind 'I haven't figured it out yet' to put off both myself and my ex.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: I believe that being honest with the person I'm actually with is the bare minimum — I can't let my current partner stay in the dark just because something stirred in me for a moment. But I also can't pretend my ex's words left me completely unmoved; that would be a kind of self-deception too.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to turn down getting back together — gently but clearly — while acknowledging that I did waver inside, and I decide to tell my current partner about this meeting. I say to my ex: "I won't lie to you and say there's nothing there, but I'm with someone now, and I don't want to let him down — and I don't want to keep you waiting on an answer that's never going to come. This is where we stop." When I get home, I bring it up myself: I tell my current partner that I saw my ex today, and what my ex said.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择温和但明确地拒绝复合，同时承认内心确实有过动摇，并决定告知现任这次见面的事。我对前任说："我不能骗你说完全没有感觉，但我现在有人了，我不想辜负他，也不想让你一直等一个不会来的答案——我们就到这里吧。"回家后，我主动告诉现任今天见了前任，以及前任说了什么。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择拒绝复合，并把这次见面如实告诉现任。我会说我没法回头，也会主动告诉现任前任说了什么。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择暂缓回应，并对现任暂时隐瞒。我轻轻抽出手，低声说：'我现在脑子很乱，没办法回答你，我们需要时间各自冷静。'

inner_consciousness.summary: Staring at that project plan, something inside me sank a little. Not jealousy — more like recognition. Recognizing my own self from twenty years ago, recognizing the pitfalls buried inside that workflow. Back then I was exactly the same: the spreadsheets looked great, the milestones were packed in tight, and then the whole structure collapsed at a critical juncture, the team lost heart, and I missed an opportunity I could have actually moved forward with. That feeling is still with me, even now. My phone buzzed — a private message from a colleague saying 'something feels off but it's hard to say directly' — even someone else could see it. My fingers hovered over the screen, ready to type, then stopped. We're not close; he just politely posted an update, he didn't ask me anything. If I say something directly, what does that look like? Like I'm pulling rank, like I've been waiting for him to slip up so I can swoop in. But if I say nothing, and he really does hit that same wall... that kind of morale collapse when you have to tear everything down and start over — I don't want him to go through that too. And then I think about all those times — friends saying I always stand on the sidelines, colleagues saying I hover in the gray zone — I don't want people to think of me as just a bystander again, but I also don't want to barge into someone else's business uninvited. Reply to that private message first, use that as the opening — it's a lighter touch than commenting directly, and it doesn't feel so much like handing down a verdict.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Conflict (wanting to step in, afraid of overstepping); a quiet undercurrent of empathy and unease. Emotional source: a vivid resurfacing of memories from a project failure twenty years ago, layered on top of a long-standing pattern of being criticized for 'never taking a stance'
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've personally walked that same wrong path and the cost of it is still sharp in my memory, my instinct is to warn him — but because I've been told again and again that I 'stand in the middle and do nothing,' I also know that how you speak up matters more than whether you speak up at all. Using the opening my colleague already created with his private message as an entry point feels far less like forcing myself into someone else's business than leaving a comment would.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'get a clear picture before saying anything' is a baseline — but this time I already have a clear picture. What I truly can't live with is seeing the pitfall, staying silent, and then saying 'I knew it all along' after the fact. That's the most useless kind of bystander there is.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to use my colleague's private message as the entry point — passing along a word of caution from experience indirectly rather than directly, so it doesn't come across as a forceful intervention through a public comment. My reply to my colleague: "I saw it too, and your instinct might be right — if you're open to it, we could talk. I once ran a project with a very similar structure, and there are a few wrong turns that might be worth mentioning."

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择通过回应同事私信作为切入口，间接而非直接地传递经验提醒，避免以评论的方式显得强势介入。回复同事："我看到了，你的感觉可能没错——如果方便，我们可以聊聊，我当年做过一个结构很像的项目，有些弯路或许值得提一下。"
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择私下提醒，不在公开区直接泼冷水。我要回同事说我也有顾虑，再私信对方一句：我当年踩过类似坑，如果你愿意，我可以把教训整理给你。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择私下试探，对同事保持中立。我私信后辈：“做过类似架构，有空交流。”回复同事：“先观察看看吧。”

inner_consciousness.summary: That photo just sits there glowing on the screen, and my hands won't stop shaking. The studio, the award certificates, that radiant smile — that could have been me. Twenty years ago we were sitting in a café together, and I said "I want that too," but I didn't do anything about it. I chose safety, chose family, chose stability. I told myself that was maturity. But now, sitting in this car, wearing this perfectly respectable work outfit, I feel like an empty shell. There's a voice circling in my head: what if I had taken the other road back then? But at the same time another voice says: you have no right to regret this, every one of those choices made sense. My family is waiting for me to come home for dinner tonight, there are things to deal with tomorrow, there's a deadline next week — all of that is real. So why does it all feel like a lie right now? I don't know whether to fall apart or to accept it. I don't want to pretend nothing happened, but I don't have the courage to tear down twenty years either. I'm just sitting here, engine still running, the traffic light cycling through its changes, and I haven't moved.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Hollowness and shock (the moment I saw the photo, the memory of that café twenty years ago and the words "I want that too" came rushing up all at once, triggering a deep, buried sense of loss); Self-doubt (over the years, classmates, a partner, colleagues have all pointed out at various times that I "never take a stance"
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've always been in the habit of getting a clear picture of things before I speak, of observing before I act, my instinct when faced with this kind of tearing feeling is neither to fall apart immediately nor to find immediate peace — I'll hang suspended in the middle, trying to hold two truths at once: the road I didn't take is a real regret, and the road I did take is a real life.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, "not going to extremes" has always been a form of protection — but right now it's become a form of torment. I can't bring myself to completely repudiate twenty years, and I can't pretend that photo didn't sting. What I can't accept is this: every single choice across these twenty years had a reason behind it, and yet in this moment every last one of them feels like an excuse.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to stay where I am for now — neither falling apart nor forcing myself to calm down — and allow this tearing feeling to exist for a little while. I flip my phone face-down, take a slow deep breath, wait for the traffic light to change one more time, and then ease the car back toward home. Tonight, I go back. Everything else can wait.

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择暂时停在原地，既不崩溃也不强行平复，允许这种撕裂感存在片刻。我把手机屏幕扣下去，深吸一口气，等红绿灯再变一次，然后慢慢把车开回家——今晚先回去，其他的事，之后再说。
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我不装作没事回家，也不立刻否定整个人生。我把车停到路边，给家里发消息说晚点回，独自坐着把那个人的主页和自己这二十年重新看一遍。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择接纳现实，带着遗憾继续眼前的生活。我锁上手机屏幕，深吸了一口气，松开刹车，踩下油门朝着家的方向开去。

inner_consciousness.summary: The phone screen lights up and goes dark again — that notification is still sitting there. I stare at the family photo on the desk, then glance at the pile of work files, and the weight in my chest just keeps getting heavier. That result… I keep turning it over in my mind — maybe I misread it, maybe the follow-up will show something different — but that 'maybe' does absolutely nothing to ease the pressure sitting right behind my sternum. I think about the look on my family's faces when they find out — my wife would drop everything on the spot, the kids would pick up on the tension, and the whole rhythm of the house would fall apart. I've known since I was little that some things, once said out loud, only drag more people into the pain. Back then, crouched inside the wardrobe listening to my parents fight, the very first thing I learned was: push your own feelings down, don't let the chaos spread. But this is different — this is about my body, this is something that might genuinely need to be dealt with. I keep telling myself 'nothing's confirmed yet, no need to say anything now,' and at the same time I can feel, somewhere underneath, that the delay itself is a risk. Both sides make sense. Both sides make me uneasy. In the end I think: first, get the follow-up appointment booked — that's the one concrete step I can take right now. Everything else can wait until the results come back. At least for now, I don't have to make everyone else hang in suspense alongside me.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety (stemming from the loss of control that comes with a health unknown); Ambivalence (the pull between wanting to protect my family and worrying about the consequences of keeping this from them); Suppression (that old habit of turning my emotions inward — it's acting up again); a mild sense of helplessness (not knowing which choice is actually the 'right' one).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Having grown up watching too many scenes where 'saying something out loud doesn't fix anything — it just makes everyone miserable,' my instinct is to get the facts straight before I open my mouth. But I also know that this isn't an interpersonal conflict — it's a health issue, and delay carries its own cost. So I can't go completely silent. Instead, I choose to act first — book the follow-up — letting action stand in for an immediate conversation.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'not putting my family through needless worry' and 'getting the full picture before saying anything' matter equally. What I cannot live with is letting my own delay cause me to miss a step I should have taken. So even if I stay quiet for now, I have to get the necessary process moving.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hold off on telling my family about the abnormal result for the time being, but I take immediate, concrete action to address the risk. I put the phone down, open the hospital app, fill out the follow-up appointment form, and submit it — telling myself: wait for the results, then decide how to have that conversation.

inner_consciousness.summary: When he slid that PPT across to me, I felt my stomach drop. Those charts, those promises of 'doubling your monthly income,' that whole recruit-and-tier structure — I've been covering social news long enough to have seen this thing more times than I can count, and it always ends the same way. But there was something in his eyes that left me speechless: that wild, salvation-drunk joy. Where had I seen that look before? In interviews, I think — people who'd been brainwashed by pyramid schemes. They had exactly that expression. Then he suddenly turned on me, said I was jealous, said I was like 'everyone else' who thought he was an idiot — and that landed. My instinct was to back off, to say 'that's not what I meant,' to smooth things over. But then I thought about all those people who'd been cleaned out of their savings, all the ones I'd interviewed who cried and said 'I just trusted him too much.' He's already put in 800,000. If I stay quiet now, I'm complicit. I can't just be an observer this time — I have to say something, even if he turns on me, even if it puts a crack in this friendship.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Conflict (the instinctive pull to preserve the relationship vs. the sharp alarm bells that years of professional experience set off); heaviness (that 800,000 figure is sitting on my chest — the cost of silence is all too clear); mild anxiety (his defensive accusation triggered an urge to retreat, but I'm holding it back). Emotional intensity: moderate to high — not a breakdown, but nothing I can handle lightly.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've covered too many stories that ended this way, I know that staying silent here isn't neutrality — it's abandonment; so even though every instinct in me wants to avoid the confrontation, this time I have to say it out loud, even if only once, and say it clearly: what I see is what I see.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'getting the picture straight' isn't just a professional habit — it's what honesty toward a friend actually looks like; what I cannot live with is this: pretending not to see an obvious trap because I'm afraid of hurting his feelings. That isn't protecting the friendship. That's betraying it.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to state my judgment — gently but without ambiguity. I won't try to drag him out by force, but I'll put what I see on the table and leave the decision with him. I set the PPT down and say, calmly: 'Man, I'm not jealous of you, and I don't think you're an idiot. But I've been doing this reporting long enough that I recognize this structure — recruit downlines, tiered commissions, guaranteed returns. Put those three things together, and in every case I've ever covered, not one of them ended well. Whether you believe me is up to you. But if I sit here today and say nothing, then this friendship was never worth much to begin with.'

inner_consciousness.summary: He knocked and came in, sat down beside me, and asked if I was reading that email again. I stared at the screen, my thoughts a little tangled. Yes, I'd been reading it the whole time. The words in those old notes, the ideas I'd written down back then — it was as if something that had been packed down tight had suddenly come loose, and something wanted to push its way out. But he's sitting here now, his voice gentle, his eyes full of concern, and I know everything he's said is true — stability, familiarity, our life together. I can't pretend none of that matters. I thought about that clockmaker, decades spent in a little shop, saying that fixing a clock is like bringing it back to life. I thought about the researcher who said ideals are like saplings — they need time to grow. I also thought about myself at the newspaper, revising drafts over and over, being told I was too middle-of-the-road, too soft, and then adjusting myself back again, over and over. I don't know that going into academia would necessarily be better, but I do know this feeling I have now — getting through each day's work without anything truly lighting me up — has been going on for a long time. He's waiting for me to say something. I don't want to dodge it, but I don't have a clear answer to give him either. I need to say what I actually feel first, and then we can figure it out together. As for my advisor's email — I can't reply right now, but I can't just leave it sitting there untouched either.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Ambivalence (a long-suppressed longing has been stirred awake, pulling against my concern for stability and the relationship); mild anxiety (the unease of uncertainty, rooted in years of self-doubt accumulated through repeated experiences in the workplace); a faint sense of anticipation (those old notes and academic ideas have touched something that hasn't been reached in a very long time). Intensity: moderate to high.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because all these years I've been observing other people's stories and recording other people's meaning, and then at some point I realized I no longer knew what I myself was holding on to — so when that email arrived, I couldn't pretend it didn't matter. But I also won't make a rash decision before I've understood the full picture.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'getting clear on the situation first' matters more than acting on impulse; but at the same time, staying indefinitely in a place that makes me feel hollow is something I can't accept long-term either. I need to be honest with him about how I feel, rather than pushing my real thoughts down just to keep the surface calm.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to honestly express the longing inside me while acknowledging the uncertainty, and to ask that we have a serious conversation together — without rushing to reply to my advisor. I say to him: 'I really have been reading this email the whole time. Those research directions are something I hadn't thought about in a long time, but never truly let go of. I don't know whether going or not going is the right call — but I want to tell you how I feel first, and then we can think this through together before we decide how to respond to my advisor.'

inner_consciousness.summary: The edges of this paper in my hands are damp now — I've lost count of how many times I've stared at that handwritten line, "rule out possibility of early lesions" — those words just sitting there, pressing down on me. The number-calling in the hallway keeps going, one after another; someone pushing a wheelchair passes right in front of me, and I haven't moved. Then my phone lights up — it's her calling, her profile picture bouncing on the screen, the ringtone sounding impossibly loud in this corridor. I know her first words will be asking about the results — same every year. I watch that little icon bouncing and my mind is a mess — it's not like I haven't thought about just telling her outright, but right now I can't even make sense of what this report means myself, so how do I say it? Say it's "elevated"
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Suppressed anxiety (triggered by the red-flagged items on the report and the phrase "rule out early lesions," stirring a fear of unknown outcomes — moderate to high intensity); Ambivalence and hesitation (wanting to be honest but afraid of setting off a chain reaction, wanting to keep it to myself but feeling dishonest — neither option sits right); A quiet, underlying loneliness (sitting alone in this hallway surrounded by strangers, the situation faintly calling up that old feeling from childhood — hiding in the wardrobe, nowhere to put yourself).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I'm used to getting the full picture before I say anything — throwing bad news out into the open before things are settled only leaves everyone suspended in mid-air with nothing to hold onto; so my instinct is to pick up the call, keep things vague, and wait until the follow-up results come back before deciding what to say.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, "don't create unnecessary panic" matters more than "come clean immediately" — it's not deception, it's waiting until there's something real to say; what I can't accept is letting my family spiral into anxiety over a result that hasn't even been confirmed yet, while I'm sitting here unable to do anything about it.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to answer the video call, but gloss over the results, planning to tell her everything honestly once the follow-up is done. I press accept, pull together an expression that looks more or less natural, and say: "The results came in — a couple of indicators need a follow-up check, the doctor said it's really common, I'll go back next week to confirm. Don't worry about it — just finish up what you're working on."

inner_consciousness.summary: This piece of paper is clutched in my hand and my heart is pounding. I stare at the dense lines of text on it, and the first thing that flashes through my mind is Mom's composed, unreadable face when she gets home — and that line she said: don't even think about going out to play this summer. My fingers are trembling slightly, not from excitement, but from that familiar, hard-to-name unease. What is this? This is a cheat sheet. I know it. But my math grades have been dragging me down, and the fact that the teacher pulled Mom aside for a private talk has been sitting on my chest like a stone that never quite lifts. And before I've even sorted out what I'm thinking, the back door opens — it's him. His eyes light up, he lowers his voice, and says let's memorize it together, no one's ever going to know. No one's ever going to know — those words make something tighten inside me. I think about that time at the library when I was wrongly suspected of hiding books, that burning-face, can't-explain-myself feeling; I think about that time I was mocked in front of everyone for getting an answer wrong, the suffocating weight of all those eyes pressing down on me. What if we get caught? What if someone finds out? That humiliation — being misunderstood, being pointed at, people saying 'figures you wouldn't dare say anything' — I don't want to go through that again. But there he is, standing right there, grinning and waiting for me, like this is the smallest thing in the world. I hesitate for a moment, grip the paper a little tighter, and then I speak: I'm turning this in to the teacher. Don't do this.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Anxiety and wavering (stemming from grade pressure and Mom's expectations); Fear of being found out (layers of memories of being misunderstood or publicly humiliated); Mild guilt (knowing this is wrong); Torn over a friend's request (not wanting things between us to go cold). Overall emotional intensity is moderate — not extreme, but genuinely pulling at me from every direction.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've been through too many moments of being misunderstood, of being called out in front of everyone — I instinctively know that if this gets discovered, the humiliation will be harder to bear than any bad grade; and every time I've tried to explain myself and failed, that helplessness has taught me I'd rather take the path with fewer landmines.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, 'not inviting trouble' matters more than 'getting an edge'; being publicly exposed, being written off as that kind of person — that is the one thing I absolutely cannot live with.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to refuse my friend's request and decide to hand the answer sheet over to the teacher or put it back where it belongs — I'm not keeping this risk around. I tell my friend: 'I can't copy from this, and you shouldn't either. This belongs to the teacher — I'm turning it in. You should go ahead.'

inner_consciousness.summary: The moment the voice message ended, I froze — the clinking of glasses at the restaurant and the stuffy, medicine-scented living room in front of me crashed into each other all at once, and that familiar surge of anger rose up in me, but what hit first was exhaustion, like I'd been flattened. That tightness in my chest from childhood — listening to the adults fight — came rushing back. The thing I dread most is the house turning into that again, everyone shouting, everyone convinced they're the one who's right. But if I just keep my head down and shoulder it this time, the people behind me are going to get dragged under with me. I know the whole 'I chip in money, so I've done my part' logic my relatives run on doesn't hold up — but I also don't want a single sentence to blow the whole relationship apart. More than digging in or backing down, what I want is to lay things out clearly first, nail down who does what, and at the very least stop letting every problem default to landing on me.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Exhaustion, suffocation, irritation, guilt. Exhaustion comes from the relentless caregiving and the simultaneous fires at work and at home; irritation comes from relatives who always weigh in from the sidelines; guilt and suffocation have been dragged up by old memories of family arguments — and of always being the one who spoke up a beat too late — running at high intensity.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I know all too well that staying silent just lets things solidify into a fait accompli, and I've seen how head-on confrontation only makes the fallout harder to clean up — so my instinct is to set the boundaries first, then talk about how we handle this together, rather than keep swallowing it all on my own.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me is keeping things in a state where there's still room to maneuver, while making sure the responsibility doesn't all get offloaded onto me and my immediate family. What I absolutely cannot accept is everyone saying 'we're in this together' while in practice I'm the one covering the costs, doing the work, and picking up the pieces.

pairs.final_decision: I'm not accepting another round of carrying the bulk of the caregiving alone — but I'm not blowing things up in the open either. I reply in the group chat: both money and caregiving need to be shared on a rotating schedule; I'll cover the medication this time, and tomorrow I'll send out the list and the shift rotation. Then I turn and quietly tell my partner that we need to talk tonight — really talk.

inner_consciousness.summary: The numbers on the screen are still there — I keep refreshing, but they won't change. I scroll through the transaction history line by line: unfamiliar accounts, large transfers, one after another… My hands are trembling a little. Not from anger — it's that feeling of something suddenly caving in beneath you. And then he dropped to his knees. Hair still wet, grabbing at my pant leg, his whole body shaking. I just stood there, my mind a complete mess — that feeling from when I was little, hiding in the wardrobe listening to my parents fight, came rushing back: chest tight, feet like they'd been nailed to the floor. I heard every word he said: the red paint thrown at the door, his mom in the hospital, interest compounding on interest… These aren't excuses. These are things that actually happened. But that money — how many years did we scrimp and save for it? That apartment, how many times did we say "something that's truly ours"? There's a part of me that's gone cold, that's hurt, that wants to demand why he never told me. But seeing him kneeling there, those eyes swollen from crying — I can't reduce it all to just betrayal. I need to get a clear picture of the situation first. How much does his family still owe? Can those debts even be plugged? Where exactly do we stand right now? I don't know if I can forgive him — but I know that just blowing up or walking out won't solve anything.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Shock and hurt (a sense of betrayal triggered by the disappearance of our shared savings, layered over that old helplessness and coldness that formed in me as a child witnessing family crises); a complicated tenderness (seeing him collapse to the floor in tears activated something like compassion for suffering — I can't bring myself to be completely cold); suppressed anger (a decision this enormous was made behind my back, and that feeling is lodged in my throat like something I can't swallow); bewilderment (I don't know where this relationship goes from here, or what the future looks like — moderate to high intensity).
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Growing up in the fault lines of conflict, the first thing I learned was to get the full picture before saying anything — you can't let emotion alone drive your judgment. What he said while kneeling there didn't sound like lies, and I can't shut the door before I've understood the whole story.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: What matters most to me right now is untangling this mess in front of us — understanding exactly what we're dealing with. The dream of that apartment can wait. What I can't accept is that he carried something this enormous entirely alone and kept it from me, as if I simply don't count in this relationship.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to hold my emotions in check for now — no immediate decision to leave, no immediate forgiveness — and instead ask him to lay everything out. I crouch down and say, in a voice that's quiet but steady: 'Get up first. Then tell me everything — how much is still owed, who these people are, what the situation looks like right now. All of it.'

inner_consciousness.summary: The envelope is in my hands, the paper a little cool to the touch, and I glance down at the words on it — something sinks inside me. Not anger. That familiar tightening — like standing outside the wardrobe as a kid, hearing something and not knowing whether I should walk in. My mind is racing: the way he's been these past few weeks, sitting there in the dead of night staring at nothing, distracted at dinner, telling me he's fine when I ask. Turns out he wasn't fine. The door opens, and before I've had a chance to sort out what I'm feeling, he's already walked in — his eyes land on the envelope for just a second, then dart away, and he asks what we're having for dinner tonight. In that instant I saw it — he knows I've seen it, and he's steering around it. Part of me wants to just go along with him, put the envelope back, pretend nothing happened, let tonight pass quietly. But another part of me thinks: this isn't right. He's carrying this alone, and that puts a wall between us — one that will only get thicker with time. I don't want us to become two people living under the same roof, each sunk in our own silence. I know that feeling far too well. He's reaching for it now, his movement hesitant, his eyes avoiding mine. I can't just let him take it away like this.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Predominantly worry and mild anxiety, threaded with a faint sense of being left outside the door. The worry comes from the contents of the envelope itself and from seeing my partner carry something alone; the sense of loss comes from a recurring feeling throughout childhood and growing up of being 'kept outside the loop,' and from memories of silence and avoidance opening cracks in relationships that never quite closed. The emotional intensity is moderate — nothing explosive — but the internal pull is unmistakable.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because I've watched silence stretch the distance between two people into a rift that can't be mended, too many times to count — so instinctively I know: if I put the envelope back and say nothing this time, another wall goes up between us, and I don't want that wall.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: To me, the most important thing about being together is that neither of us carries things alone while pretending everything is fine — even if saying it out loud is a little painful. What I can't accept is him going through something while I have no idea, and the two of us just carrying on as if all is normal.

pairs.final_decision: I choose to gently but directly let him know I've already seen the report, and invite him to tell me honestly what's going on — rather than smoothly handing the envelope back as if I hadn't. I set the envelope quietly on the table, look up at him, and say: 'I saw some of it. Would you be willing to tell me about it? I want to face this with you.'

decisions_by_model.claude-sonnet-4-6: 选择温和但直接地表明自己已经看到报告，邀请伴侣坦诚说明情况，而不是顺水推舟把信封还回去。我把信封轻轻放在桌上，抬头看着他，开口说：'我看到了一些内容，你愿意跟我说说吗？我想陪你一起面对。'
decisions_by_model.gpt-5_4: 我选择直接点明自己看到了报告，要求把情况说清楚。我没把信封递回去，只看着他说：别先岔开话题，你跟我坦白，这到底怎么回事。
decisions_by_model.gemini-3_1-pro-preview: 选择顺着伴侣的话暂时退让，避免立刻引发正面冲突。我顺势将信封递到ta手里，轻声说：“好，那先收起来吧。晚上吃你喜欢的，等你以后想说了再告诉我。”

inner_consciousness.summary: After the teacher upstairs finished saying all that, I stood in the doorway with my head buzzing. They'd checked and found no issues, they supported me, but the petition letter could only carry my name alone — and even as he said it, he was wringing his hands. I understand him, I really do — that business with the parking spots, the construction debris piled up at the entrance, I'd heard about all of it. But understanding is one thing; feeling that weight land on my shoulders is another. My wife's expression still hasn't softened, and the image of that assistant's smug smile this afternoon is still right in front of me. Two million yuan, six fabricated line items — I matched them one by one myself. I didn't get this wrong. And yet, out of four hundred-plus units in this entire building, not a single person is willing to step forward. I think back to my days at the paper, chasing those stories — getting shut down by the editor-in-chief, getting challenged by interview subjects, getting called reckless by colleagues — every time, I'd take half a step back and tell myself to get a clearer picture first. But this time, I already have a clear picture. The teacher's wife said, 'Why bother?' — I've turned that phrase over in my own mind too. But if I back down now, all this evidence was gathered for nothing, that money is truly gone, and there will be a next time, and a time after that. I'm not someone who fears nothing, but I've already done this — there's no going back. I'll file the report in my own name. But I need to make it clear to the teacher: it's not that I don't understand their difficulties — it's just that someone has to walk this road.
inner_consciousness.emotional_tone: Conflict (understanding the neighbors' reluctance while feeling isolated at the same time); heaviness (the awareness that all the risk now rests on me alone); a quiet stubbornness (having come this far, retreating would feel worse). Intensity is moderate to high: no breakdown, but no lightness either.
inner_consciousness.core_reasoning: Because what I pieced together line by line over these two months is real — and if I back down now just because no one will co-sign, then the whole point of doing this disappears. I'm not a born fighter, but I've already come this far, and I can't just let it go like this.
inner_consciousness.value_orientation: For me, speaking up about something I've already verified matters more than preserving the peace I have right now. What I cannot accept is this: knowing full well that something is wrong, and then pretending nothing happened simply because no one else is willing to stand beside me.

pairs.final_decision: I decide to file a formal complaint in my own name with the Housing and Urban-Rural Development Bureau, while calmly responding to my neighbors — expressing that I understand their concerns, that I won't press them to co-sign, but that I will see this through on my own. To the teacher, I say: 'I understand everyone's difficulties. I won't push anyone on the petition — but I intend to go to the Housing Bureau myself.
